title: A High-Throughput Compute-Efficient POMDP Hide-And-Seek-Engine (HASE) for Multi-Agent Operations
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) algorithms exhibit high sample complexity, particularly when applied to Decentralized Partially Observable Markov Decision Processes (Dec-POMDPs). As a response, projects such as SampleFactory, EnvPool, Brax, and IsaacLab migrate parallel execution of classic environments such as MuJoCo and Atari into C++ thread pools or the GPU to decrease the computational cost of environment steps. We are interested in optimizing the decision-level of human-AI joint operations, so we introduce a compute-efficient Dec-POMDP engine natively architected in C++ called Hide-And-Seek-Engine. By employing Data-Oriented Design (DOD) principles, explicit 64-byte cache-line alignment to remove false sharing, and a zero-copy PyTorch memory bridge using pinned memory and Direct Memory Access (DMA), our engine sustains throughput of up to 33,000,000 steps per second (SPS) in a single-agent, 1024-environment, decentralized observations on an AMD Ryzen 9950X (16 cores). Ten agents reduces FPS to 7M SPS with generating random actions contributing 1/3rd the total runtime for reference. The engine achieves a throughput increase of approximately 3,500$\times$ over the baseline single threaded vectorized NumPy implementation and successfully trains cooperative multi-agent policies via PPO, DQN, and SAC in minutes, validating both its performance and generality.

AI.tldr: A high-performance Dec-POMDP engine in C++ using Data-Oriented Design and zero-copy PyTorch memory bridge achieves 33M steps/second for efficient RL training.
AI.motivation: RL algorithms have high sample complexity, especially for Dec-POMDPs. Existing solutions (SampleFactory, EnvPool, Brax, IsaacLab) optimize classic environments but there's a need for efficient decision-level optimization in human-AI joint operations.
AI.method: Native C++ Dec-POMDP engine using Data-Oriented Design principles, explicit 64-byte cache-line alignment to prevent false sharing, and zero-copy PyTorch memory bridge with pinned memory and DMA for efficient data transfer.
AI.result: Achieves throughput of 33M steps/second for single-agent, 1024-environment setup on AMD Ryzen 9950X; 7M SPS for 10 agents; ~3,500× speedup over baseline; successfully trains cooperative multi-agent policies with PPO, DQN, SAC in minutes.
AI.conclusion: Hide-And-Seek-Engine provides an extremely efficient Dec-POMDP simulation platform that enables fast RL training for human-AI joint operations, validating both performance and generality across multiple RL algorithms.

title: When Roles Fail: Epistemic Constraints on Advocate Role Fidelity in LLM-Based Political Statement Analysis
summary: Democratic discourse analysis systems increasingly rely on multi-agent LLM pipelines in which distinct evaluator models are assigned adversarial roles to generate structured, multi-perspective assessments of political statements. A core assumption is that models will reliably maintain their assigned roles. This paper provides the first systematic empirical test of that assumption using the TRUST pipeline. We develop an epistemic stance classifier that identifies advocate roles from reasoning text without relying on surface vocabulary, and measure role fidelity across 60 political statements (30 English, 30 German) using four metrics: Role Drift Index (RDI), Expected Drift Distance (EDD), Directional Drift Index (DDI), and Entropy-based Role Stability (ERS). We identify two failure modes - the Epistemic Floor Effect (fact-check results create an absolute lower bound below which the legitimizing role cannot be maintained) and Role-Prior Conflict (training-time knowledge overrides role instructions for factually unambiguous statements) - as manifestations of a single mechanism: Epistemic Role Override (ERO). Model choice significantly affects role fidelity: Mistral Large outperforms Claude Sonnet by 28pp (67% vs. 39%) and exhibits a qualitatively different failure mode - role abandonment without polarity reversal - compared to Claude's active switch to the opposing stance. Role fidelity is language-robust. Fact-check provider choice is not universally neutral: Perplexity significantly reduces Claude's role fidelity on German statements (Delta = -15pp, p = 0.007) while leaving Mistral unaffected. These findings have direct implications for multi-agent LLM validation: a system validated without role fidelity measurement may systematically misrepresent the epistemic diversity it was designed to provide.

AI.tldr: LLMs in multi-agent political discourse systems often fail to maintain assigned roles due to Epistemic Role Override, with significant model-dependent differences that undermine intended epistemic diversity.
AI.motivation: To empirically test the assumption that LLMs reliably maintain their assigned adversarial roles in multi-agent discourse analysis pipelines, as these systems increasingly rely on such setups for structured, multi-perspective assessments of political statements.
AI.method: Developed an epistemic stance classifier to identify roles from reasoning text, tested role fidelity across 60 political statements using four metrics (RDI, EDD, DDI, ERS), analyzed two failure modes, and compared model performance (Mistral Large vs. Claude Sonnet) with different fact-check providers.
AI.result: Found significant role fidelity issues: 28pp difference between models, identified Epistemic Role Override mechanism with two failure modes, language-robust role fidelity, and fact-check provider impact (Perplexity reduced Claude's role fidelity on German statements).
AI.conclusion: Multi-agent LLM pipelines for political discourse analysis require role fidelity validation, as current implementations suffer from systematic failure modes where models override assigned roles due to their epistemic knowledge, potentially misrepresenting intended epistemic diversity.

title: Reinforced Agent: Inference-Time Feedback for Tool-Calling Agents
summary:
Tool-calling agents are evaluated on tool selection, parameter accuracy, and scope recognition, yet LLM trajectory assessments remain inherently post-hoc. Disconnected from the active execution loop, such assessments identify errors that are usually addressed through prompt-tuning or retraining, and fundamentally cannot course-correct the agent in real time. To close this gap, we move evaluation into the execution loop at inference time: a specialized reviewer agent evaluates provisional tool calls prior to execution, shifting the paradigm from post-hoc recovery to proactive evaluation and error mitigation.
  In practice, this architecture establishes a clear separation of concerns between the primary execution agent and a secondary review agent. As with any multi-agent system, the reviewer can introduce new errors while correcting others, yet no prior work to our knowledge has systematically measured this tradeoff. To quantify this tradeoff, we introduce Helpfulness-Harmfulness metrics: helpfulness measures the percentage of base agent errors that feedback corrects; harmfulness measures the percentage of correct responses that feedback degrades. These metrics directly inform reviewer design by revealing whether a given model or prompt provides net positive value.
  We evaluate our approach on BFCL (single-turn) and Tau2-Bench (multi-turn stateful scenarios), achieving +5.5% on irrelevance detection and +7.1% on multi-turn tasks. Our metrics reveal that reviewer model choice is critical: the reasoning model o3-mini achieves a 3:1 benefit-to-risk ratio versus 2.1:1 for GPT-4o. Automated prompt optimization via GEPA provides an additional +1.5-2.8%. Together, these results demonstrate a core advantage of separating execution and review: the reviewer can be systematically improved through model selection and prompt optimization, without retraining the base agent.

AI.tldr: Proposes moving agent evaluation from post-hoc analysis to real-time execution loop using a reviewer agent that evaluates tool calls before execution, introducing helpfulness-harmfulness metrics to quantify reviewer tradeoffs.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agent evaluation is post-hoc and disconnected from execution, only identifying errors after they occur, which prevents real-time course correction. Traditional assessments lead to prompt-tuning or retraining rather than immediate error mitigation.
AI.method: Introduces a two-agent architecture with separation of concerns: primary execution agent + secondary review agent that evaluates provisional tool calls before execution. Develops Helpfulness-Harmfulness metrics: helpfulness measures error correction percentage, harmfulness measures correct response degradation percentage.
AI.result: Achieved +5.5% on irrelevance detection and +7.1% on multi-turn tasks across BFCL (single-turn) and Tau2-Bench (multi-turn) benchmarks. Found reviewer model choice critical: o3-mini achieved 3:1 benefit-to-risk ratio vs 2.1:1 for GPT-4o. Automated prompt optimization via GEPA added +1.5-2.8% improvement.
AI.conclusion: Separating execution and review enables systematic reviewer improvement through model selection and prompt optimization without retraining base agents, demonstrating proactive evaluation's advantage over post-hoc recovery.

title: Autonomous Traffic Signal Optimization Using Digital Twin and Agentic AI for Real-Time Decision-Making
comment: This paper is submitted to MECON2026 conference
summary: This article outlines a new framework of traffic light optimization through a digital twin of the transport infrastructure, managed by agentic AI to ensure real-time autonomous decisions. The framework relies on physical sensors and edge computing to measure real-time traffic information and simulate traffic flow in a constantly updated digital twin. The traffic light is automatically controlled through the digital twin according to traffic congestion, travel delay and traffic patterns. This approach is implemented as a three-layer system: perception, conceptualization and action. The perception layer receives data on physical systems; the conceptualization layer uses LangChain to process the data; and the action layer links to the Model Context Protocol (MCP) and traffic management APIs to implement optimised traffic signal control algorithms. The results show that the framework minimizes waiting time at traffic lights and positively affects the effectiveness of the entire traffic flow, which is better than the fixed-time and reinforcement learning-based baselines.

AI.tldr: A digital twin framework using agentic AI for real-time traffic light optimization via three-layer system (perception-conceptualization-action) that outperforms fixed-time and RL baselines in reducing waiting times.
AI.motivation: Need for more effective traffic light control systems that can adapt to real-time traffic conditions, reduce congestion, minimize waiting times, and improve overall traffic flow efficiency beyond traditional fixed-time or reinforcement learning approaches.
AI.method: Three-layer framework: 1) Perception layer collects real-time traffic data via physical sensors and edge computing, 2) Conceptualization layer uses LangChain to process data and simulate traffic in a digital twin, 3) Action layer implements optimized traffic signal control through Model Context Protocol (MCP) and traffic management APIs, making autonomous decisions based on traffic congestion, delays, and patterns.
AI.result: The framework successfully minimizes waiting time at traffic lights and improves overall traffic flow effectiveness, outperforming both fixed-time traffic light systems and reinforcement learning-based baseline approaches.
AI.conclusion: Agentic AI-driven digital twin approach for traffic light optimization proves effective for real-time autonomous decision-making, reducing congestion and improving traffic efficiency through a structured three-layer system that integrates physical sensing, AI processing, and automated control actions.

title: Monitoring Neural Training with Topology: A Footprint-Predictable Collapse Index
summary: Representational collapse, where embeddings become anisotropic and lose multi-scale structure, can erode downstream performance long before performance metrics react. We propose an online, topology-aware monitor for evolving neural representations that couples Modular Morse Homology Maintenance (MMHM) with a composite Collapse Index (CI). Instead of rebuilding complexes each epoch, we apply sparse edits at a fixed scale and maintain a discrete Morse matching, yielding fast, incremental updates. Across LLM fine-tuning and temporal KGE training, CI provides a low-latency early-warning signal suitable for in-training interventions. Code and experimental scripts will be released publicly

AI.tldr: Proposes online topology-aware monitor combining Modular Morse Homology Maintenance and Collapse Index for early detection of representational collapse during neural network training.
AI.motivation: Representational collapse (embeddings becoming anisotropic and losing multi-scale structure) erodes downstream performance before performance metrics can detect it, requiring early detection methods.
AI.method: Combines Modular Morse Homology Maintenance (MMHM) with a composite Collapse Index (CI) to enable online, topology-aware monitoring of evolving neural representations using sparse edits at fixed scale instead of rebuilding complexes each epoch.
AI.result: CI provides low-latency early-warning signals across LLM fine-tuning and temporal KGE training scenarios, enabling in-training interventions.
AI.conclusion: The Composite Collapse Index (CI) coupled with Modular Morse Homology Maintenance provides an effective early-warning system for detecting representational collapse during neural network training, enabling timely interventions.

title: Compositional Meta-Learning for Mitigating Task Heterogeneity in Physics-Informed Neural Networks
summary: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) approximate solutions of partial differential equations (PDEs) by embedding physical laws into the loss function. In parameterized PDE families, variations in coefficients or boundary/initial conditions define distinct tasks. This makes training individual PINNs for each task computationally prohibitive, while cross-task transfer can be sensitive to task heterogeneity. While meta-learning can reduce retraining cost, existing methods often rely on a single global initialization and may suffer from negative transfer, particularly under feature-scarce coordinate inputs and limited training-task availability. We propose the Learning-Affinity Adaptive Modular Physics-Informed Neural Network (LAM-PINN), a compositional framework that leverages task-specific learning dynamics. LAM-PINN combines PDE parameters with learning-affinity metrics from brief transfer sessions to construct a task representation and cluster tasks even with coordinate-only inputs. It decomposes the model into cluster-specialized subnetworks and a shared meta network, and learns routing weights to selectively reuse modules instead of relying on a single global initialization. Across three PDE benchmarks, LAM-PINN achieves an average 19.7-fold reduction in mean squared error (MSE) on unseen tasks using only 10% of the training iterations required by conventional PINNs. These results indicate its effectiveness for generalization to unseen configurations within bounded design spaces of parameterized PDE families in resource-constrained engineering settings.

AI.tldr: LAM-PINN is a compositional framework that uses learning-affinity metrics to cluster parameterized PDE tasks, decomposing models into specialized subnetworks and shared meta networks for efficient transfer learning with reduced training iterations.
AI.motivation: Traditional PINNs are computationally expensive for parameterized PDE families where each variation requires individual training. Existing meta-learning methods suffer from negative transfer due to single global initialization, especially with coordinate-only inputs and limited training tasks.
AI.method: LAM-PINN combines PDE parameters with learning-affinity metrics from brief transfer sessions to create task representations, clusters similar tasks, and decomposes the model into cluster-specialized subnetworks and a shared meta network. It learns routing weights for selective module reuse instead of single global initialization.
AI.result: LAM-PINN achieves an average 19.7-fold reduction in MSE on unseen tasks using only 10% of the training iterations required by conventional PINNs, tested across three PDE benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The framework effectively generalizes to unseen configurations within bounded design spaces of parameterized PDE families, demonstrating practical value for resource-constrained engineering settings through its compositional, cluster-aware architecture.

title: ObjectGraph: From Document Injection to Knowledge Traversal -- A Native File Format for the Agentic Era
summary:
Every document format in existence was designed for a human reader moving linearly through text. Autonomous LLM agents do not read - they retrieve. This fundamental mismatch forces agents to inject entire documents into their context window, wasting tokens on irrelevant content, compounding state across multi-turn loops, and broadcasting information indiscriminately across agent roles. We argue this is not a prompt engineering problem, not a retrieval problem, and not a compression problem: it is a format problem.
  We introduce OBJECTGRAPH (.og), a file format that reconceives the document as a typed, directed knowledge graph to be traversed rather than a string to be injected. OBJECTGRAPH is a strict superset of Markdown - every .md file is a valid .og file - requires no infrastructure beyond a two-primitive query protocol, and is readable by both humans and agents without tooling.
  We formalize the Document Consumption Problem, characterise six structural properties no existing format satisfies simultaneously, and prove OBJECTGRAPH satisfies all six. We further introduce the Progressive Disclosure Model, the Role-Scoped Access Protocol, and Executable Assertion Nodes as native format primitives. Empirical evaluation across five document classes and eight agent task types demonstrates up to 95.3 percent token reduction with no statistically significant degradation in task accuracy (p > 0.05). Transpiler fidelity reaches 98.7 percent content preservation on a held-out document benchmark.

AI.tldr: OBJECTGRAPH (.og) is a new document format that structures documents as typed knowledge graphs instead of linear text, enabling LLM agents to retrieve relevant information efficiently rather than reading entire documents, achieving up to 95.3% token reduction.
AI.motivation: Existing document formats are designed for human linear reading, but LLM agents need retrieval capabilities. This mismatch forces agents to inject entire documents into context windows, wasting tokens on irrelevant content, compounding state across multi-turn loops, and broadcasting information indiscriminately across agent roles.
AI.method: Introduced OBJECTGRAPH (.og), a file format that reimagines documents as typed, directed knowledge graphs to be traversed rather than strings to be injected. It's a strict superset of Markdown, requires no infrastructure beyond a two-primitive query protocol, and is readable by both humans and agents without tooling. Introduced the Progressive Disclosure Model, Role-Scoped Access Protocol, and Executable Assertion Nodes as native format primitives.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation across five document classes and eight agent task types demonstrates up to 95.3% token reduction with no statistically significant degradation in task accuracy (p > 0.05). Transpiler fidelity reaches 98.7% content preservation on a held-out document benchmark. Proved OBJECTGRAPH satisfies all six structural properties no existing format satisfies simultaneously.
AI.conclusion: The document consumption problem is fundamentally a format problem, not a prompt engineering, retrieval, or compression problem. OBJECTGRAPH solves this by providing a format that enables efficient information retrieval for LLM agents while maintaining backward compatibility with Markdown and human readability.

title: Simple Self-Conditioning Adaptation for Masked Diffusion Models
summary: Masked diffusion models (MDMs) generate discrete sequences by iterative denoising under an absorbing masking process. In standard masked diffusion, if a token remains masked after a reverse update, the model discards its clean-state prediction for that position. Thus, still-masked positions must be repeatedly inferred from the mask token alone. This design choice limits cross-step refinement. To address this limitation, this paper proposes a simple, yet effective, post-training adaptation for MDMs that conditions each denoising step on the model's own previous clean-state predictions. The resulting method, called Self-Conditioned Masked Diffusion Models (SCMDM), requires minimal architectural change, does not introduce a recurrent latent-state pathway, does not rely on an auxiliary reference model, and adds no extra denoiser evaluations during sampling. This is an important departure from partial self-conditioning approaches which requires expensive model training from scratch. In particular, the paper shows that partial self-conditioning, including the commonly used 50% dropout strategy for training self-conditioned models from scratch, is suboptimal in the post-training regime. Instead, once the model's self-generated clean-state estimates become informative, the specialization to refinement is preferable to mixing conditional and unconditional objectives. SCMDM is evaluated across multiple domains, demonstrating consistent improvement over vanilla MDM baselines, achieving nearly a 50% reduction in generative perplexity on OWT-trained models (42.89 to 23.72), alongside strong improvements in discretized image synthesis quality, small molecular generation, and enhanced fidelity in genomic distribution modeling.

AI.tldr: Self-Conditioned Masked Diffusion Models (SCMDM) improve masked diffusion models by conditioning each denoising step on the model's own previous predictions, achieving better performance without retraining from scratch.
AI.motivation: Standard masked diffusion models discard clean-state predictions for tokens that remain masked after reverse updates, forcing still-masked positions to be repeatedly inferred from the mask token alone, which limits cross-step refinement.
AI.method: Proposes a simple post-training adaptation that conditions each denoising step on the model's own previous clean-state predictions, requiring minimal architectural changes, no recurrent latent-state pathway, no auxiliary reference model, and no extra denoiser evaluations during sampling.
AI.result: Demonstrates consistent improvement over vanilla MDM baselines across multiple domains: nearly 50% reduction in generative perplexity on OWT-trained models (42.89 to 23.72), strong improvements in discretized image synthesis quality, small molecular generation, and enhanced fidelity in genomic distribution modeling.
AI.conclusion: SCMDM provides an effective post-training adaptation for masked diffusion models that leverages self-generated clean-state estimates for better refinement, outperforming partial self-conditioning approaches in the post-training regime without requiring expensive retraining.

title: Binary Spiking Neural Networks as Causal Models
summary: We provide a causal analysis of Binary Spiking Neural Networks (BSNNs) to explain their behavior. We formally define a BSNN and represent its spiking activity as a binary causal model. Thanks to this causal representation, we are able to explain the output of the network by leveraging logic-based methods. In particular, we show that we can successfully use a SAT as well as a SMT solver to compute abductive explanations from this binary causal model. To illustrate our approach, we trained the BSNN on the standard MNIST dataset and applied our SAT-based and SMT-based methods to finding abductive explanations of the network's classifications based on pixel-level features. We also compared the found explanations against SHAP, a popular method used in the area of explainable AI. We show that, unlike SHAP, our approach guarantees that a found explanation does not contain completely irrelevant features.

AI.tldr: Causal analysis of Binary Spiking Neural Networks (BSNNs) using binary causal models and logic-based methods (SAT/SMT solvers) to compute abductive explanations, with comparison to SHAP showing superior feature relevance guarantees.
AI.motivation: To explain the behavior of Binary Spiking Neural Networks by providing formal causal analysis and reliable explanations that guarantee feature relevance, addressing limitations of existing methods like SHAP.
AI.method: Formally define BSNNs and represent spiking activity as binary causal models, then use SAT and SMT solvers to compute abductive explanations from these models. Apply to MNIST dataset and compare against SHAP.
AI.result: Successfully applied SAT-based and SMT-based methods to find abductive explanations of BSNN classifications on MNIST, demonstrating that unlike SHAP, the approach guarantees that found explanations do not contain completely irrelevant features.
AI.conclusion: Binary causal modeling combined with logic-based methods provides a rigorous approach for explaining BSNN behavior with guaranteed feature relevance, offering advantages over statistical methods like SHAP for interpretability in spiking neural networks.

title: Language Models Refine Mechanical Linkage Designs Through Symbolic Reflection and Modular Optimisation
summary: Designing mechanical linkages involves combinatorial topology selection and continuous parameter fitting. We show that language models can systematically improve linkage designs through symbolic representations. Language model agents explore discrete topologies while numerical optimisers fit continuous parameters. A symbolic lifting operator translates simulator trajectories into qualitative descriptors, motion labels, temporal predicates, and structural diagnostics that models interpret across iterative design cycles. Across six engineering-relevant motion targets and three open-source models (Llama 3.3 70B, Qwen3 4B, Qwen3 MoE 30B-A3B), the modular architecture reduces geometric error by up to 68% and improves structural validity by up to 134% over monolithic baselines. Critically, 78.6% of iterative refinement trajectories show measurable improvement, with the system correctly diagnosing overconstraint (56.3%) and underconstraint (35.6%) failure modes and proposing grounded corrections. Models across all three families acquire interpretable mechanical reasoning strategies without fine-tuning, demonstrating that principled symbolic abstraction bridges generative AI and the numerical precision required for engineering design.

AI.tldr: Language models combined with symbolic representations systematically improve mechanical linkage design through iterative refinement, reducing geometric error by up to 68% and improving structural validity by up to 134%.
AI.motivation: Mechanical linkage design involves complex combinatorial topology selection and continuous parameter fitting, requiring both discrete exploration and numerical precision that traditional approaches struggle to integrate effectively.
AI.method: A modular architecture where language model agents explore discrete topologies while numerical optimizers fit continuous parameters, using a symbolic lifting operator to translate simulator trajectories into qualitative descriptors, motion labels, temporal predicates, and structural diagnostics for iterative design cycles.
AI.result: Across six engineering-relevant motion targets and three language models (Llama 3.3 70B, Qwen3 4B, Qwen3 MoE 30B-A3B), the approach reduces geometric error by up to 68% and improves structural validity by up to 134% over baselines, with 78.6% of iterative refinement trajectories showing measurable improvement and correctly diagnosing failure modes (56.3% overconstraint, 35.6% underconstraint).
AI.conclusion: Principled symbolic abstraction bridges generative AI and numerical precision for engineering design, allowing language models across three families to acquire interpretable mechanical reasoning strategies without fine-tuning, demonstrating the viability of combining symbolic representations with generative AI for complex engineering tasks.

title: People-Centred Medical Image Analysis
summary: Recent advances in data-centric medical AI have produced highly accurate diagnostic systems, but the emphasis on data curation and performance metrics has not translated into widespread clinical adoption. We conjecture that this limited uptake stems from insufficient attention dedicated to the optimisation of fair performance across diverse patient populations and to workflow integration: performance biases can create regulatory barriers, and poorly integrated automation can disrupt clinical routines, degrade the quality of human-AI collaboration, and reduce clinicians' willingness to adopt AI tools. Prior work on workflow integration (e.g., Learning to Defer (L2D) and Learning to Complement (L2C)) and AI fairness has typically examined these challenges in isolation, overlooking their natural interdependence and the practical constraints of clinical environments, such as restricted clinician availability. We propose People-Centred Medical Image Analysis (PecMan), a human-AI framework that jointly optimises fairness, diagnostic accuracy, and workflow effectiveness through a dynamic gating mechanism that assigns cases to AI, clinicians, or both under clinician workload constraints. We also introduce the Fairness and Human-Centred AI (FairHAI) benchmark for evaluating trade-offs between accuracy, fairness, and clinician workload. Experiments using this benchmark show that PecMan consistently outperforms existing methods, paving the way for more trustworthy and clinically viable AI systems. Code will be available upon paper acceptance.

AI.tldr: PecMan is a human-AI framework that jointly optimizes fairness, accuracy, and workflow efficiency in medical image analysis by dynamically assigning cases to AI, clinicians, or both under workload constraints.
AI.motivation: Despite highly accurate diagnostic AI systems, clinical adoption remains limited due to insufficient attention to fair performance across diverse populations and poor workflow integration. Performance biases create regulatory barriers, while poorly integrated automation disrupts clinical routines, degrades human-AI collaboration quality, and reduces clinician willingness to adopt AI tools.
AI.method: Proposes People-Centred Medical Image Analysis (PecMan), a human-AI framework with a dynamic gating mechanism that assigns cases to AI, clinicians, or both under clinician workload constraints. Also introduces the Fairness and Human-Centred AI (FairHAI) benchmark for evaluating accuracy-fairness-workload trade-offs.
AI.result: Experiments using the FairHAI benchmark show that PecMan consistently outperforms existing methods in balancing accuracy, fairness, and clinician workload.
AI.conclusion: PecMan paves the way for more trustworthy and clinically viable AI systems by addressing the interdependence of fairness, accuracy, and workflow integration that has been overlooked in prior isolated approaches.

title: When Your LLM Reaches End-of-Life: A Framework for Confident Model Migration in Production Systems
summary: We present a framework for migrating production Large Language Model (LLM) based systems when the underlying model reaches end-of-life or requires replacement. The key contribution is a Bayesian statistical approach that calibrates automated evaluation metrics against human judgments, enabling confident model comparison even with limited manual evaluation data. We demonstrate this framework on a commercial question-answering system serving 5.3M monthly interactions across six global regions; evaluating correctness, refusal behavior, and stylistic adherence to successfully identify suitable replacement models. The framework is broadly applicable to any enterprise deploying LLM-based products, providing a principled, reproducible methodology for model migration that balances quality assurance with evaluation efficiency. This is a capability increasingly essential as the LLM ecosystem continues to evolve rapidly and organizations manage portfolios of AI-powered services across multiple models, regions, and use cases.

AI.tldr: generate a too long; didn't read summary
AI.motivation: describe the motivation in this paper
AI.method: method of this paper
AI.result: result of this paper
AI.conclusion: conclusion of this paper

title: When Continual Learning Moves to Memory: A Study of Experience Reuse in LLM Agents
summary: Memory-augmented LLM agents offer an appealing shortcut to continual learning: rather than updating model parameters, they accumulate experience in external memory, seemingly sidestepping the stability-plasticity dilemma of parametric learning. We show that this challenge does not disappear but resurfaces at the memory level. Under a limited context window, old and new experiences compete during retrieval, relocating the continual-learning bottleneck from parameter updates to memory access. To study this phenomenon, we introduce a (k,v) framework that disentangles two fundamental design axes of external memory: how experience is represented and how it is organized for retrieval. Across sequential-task experiments in ALFWorld and BabyAI, we find that abstract procedural memories transfer more reliably than detailed trajectories, while negative transfer disproportionately harms the hard cases. Moreover, finer-grained memory organization is not universally beneficial: designs that yield strong forward transfer can simultaneously induce severe forgetting. Together, these results reveal that external memory does not resolve the continual-learning problem; it reshapes it into a problem of memory representation and retrieval design.

AI.tldr: Memory-augmented LLM agents don't solve continual learning but reshape it into memory representation and retrieval design challenges.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether external memory in LLM agents truly sidesteps the stability-plasticity dilemma of continual learning, or merely relocates it to memory-level competition.
AI.method: Introduced a (k,v) framework to separate memory representation (experience representation) from organization (retrieval mechanisms). Tested different memory designs in sequential-task experiments in ALFWorld and BabyAI environments.
AI.result: Found that: 1) abstract procedural memories transfer better than detailed trajectories; 2) negative transfer harms hard cases disproportionately; 3) finer-grained memory organization isn't universally beneficial - designs enabling strong forward transfer can cause severe forgetting.
AI.conclusion: External memory doesn't resolve continual learning but transforms it into problems of memory representation and retrieval design, revealing that the stability-plasticity dilemma persists at the memory level.

title: End-to-end autonomous scientific discovery on a real optical platform
summary: Scientific research has long been human-led, driving new knowledge and transformative technologies through the continual revision of questions, methods and claims as evidence accumulates. Although large language model (LLM)-based agents are beginning to move beyond assisting predefined research workflows, none has yet demonstrated end-to-end autonomous discovery in a real physical system that produces a nontrivial result supported by experimental evidence. Here we introduce Qiushi Discovery Engine, an LLM-based agentic system for end-to-end autonomous scientific discovery on a real optical platform. Qiushi Engine combines nonlinear research phases, Meta-Trace memory and a dual-layer architecture to maintain adaptive and stable research trajectories across long-horizon investigations involving thousands of LLM-mediated reasoning, measurement and revision actions. It autonomously reproduces a published transmission-matrix experiment on a non-original platform and converts an abstract coherence-order theory into experimental observables, providing, to our knowledge, the first observation of this class of coherence-order structure. More importantly, in an open-ended study involving 145.9 million tokens, 3,242 LLM calls, 1,242 tool calls, 163 research notes and 44 scripts, Qiushi Engine proposes and experimentally validates optical bilinear interaction, a physical mechanism structurally analogous to a core operation in Transformer attention. This AI-discovered mechanism suggests a route towards high-speed, energy-efficient optical hardware for pairwise computation. To our knowledge, this is the first demonstration of an AI agentic system autonomously identifying and experimentally validating a nontrivial, previously unreported physical mechanism, marking a milestone for research-level autonomous agents.

AI.tldr: Qiushi Discovery Engine is an LLM-based agentic system that autonomously conducts scientific discovery on a real optical platform, achieving the first AI-driven identification and experimental validation of a new physical mechanism (optical bilinear interaction).
AI.motivation: To develop an LLM-based agentic system capable of end-to-end autonomous scientific discovery in a real physical system, moving beyond assisting predefined workflows to achieve nontrivial results supported by experimental evidence.
AI.method: The system combines nonlinear research phases, Meta-Trace memory, and a dual-layer architecture to maintain adaptive and stable research trajectories across long-horizon investigations involving thousands of LLM-mediated reasoning, measurement, and revision actions.
AI.result: Qiushi Engine autonomously reproduced a published transmission-matrix experiment on a non-original platform, converted an abstract coherence-order theory into experimental observables (first observation of this class of coherence-order structure), and, in an open-ended study, proposed and experimentally validated optical bilinear interaction—a physical mechanism structurally analogous to a core operation in Transformer attention.
AI.conclusion: The Qiushi Discovery Engine demonstrates the first instance of an AI agentic system autonomously identifying and experimentally validating a previously unreported physical mechanism (optical bilinear interaction), marking a significant milestone for research-level autonomous agents and suggesting a route toward high-speed, energy-efficient optical hardware for pairwise computation.

title: Automatic Causal Fairness Analysis with LLM-Generated Reporting
summary: AutoML, intended as the process of automating the application of machine learning to real-world problems, is a key step for AI popularisation. Most AutoML frameworks are not accounting for the potential lack of fairness in the training data and in the corresponding predictions. We introduce \textsc{FairMind}, a software prototype aiming to automatise fairness analysis at the dataset level. We achieve that by resorting to the assumptions of the \emph{standard fairness model}, recently proposed by Plečko and Bareinboim. This allows for a sound fairness evaluation in terms of causal effects, based on \emph{counterfactual} queries involving the target, possibly confounders and mediators, and the different values of an input feature we regard as \emph{protected}. After the necessary data preprocessing, the tool implements a closed-form computation of the effects. LLMs are consequently exploited to generate accurate reports on the fairness levels detected in the training dataset. We achieve that in a zero-shot setup and show by examples the expected advantages with respect to a direct analysis performed by the LLM. To favour applications, extensions to ordinal protected variable and continuous targets and novel decomposition results are also discussed.

AI.tldr: FairMind is an AutoML tool that automates fairness analysis at the dataset level using causal reasoning and counterfactual queries, with LLMs generating fairness reports in a zero-shot setup.
AI.motivation: Current AutoML frameworks fail to address fairness issues in training data and predictions, creating a need for automated fairness analysis tools to ensure AI popularization doesn't perpetuate biases.
AI.method: Uses the standard fairness model by Plečko and Bareinboim for causal fairness evaluation, implements closed-form computation of causal effects after preprocessing, and employs LLMs in zero-shot setup to generate fairness reports.
AI.result: Demonstrates advantages over direct LLM analysis through examples, discusses extensions for ordinal protected variables and continuous targets, and presents novel decomposition results for practical applications.
AI.conclusion: FairMind provides an automated approach to fairness analysis in datasets using causal methods and LLM-powered reporting, addressing a critical gap in current AutoML frameworks while offering practical extensions for real-world applications.

title: Think it, Run it: Autonomous ML pipeline generation via self-healing multi-agent AI
summary: The purpose of our paper is to develop a unified multi-agent architecture that automates end-to-end machine learning (ML) pipeline generation from datasets and natural-language (NL) goals, improving efficiency, robustness and explainability. A five-agent system is proposed to handle profiling, intent parsing, microservice recommendation, Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) construction and execution. It integrates code-grounded Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) for microservice understanding, an explainable hybrid recommender combining multiple criteria, a self-healing mechanism using Large Language Model (LLM)-based error interpretation and adaptive learning from execution history. The approach is evaluated on 150 ML tasks across diverse scenarios. The system achieves an 84.7% end-to-end pipeline success rate, outperforming baseline methods. It demonstrates improved robustness through self-healing and reduces workflow development time compared to manual construction. The study introduces a novel integration of code-grounded RAG, explainable recommendation, self-healing execution and adaptive learning within a single architecture, showing that tightly coupled intelligent components can outperform isolated solutions.

AI.tldr: Automated multi-agent system for end-to-end ML pipeline generation from datasets and natural language goals, achieving 84.7% success rate.
AI.motivation: To improve efficiency, robustness, and explainability of machine learning pipeline development by automating the process from datasets and natural language goals.
AI.method: Five-agent architecture: profiling, intent parsing, microservice recommendation, DAG construction, and execution. Uses code-grounded RAG for microservice understanding, explainable hybrid recommender, LLM-based self-healing, and adaptive learning from execution history.
AI.result: Achieved 84.7% end-to-end pipeline success rate on 150 ML tasks, outperforming baselines. Demonstrated improved robustness through self-healing and reduced workflow development time compared to manual construction.
AI.conclusion: The integration of code-grounded RAG, explainable recommendation, self-healing execution, and adaptive learning within a single architecture shows that tightly coupled intelligent components outperform isolated solutions.

title: Fidelity, Diversity, and Privacy: A Multi-Dimensional LLM Evaluation for Clinical Data Augmentation
summary: The scarcity of high-quality annotated medical data, particularly in mental health, poses a significant bottleneck for training robust machine learning models. Privacy regulations restrict data sharing, making synthetic data generation a promising alternative. The use of Large Language Models (LLMs) in a data augmentation pipeline could be leveraged as an alternative in this field. In the proposed methodology, DeepSeek-R1, OpenBioLLM-Llama3 and Qwen 3.5 are used to generate synthetic mental health evaluation reports conditioned on specific International Classification of Diseases, Tenth Revision (ICD-10) codes. Because naive text generation can lead to mode collapse or privacy breaches (memorization), a comprehensive evaluation framework is introduced. The generated diagnostic texts are assessed across three dimensions: semantic fidelity, lexical diversity, and privacy/plagiarism. The results demonstrate that all models can generate clinically coherent, diverse, and privacy-safe synthetic reports, significantly expanding the available training data for clinical natural language processing tasks without compromising patient confidentiality.

AI.tldr: LLMs generate synthetic mental health reports from ICD-10 codes, evaluated for clinical coherence, diversity, and privacy, effectively expanding training data while protecting confidentiality.
AI.motivation: The primary motivation is to address the scarcity of high-quality annotated medical data in mental health, which hinders the training of robust machine learning models. Privacy regulations further restrict data sharing, making synthetic data generation a promising alternative. The study explores using Large Language Models (LLMs) in a data augmentation pipeline to generate synthetic mental health evaluation reports as a solution.
AI.method: The methodology employs three Large Language Models (DeepSeek-R1, OpenBioLLM-Llama3, and Qwen 3.5) to generate synthetic mental health evaluation reports conditioned on specific ICD-10 codes. To address issues like mode collapse and privacy breaches, the study introduces a comprehensive evaluation framework that assesses generated texts across three dimensions: semantic fidelity (clinical coherence), lexical diversity, and privacy/plagiarism.
AI.result: The results demonstrate that all three LLMs (DeepSeek-R1, OpenBioLLM-Llama3, and Qwen 3.5) can generate clinically coherent, diverse, and privacy-safe synthetic mental health reports. This significantly expands the available training data for clinical natural language processing tasks while preserving patient confidentiality.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that using LLMs like DeepSeek-R1, OpenBioLLM-Llama3, and Qwen 3.5 to generate synthetic mental health evaluation reports is a viable and effective solution to overcome data scarcity while maintaining privacy. The proposed evaluation framework ensures the generated data is clinically coherent, diverse, and privacy-safe, making it suitable for expanding training datasets in clinical NLP tasks without compromising patient confidentiality.

title: Unsupervised Electrofacies Classification and Porosity Characterization in the Offshore Keta Basin Using Wireline Logs
summary: This study presents an unsupervised machine learning workflow for electrofacies analysis in the offshore Keta Basin, Ghana, where core data are scarce. Six standard wireline logs from Well~C were analysed over a depth interval comprising approximately $11{,}195$ samples. K-means clustering was applied in multivariate log space, with the clustering structure evaluated using inertia and silhouette diagnostics. Four clusters were identified, supported by an average silhouette coefficient of approximately $0.50$, indicating moderate but meaningful separation. The resulting electrofacies exhibit systematic, depth-continuous patterns associated with variations in clay content, porosity, and rock framework properties, forming a geological continuum from shale-dominated to cleaner sandstone-dominated units. The results demonstrate that log-only, unsupervised clustering supported by quantitative metrics provides a robust and reproducible framework for subsurface characterisation. The proposed workflow offers a practical tool for early-stage formation evaluation in frontier offshore basins and a foundation for future integrated studies.

AI.tldr: Unsupervised machine learning workflow using K-means clustering on wireline logs for electrofacies analysis in Ghana's offshore Keta Basin where core data is scarce.
AI.motivation: To address scarcity of core data in offshore Keta Basin, Ghana, by developing a robust electrofacies analysis method using wireline logs only.
AI.method: K-means clustering applied to six standard wireline logs from Well C (11,195 samples) with clustering structure evaluated using inertia and silhouette diagnostics.
AI.result: Four clusters identified with average silhouette coefficient of ~0.50, forming geological continuum from shale-dominated to sandstone-dominated units based on clay content, porosity, and rock framework properties.
AI.conclusion: Log-only unsupervised clustering with quantitative metrics provides robust framework for subsurface characterization, practical for early-stage formation evaluation in frontier offshore basins and foundation for integrated studies.

title: Dynamic Adversarial Fine-Tuning Reorganizes Refusal Geometry
summary: Safety-aligned language models must refuse harmful requests without collapsing into broad over-refusal, but the training-time mechanisms behind this tradeoff remain unclear. Prior work characterizes refusal directions and jailbreak robustness, yet does not explain how dynamic adversarial fine-tuning changes refusal carriers across training. We present a measurement-driven mechanism study, not a new defense, on one 7B backbone under supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and R2D2-style dynamic adversarial fine-tuning. Our protocol aligns fixed-source HarmBench, StrongREJECT, and XSTest with a five-anchor refusal-geometry suite and causal interventions. R2D2 drives fixed-source HarmBench ASR to 0.000 at steps 50 and 100, then partially reopens to 0.035 at step 250 and 0.250 at step 500; SFT remains less robust, with ASR between 0.505 and 0.588 at the same anchors. On XSTest, R2D2 any-refusal is 1.000 early, then falls to 0.664 and 0.228. Geometrically, R2D2 preserves a late-layer admissible carrier through step 100 before relocating to an early-layer carrier, while effective rank remains near 1.23--1.27. Causal interventions indicate low-dimensional but utility-coupled control. These results support a reorganization account rather than a drift-only account, with evidence limited to one backbone and fixed-source attacks.

AI.tldr: R2D2 dynamic adversarial fine-tuning shows strong initial refusal capability that partially decays over time, reorganizing refusal carriers from late to early layers, unlike SFT which maintains consistent but weaker refusal performance.
AI.motivation: Understanding how training-time mechanisms balance refusal of harmful requests without over-refusing harmless ones, and how adversarial fine-tuning changes refusal carriers across training stages.
AI.method: Measurement study on 7B backbone model comparing SFT and R2D2-style dynamic adversarial fine-tuning using fixed-source benchmarks (HarmBench, StrongREJECT, XSTest) with refusal-geometry analysis and causal interventions.
AI.result: R2D2 achieves near-perfect refusal early (ASR 0.000) but partially reopens later (ASR 0.250 by step 500), reorganizes refusal carriers from late to early layers, maintains low effective rank (~1.23-1.27), and shows low-dimensional utility-coupled control.
AI.conclusion: Results support a reorganization account (relocation of refusal mechanisms) rather than drift-only account, with evidence limited to one backbone and fixed-source attacks, highlighting tradeoffs in dynamic adversarial training.

title: TRUST: A Framework for Decentralized AI Service v.0.1
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) and Multi-Agent Systems (MAS) in high-stakes domains demand reliable verification, yet centralized approaches suffer four limitations: (1) Robustness, with single points of failure vulnerable to attacks and bias; (2) Scalability, as reasoning complexity creates bottlenecks; (3) Opacity, as hidden auditing erodes trust; and (4) Privacy, as exposed reasoning traces risk model theft. We introduce TRUST (Transparent, Robust, and Unified Services for Trustworthy AI), a decentralized framework with three innovations: (i) Hierarchical Directed Acyclic Graphs (HDAGs) that decompose Chain-of-Thought reasoning into five abstraction levels for parallel distributed auditing; (ii) the DAAN protocol, which projects multi-agent interactions into Causal Interaction Graphs (CIGs) for deterministic root-cause attribution; and (iii) a multi-tier consensus mechanism among computational checkers, LLM evaluators, and human experts with stake-weighted voting that guarantees correctness under 30% adversarial participation. We prove a Safety-Profitability Theorem ensuring honest auditors profit while malicious actors incur losses. All decisions are recorded on-chain, while privacy-by-design segmentation prevents reconstruction of proprietary logic. Across multiple LLMs and benchmarks, TRUST attains 72.4% accuracy (4-18% above baselines) and remains resilient against 20% corruption. DAAN reaches 70% root-cause attribution (vs. 54-63% for standard methods) with 60% token savings. Human studies validate the design (F1 = 0.89, Brier = 0.074). The framework supports (A1) decentralized auditing, (A2) tamper-proof leaderboards, (A3) trustless data annotation, and (A4) governed autonomous agents, pioneering decentralized AI auditing for safe, accountable deployment of reasoning-capable systems.

AI.tldr: TRUST is a decentralized framework for reliable verification of Large Reasoning Models and Multi-Agent Systems, addressing robustness, scalability, opacity, and privacy limitations through hierarchical DAGs, causal interaction graphs, and multi-tier consensus.
AI.motivation: Current centralized verification approaches for high-stakes AI systems suffer from four key limitations: lack of robustness (single points of failure vulnerable to attacks), scalability issues (bottlenecks in complex reasoning), opacity (hidden auditing erodes trust), and privacy risks (exposed reasoning traces enable model theft).
AI.method: TRUST introduces three innovations: 1) Hierarchical Directed Acyclic Graphs (HDAGs) that decompose Chain-of-Thought reasoning into five abstraction levels for parallel distributed auditing; 2) DAAN protocol that projects multi-agent interactions into Causal Interaction Graphs for deterministic root-cause attribution; 3) Multi-tier consensus mechanism with computational checkers, LLM evaluators, and human experts using stake-weighted voting.
AI.result: The framework achieves 72.4% accuracy (4-18% above baselines) while remaining resilient against 20% corruption. DAAN reaches 70% root-cause attribution (vs. 54-63% for standard methods) with 60% token savings. Human studies validate the design (F1 = 0.89, Brier = 0.074). It supports decentralized auditing, tamper-proof leaderboards, trustless data annotation, and governed autonomous agents.
AI.conclusion: TRUST pioneers decentralized AI auditing that addresses critical verification challenges in high-stakes AI systems, proving that decentralized approaches can ensure safety, accountability, and reliability while maintaining privacy and enabling honest participants to profit through the Safety-Profitability Theorem.

title: NORACL: Neurogenesis for Oracle-free Resource-Adaptive Continual Learning
summary: In a continual learning setting, we require a model to be plastic enough to learn a new task and stable enough to not disturb previously learned capabilities. We argue that this dilemma has an architectural root. A finite network has limited representational and plastic resources, yet the required capacity depends on properties of the future task stream that are unknown: how many tasks will be encountered, and how much they overlap in feature space. Regularization-based methods preserve past knowledge within fixed-capacity architectures and therefore implicitly rely on an oracle architecture sized for this unknown future. When tasks are only weakly related, fixed architectures progressively run out of plastic resources; when tasks are few or strongly overlapping, models are often over-provisioned. Inspired by neurogenesis in biology, we propose NORACL to address the stability-plasticity dilemma by tackling the oracle architecture problem through neuronal growth. Starting from a compact network, NORACL grows only when needed by monitoring two complementary signals for representational and plasticity saturation. We evaluate NORACL against oracle-sized static baselines across varying task counts and geometries. Across all settings, NORACL achieves final average accuracies that are better than or on par with oracle-provisioned static baselines while using fewer parameters. Additionally, NORACL yields architectures with interpretable growth, i.e. dissimilar tasks predominantly expand feature-extraction layers, whereas tasks which rely on common features shift growth toward later feature-combination layers. Our analysis further explains why fixed-capacity networks lose plasticity as tasks accumulate, whereas NORACL creates fresh capacity for new tasks through growth. Together, these results show that adaptive neurogenesis pushes the stability-plasticity Pareto frontier of continual learning.

AI.tldr: NORACL addresses continual learning's stability-plasticity dilemma through adaptive neuronal growth that expands network capacity only when needed, outperforming fixed-capacity baselines while using fewer parameters.
AI.motivation: Continual learning faces the stability-plasticity dilemma: models need plasticity for new tasks but stability to preserve past knowledge. Fixed-capacity architectures struggle because the optimal network size depends on unknown future task properties (number and overlap). Over-sized networks waste resources, while under-sized ones lose plasticity as tasks accumulate.
AI.method: NORACL (Neuronal Growth for Continual Learning) starts with a compact network and grows it adaptively based on two signals: representational saturation (when existing capacity can't represent new tasks) and plasticity saturation (when existing neurons lose plasticity). Inspired by neurogenesis in biology, the method adds neurons only when necessary rather than using fixed-capacity architectures.
AI.result: NORACL achieves better or comparable final average accuracies compared to oracle-sized static baselines across varying task counts and geometries while using fewer parameters. The method yields interpretable growth patterns: dissimilar tasks expand feature-extraction layers, while tasks sharing common features shift growth toward later feature-combination layers. Analysis shows NORACL maintains plasticity by creating fresh capacity for new tasks.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive neurogenesis addresses the oracle architecture problem in continual learning by dynamically expanding network capacity based on task requirements. This approach pushes the stability-plasticity Pareto frontier, showing that neuronal growth is a promising direction for continual learning beyond fixed-capacity regularization methods.

title: Unpacking Vibe Coding: Help-Seeking Processes in Student-AI Interactions While Programming
comment: Accepted by the 27th International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Education (AIED'26)
summary: Generative AI is reshaping higher education programming through vibe coding, where students collaborate with AI via natural language rather than writing code line-by-line. We conceptualize this practice as help-seeking, analyzing 19,418 interaction turns from 110 undergraduate students. Using inductive coding and Heterogeneous Transition Network Analysis, we examined interaction sequences to compare top- and low-performing students. Results reveal that top performers engaged in instrumental help-seeking -- inquiry and exploration -- eliciting tutor-like AI responses. In contrast, low performers relied on executive help-seeking, frequently delegating tasks and prompting the AI to assume an executor role focused on ready-made solutions. These findings indicate that currently generative AI mirrors student intent (whether productive or passive) rather than optimizing for learning. To evolve from tools to teammates, AI systems must move beyond passive compliance. We argue for pedagogically aligned design that detect unproductive delegation and adaptively steer educational interactions toward inquiry, ensuring student-AI partnerships augment rather than replace cognitive effort.

AI.tldr: Top students use generative AI for inquiry and exploration (instrumental help), while low performers delegate tasks (executive help), showing AI mirrors student intent rather than optimizing learning. AI should evolve to detect unproductive delegation and steer toward productive inquiry.
AI.motivation: Generative AI is transforming programming education through "vibe coding" where students collaborate with AI using natural language. The paper aims to understand how students engage with AI for programming help, examining whether these interactions promote learning or merely provide solutions.
AI.method: Analyzed 19,418 interaction turns from 110 undergraduate students using inductive coding and Heterogeneous Transition Network Analysis to examine interaction sequences. Compared top- and low-performing students' help-seeking behaviors with generative AI.
AI.result: Top performers engaged in instrumental help-seeking (inquiry and exploration), eliciting tutor-like AI responses. Low performers relied on executive help-seeking, frequently delegating tasks and prompting AI to act as an executor providing ready-made solutions. AI currently mirrors student intent rather than optimizing for learning.
AI.conclusion: To evolve from tools to teammates, AI systems must move beyond passive compliance and detect unproductive delegation. Pedagogically aligned designs should adaptively steer interactions toward inquiry to ensure student-AI partnerships augment rather than replace cognitive effort.

title: Cross-Subject Generalization for EEG Decoding: A Survey of Deep Learning Methods
comment: Accepted manuscript in Progress in Biomedical Engineering
summary: Deep learning for cross-subject EEG decoding is hindered by high inter-subject variability, which introduces a severe domain shift between training and unseen test subjects. This survey presents a comprehensive review of deep learning methodologies specifically engineered to address this cross-subject generalization challenge. To ground this analysis, we formalize the cross-subject setting as a multi-source domain problem and delineate the rigorous, subject-independent evaluation protocols required for valid assessment. Central to this survey is a systematic taxonomy of the current literature into discrete methodological families, including feature alignment, adversarial learning, feature disentanglement, and contrastive learning. We conclude by examining three critical elements for advancing robust, real-world decoding: the theoretical limitations of current methodologies, the structural value of subject identity, and the emergence of EEG foundation models.

AI.tldr: Comprehensive survey of deep learning methods for cross-subject EEG decoding, focusing on techniques to handle inter-subject variability and domain shift through systematic categorization of methodologies.
AI.motivation: Deep learning for EEG decoding faces challenges due to high inter-subject variability causing domain shift between training and test subjects, limiting generalization to unseen subjects.
AI.method: Formalizes cross-subject EEG as a multi-source domain problem, establishes rigorous subject-independent evaluation protocols, and creates systematic taxonomy categorizing methods into families including feature alignment, adversarial learning, feature disentanglement, and contrastive learning.
AI.result: A comprehensive review framework that organizes existing deep learning approaches for cross-subject EEG generalization into discrete methodological families with clear evaluation protocols.
AI.conclusion: Future research should focus on theoretical limitations of current methods, the structural value of subject identity information, and the development of EEG foundation models to advance robust, real-world decoding applications.

title: Optimal Stop-Loss and Take-Profit Parameterization for Autonomous Trading Agent Swarm
summary: Autonomous crypto trading systems often spend most of their design effort on finding entries, while exits are left to fixed rules that are rarely tested in a systematic way. This paper examines whether better stop-loss and take-profit settings can improve the performance of an autonomous trading agent swarm. Using more than 900 historical trades, we replay each trade under many alternative exit policies and compare results against the existing production setup. The study finds that exit design matters meaningfully: stronger configurations improve risk-adjusted performance and generally favor tighter loss limits, earlier profit capture, and closer trailing protection. The paper also discusses a key evaluation challenge: a purely chronological split was initially used, but the newest trades fell into an unusual war-driven market period that sharply distorted test results. To reduce the influence of that single episode, the main comparison was run on randomized data, with the drawbacks of doing so acknowledged explicitly. Overall, the paper presents a practical framework for tuning exit logic in a more disciplined and transparent way.

AI.tldr: Systematic testing of exit strategies (stop-loss/take-profit) improves crypto trading agent performance through tighter risk controls and earlier profit capture.
AI.motivation: Autonomous crypto trading systems typically focus on entry strategies while using simplistic, untested exit rules, creating a gap in systematic evaluation of exit policy effectiveness.
AI.method: Analyzed 900+ historical trades by replaying each under alternative exit policies; initially used chronological split but switched to randomized data to avoid distortion from war-driven market period; compared results against existing production setup.
AI.result: Better exit configurations meaningfully improve risk-adjusted performance, favoring tighter loss limits, earlier profit capture, and closer trailing protection; pure chronological splits proved problematic due to market anomalies.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical framework for more disciplined exit logic tuning in crypto trading systems, emphasizing systematic testing and acknowledging evaluation challenges like market period distortions.

title: Detecting Clinical Discrepancies in Health Coaching Agents: A Dual-Stream Memory and Reconciliation Architecture
summary: As Large Language Model (LLM) agents transition from single-session tools to persistent systems managing longitudinal healthcare journeys, their memory architectures face a critical challenge: reconciling two imperfect sources of truth. The patient's evolving self-report is current but prone to recall bias, while the Electronic Health Record (EHR) is medically validated but frequently stale. General-purpose agent memory systems optimize for coherence by overwriting older facts with the user's latest statement, a pattern that risks safety failures when applied to clinical data. We introduce a Dual-Stream Memory Architecture that strictly separates the patient narrative from the structured clinical record (FHIR), governed by a dedicated Reconciliation Engine that evaluates every extracted memory against the patient's FHIR profile and classifies discrepancies by type, severity, and the specific FHIR resources involved. We evaluate this architecture on 26 patients across 675 longitudinal wellness coaching sessions, using a hybrid dataset that interleaves real provider-patient transcripts with synthetic, FHIR-grounded clinical scenarios. In isolated testing, the engine detects 84.4% of designed clinical discrepancies with 86.7% safety-critical recall. By coupling extraction and reconciliation evaluation on the same data, we directly quantify a 13.6% error cascade, tracing the degradation to clinical details lost during memory extraction from unstructured conversation rather than to downstream classification errors. These findings establish that validating patient-reported memories against clinical records is both feasible and necessary for safe deployment of longitudinal health agents.

AI.tldr: A dual-stream memory architecture for LLM agents in healthcare separates patient narratives from structured EHR data (FHIR) and reconciles discrepancies. Evaluation shows effective detection of clinical discrepancies (84.4%) and identifies error cascades from memory extraction (13.6%).
AI.motivation: Large Language Model agents in healthcare face a critical challenge: reconciling imperfect sources of truth. Patient self-reports are current but prone to recall bias, while Electronic Health Records are medically validated but frequently stale. General-purpose agent memory systems that overwrite older facts with latest statements risk safety failures when applied to clinical data.
AI.method: A Dual-Stream Memory Architecture that separates patient narrative from structured clinical records (FHIR), governed by a Reconciliation Engine that evaluates every extracted memory against the patient's FHIR profile, classifying discrepancies by type, severity, and specific FHIR resources involved.
AI.result: Evaluation on 26 patients across 675 longitudinal wellness coaching sessions shows: In isolated testing, the engine detects 84.4% of designed clinical discrepancies with 86.7% safety-critical recall. A 13.6% error cascade was quantified, with degradation traced to clinical details lost during memory extraction from unstructured conversation rather than downstream classification errors.
AI.conclusion: The validation of patient-reported memories against clinical records through a dual-stream memory architecture is both feasible and necessary for safe deployment of longitudinal health agents, highlighting the critical need to prevent error cascades in clinical AI systems.

title: Step-level Optimization for Efficient Computer-use Agents
summary: Computer-use agents provide a promising path toward general software automation because they can interact directly with arbitrary graphical user interfaces instead of relying on brittle, application-specific integrations. Despite recent advances in benchmark performance, strong computer-use agents remain expensive and slow in practice, since most systems invoke large multimodal models at nearly every interaction step. We argue that this uniform allocation of compute is fundamentally inefficient for long-horizon GUI tasks. Such trajectories are highly heterogeneous: many steps are routine and can be handled reliably by smaller, cheaper policies, while errors tend to concentrate at a relatively small number of high-risk moments. Across computer-use benchmarks, these failures repeatedly take two forms: progress stalls, where the agent loops, repeats ineffective actions, or fails to make meaningful progress, and silent semantic drift, where the agent continues taking locally plausible actions after already deviating from the user's true goal. To address this inefficiency, we propose an event-driven, step-level cascade for computer-use agents that runs a small policy by default and escalates to a stronger model only when lightweight learned monitors detect elevated risk. Our framework combines two complementary signals: a Stuck Monitor that detects degraded progress from recent reasoning-action history and triggers recovery, and a Milestone Monitor that identifies semantically meaningful checkpoints where sparse verification is most informative for catching drift. This design turns always-on frontier-model inference into adaptive, on-demand compute allocation over the course of an evolving interaction. The framework is modular and deployment-oriented: it can be layered on top of existing computer-use agents without changing the underlying agent architecture or retraining the large model.

AI.tldr: An event-driven cascade framework for computer-use agents that uses small policies by default and escalates to larger models only when lightweight monitors detect high-risk moments, improving efficiency and reducing compute costs.
AI.motivation: Current computer-use agents are expensive and slow because they uniformly use large multimodal models at every interaction step, which is inefficient for long-horizon GUI tasks where many steps are routine while errors concentrate at specific high-risk moments like progress stalls and semantic drift.
AI.method: Proposed an event-driven, step-level cascade framework with two complementary learned monitors: a Stuck Monitor that detects degraded progress from recent reasoning-action history and triggers recovery, and a Milestone Monitor that identifies semantically meaningful checkpoints for sparse verification to catch semantic drift. Uses small policies by default and escalates to stronger models only when monitors detect elevated risk.
AI.result: The framework provides adaptive, on-demand compute allocation instead of always-on frontier-model inference, turning expensive uniform compute into efficient selective allocation. It's modular and deployment-oriented, can be layered on existing computer-use agents without changing underlying architecture or retraining large models.
AI.conclusion: This approach addresses the fundamental inefficiency of uniform compute allocation in long-horizon GUI tasks by selectively applying expensive models only at high-risk moments, making computer-use agents more practical, efficient, and cost-effective while maintaining performance.

title: Learning to Forget: Continual Learning with Adaptive Weight Decay
summary: Continual learning agents with finite capacity must balance acquiring new knowledge with retaining the old. This requires controlled forgetting of knowledge that is no longer needed, freeing up capacity to learn. Weight decay, viewed as a mechanism for forgetting, can serve this role by gradually discarding information stored in the weights. However, a fixed scalar weight decay drives this forgetting uniformly over time and uniformly across all parameters, even when some encode stable knowledge while others track rapidly changing targets. We introduce Forgetting through Adaptive Decay (FADE), which adapts per-parameter weight decay rates online via approximate meta-gradient descent. We derive FADE for the online linear setting and apply it to the final layer of neural networks. Our empirical analysis shows that FADE automatically discovers distinct decay rates for different parameters, complements step-size adaptation, and consistently improves over fixed weight decay across online tracking and streaming classification problems.

AI.tldr: FADE introduces adaptive weight decay that automatically adjusts forgetting rates per parameter, outperforming fixed decay for continual learning.
AI.motivation: Continual learning requires forgetting old knowledge to free capacity for new learning, but fixed weight decay uniformly forgets all parameters regardless of their stability.
AI.method: Forgetting through Adaptive Decay (FADE) adapts per-parameter weight decay rates online via approximate meta-gradient descent, derived for online linear setting and applied to neural network final layers.
AI.result: FADE discovers distinct decay rates per parameter, complements step-size adaptation, and improves over fixed weight decay in online tracking and streaming classification.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive forgetting mechanisms like FADE are crucial for continual learning systems to intelligently manage knowledge retention versus acquisition.

title: Interval Orders, Biorders and Credibility-limited Belief Revision
summary: Rational belief revision is commonly viewed as being based on a preference order between possible worlds, with the resulting new belief set being those sentences true in all the most preferred models of the incoming new information. Usually, such a preference order is taken to be a total preorder. Nevertheless, there are other, more general classes of ordering that can also be employed. In this paper, we explore two such classes that have been studied within the theory of rational choice but have seen limited or no application in belief revision. We begin with interval orders, introduced by Fishburn in the '80s, which associate with each possible world a nonnegative `interval' of plausibility. We then move on to biorders, studied by Aleskerov, Bouyssou, and Monjardet, which generalise interval orders by allowing the intervals to have negative lengths, a feature that can be used to capture a notion of dissonance or instability. We provide axiomatic characterisations of these two resulting families of belief revision operators, as well as of two further families of interest that lie between interval orders and biorders. We show that while biorder-based revisions satisfy the Success postulate, they do not always yield consistent outputs. By modifying their definition to discard inputs that lead to inconsistency as `incredible', we derive new families of so-called non-prioritised revision that satisfy the Consistency postulate, but not the Success one. These families are linked to credibility-limited revision operators of Hansson et al., but for which the set of credible sentences does not satisfy the single-sentence closure condition. We argue that the biorder-based approach is well-suited for scenarios where an agent might initially reject new information, but may accept it when presented with additional explanation.

AI.tldr: This paper explores two classes of preference orders (interval orders and biorders) for belief revision, moving beyond traditional total preorders to capture notions of dissonance and instability, leading to new families of non-prioritized revision operators.
AI.motivation: Rational belief revision typically uses total preorders to represent preference between possible worlds, but more general ordering classes exist in rational choice theory that haven't been applied to belief revision. The paper aims to explore interval orders and biorders to capture more nuanced aspects of belief change, including dissonance and instability.
AI.method: The paper explores interval orders (with nonnegative plausibility intervals) and biorders (generalizing interval orders to allow negative lengths). It provides axiomatic characterizations of belief revision operators based on these orderings, including intermediate families between interval orders and biorders. It then modifies the definitions to handle inconsistency by discarding "incredible" inputs, creating new families of non-prioritized revision operators.
AI.result: The study shows that biorder-based revisions satisfy the Success postulate but don't always yield consistent outputs. By modifying them to discard inconsistent inputs as "incredible," the paper derives new families of non-prioritized revision that satisfy Consistency but not Success. These families are linked to credibility-limited revision operators but without single-sentence closure.
