Here we show that a single self-supervised representation can generalize across heterogeneous brain MRI endpoints. We trained BrainDINO, a self-distilled foundation model, on approximately 6.6 million unlabeled axial slices from 20 datasets encompassing broad variation in population, disease, and acquisition setting. Using a frozen encoder with lightweight task heads, BrainDINO supported transfer across tumor segmentation, neurodegenerative and neurodevelopmental conditions classification, brain age estimation, post-stroke temporal prediction, molecular status prediction, MRI sequence classification, and survival modeling. Across tasks and supervision regimes, BrainDINO consistently equaled or exceeded natural-image and MRI-specific self-supervised baselines, with particularly strong advantages under label scarcity. Representation analyses further showed anatomically organized and pathology-sensitive feature structure in the absence of task-specific supervision. Our findings indicate that large-scale slice-wise self-supervised learning can yield a unified brain MRI representation that supports diverse neuroimaging tasks without volumetric pretraining or full-network fine-tuning, establishing a scalable foundation for robust and data-efficient brain imaging analysis.

AI.tldr: BrainDINO is a self-supervised foundation model trained on 6.6M unlabeled brain MRI slices that generalizes across diverse neuroimaging tasks without task-specific supervision, outperforming existing methods especially in low-data scenarios.
AI.motivation: Most learning-based brain MRI methods are task-specific and require substantial labeled data, which is expensive and limited. There's a need for a unified representation that can generalize across diverse neuroimaging applications without heavy task-specific supervision.
AI.method: BrainDINO is a self-distilled foundation model trained on approximately 6.6 million unlabeled axial MRI slices from 20 diverse datasets covering different populations, diseases, and acquisition settings. The model uses a frozen encoder with lightweight task heads for transfer learning.
AI.result: BrainDINO successfully transferred across 8 different neuroimaging tasks including tumor segmentation, neurodegenerative classification, brain age estimation, stroke prediction, molecular status prediction, sequence classification, and survival modeling. It consistently equaled or exceeded both natural-image and MRI-specific self-supervised baselines, with particularly strong advantages when labeled data was scarce.
AI.conclusion: Large-scale slice-wise self-supervised learning can create a unified brain MRI representation that supports diverse neuroimaging tasks without requiring volumetric pretraining or full-network fine-tuning, establishing a scalable foundation for robust and data-efficient brain imaging analysis.

title: Investigating More Explainable and Partition-Free Compositionality Estimation for LLMs: A Rule-Generation Perspective
summary: Compositional generalization tests are often used to estimate the compositionality of LLMs. However, such tests have the following limitations: (1) they only focus on the output results without considering LLMs' understanding of sample compositionality, resulting in explainability defects; (2) they rely on dataset partition to form the test set with combinations unseen in the training set, suffering from combination leakage issues. In this work, we propose a novel rule-generation perspective for compositionality estimation for LLMs. It requires LLMs to generate a program as rules for dataset mapping and provides estimates of the compositionality of LLMs using complexity-based theory. The perspective addresses the limitations of compositional generalization tests and provides a new way to analyze the compositionality characterization of LLMs. We conduct experiments and analysis of existing advanced LLMs based on this perspective on a string-to-grid task, and find various compositionality characterizations and compositionality deficiencies exhibited by LLMs.

AI.tldr: A novel rule-generation approach for estimating LLM compositionality using program complexity theory, addressing limitations of traditional compositional generalization tests.
AI.motivation: Traditional compositional generalization tests have two main limitations: (1) they only focus on output results without considering LLMs' understanding of compositionality (explainability defects), and (2) they suffer from combination leakage issues due to dataset partition methods.
AI.method: Proposes a rule-generation perspective where LLMs generate programs as rules for dataset mapping, using complexity-based theory to estimate compositionality, addressing the limitations of existing tests.
AI.result: Experiments on a string-to-grid task with existing advanced LLMs reveal various compositionality characterizations and deficiencies exhibited by LLMs.
AI.conclusion: The proposed rule-generation perspective provides a new way to analyze LLM compositionality that addresses the limitations of traditional compositional generalization tests, offering better explainability and avoiding combination leakage issues.

title: Predicting Covariate-Driven Spatial Deformation for Nonstationary Gaussian Processes
summary:
Nonstationary Gaussian processes (GPs) are essential for modeling complex, locally heterogeneous spatial data. A common modeling approach is the spatial deformation method that warps the domain to recover isotropy. However, this static method does not account for changes in spatial correlation induced by covariates, limiting its ability to predict nonstationary GPs under new covariate conditions.
  To enable predictive modeling of the deformation method, we propose to model the spatial deformation as a function of covariates. The spaces of diffeomorphic deformations and Euclidean covariate vectors are connected by characterizing deformations as generated by velocity fields living in a Lie algebra. To overcome the estimation instability caused by high-order interactions between multiple covariates in a general Lie algebra, we prove that those interactions can be truncated with a moderate physical assumption. Based on the theoretical results, a concise functional form of deformations driven by multiple covariates can be established, and an efficient estimation-inference algorithm is developed for out-of-sample nonstationary GP prediction with limited covariate-deformation sample pairs. The effectiveness and generalizability of the method are demonstrated on a simulation study and two case studies, in the fields of manufacturing and geostatistics, respectively.

AI.tldr: Proposes a method to model spatial deformation as a function of covariates for nonstationary Gaussian process prediction, enabling out-of-sample prediction under new covariate conditions.
AI.motivation: Current spatial deformation methods for nonstationary Gaussian processes are static and don't account for how covariates affect spatial correlation, limiting predictive ability under new covariate conditions.
AI.method: Models spatial deformation as a function of covariates using Lie algebra theory, truncates high-order interactions between covariates, develops concise functional form of deformations, and creates efficient estimation-inference algorithm for limited sample scenarios.
AI.result: The method demonstrates effectiveness and generalizability in simulation studies and two real-world case studies (manufacturing and geostatistics), enabling out-of-sample nonstationary GP prediction with limited covariate-deformation sample pairs.
AI.conclusion: By connecting covariate spaces to deformation spaces via Lie algebra theory and developing an efficient algorithm, the proposed method enables predictive modeling of nonstationary Gaussian processes under new covariate conditions.

title: Heterogeneous Scientific Foundation Model Collaboration
summary: Agentic large language model systems have demonstrated strong capabilities. However, their reliance on language as the universal interface fundamentally limits their applicability to many real-world problems, especially in scientific domains where domain-specific foundation models have been developed to address specialized tasks beyond natural language. In this work, we introduce Eywa, a heterogeneous agentic framework designed to extend language-centric systems to a broader class of scientific foundation models. The key idea of Eywa is to augment domain-specific foundation models with a language-model-based reasoning interface, enabling language models to guide inference over non-linguistic data modalities. This design allows predictive foundation models, which are typically optimized for specialized data and tasks, to participate in higher-level reasoning and decision-making processes within agentic systems. Eywa can serve as a drop-in replacement for a single-agent pipeline (EywaAgent) or be integrated into existing multi-agent systems by replacing traditional agents with specialized agents (EywaMAS). We further investigate a planning-based orchestration framework in which a planner dynamically coordinates traditional agents and Eywa agents to solve complex tasks across heterogeneous data modalities (EywaOrchestra). We evaluate Eywa across a diverse set of scientific domains spanning physical, life, and social sciences. Experimental results demonstrate that Eywa improves performance on tasks involving structured and domain-specific data, while reducing reliance on language-based reasoning through effective collaboration with specialized foundation models.

AI.tldr: Eywa extends language-centric agentic systems to integrate specialized foundation models for better reasoning over non-linguistic scientific data.
AI.motivation: Language-centric agentic systems have limited applicability to real-world problems, especially in scientific domains where domain-specific foundation models exist for specialized tasks beyond natural language.
AI.method: Eywa augments domain-specific foundation models with a language-model-based reasoning interface, enabling language models to guide inference over non-linguistic data. It can function as a single-agent pipeline (EywaAgent), be integrated into multi-agent systems (EywaMAS), or use a planning-based orchestration framework (EywaOrchestra).
AI.result: Eywa improves performance on tasks involving structured and domain-specific data across physical, life, and social sciences, while reducing reliance on language-based reasoning through collaboration with specialized foundation models.
AI.conclusion: Eywa presents a novel framework for integrating domain-specific foundation models into language-centric agentic systems, enabling more effective reasoning over non-linguistic data modalities and reducing reliance on language-based reasoning.

title: BoostLoRA: Growing Effective Rank by Boosting Adapters
summary: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods face a tradeoff between adapter size and expressivity: ultra-low-parameter adapters are confined to fixed low-rank subspaces, capping performance even with extended training. We propose BoostLoRA, a gradient-boosting framework that overcomes this limit by iteratively training and merging minimal adapters on the examples the current model gets wrong. A ROTATE SVD basis strategy assigns each round to an orthogonal subspace, so cumulative effective rank grows linearly with the number of rounds while each adapter remains ultra-low-rank. After merging, adapters are discarded, leaving zero inference overhead. On Qwen2.5-3B, BoostLoRA reaches 89.1% on GSM8K and 68.8% on MATH-500, surpassing both the best single-shot ultra-low parameter adapter (TinyLoRA) and full fine-tuning; on code generation it reaches 57.2% on MBPP and 80.4% on HumanEval while full fine-tuning drops below the zero-shot baseline. We also demonstrate cross-architecture transfer on protein binding classification with ESM2-650M and cross-entropy training. BoostLoRA is, to our knowledge, the first PEFT method whose effective rank grows with training, separating per-round parameter cost from total representational capacity.

AI.tldr: BoostLoRA is a gradient-boosting PEFT framework that iteratively trains and merges ultra-low-rank adapters on difficult examples, enabling cumulative effective rank growth without inference overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing PEFT methods face a tradeoff between adapter size and expressivity, where ultra-low-parameter adapters are limited to fixed low-rank subspaces, capping performance even with extended training.
AI.method: Proposes BoostLoRA, a gradient-boosting framework that iteratively trains and merges minimal adapters on examples the current model gets wrong, using a ROTATE SVD basis strategy to assign each round to an orthogonal subspace.
AI.result: On Qwen2.5-3B, achieves 89.1% on GSM8K and 68.8% on MATH-500, surpassing both TinyLoRA and full fine-tuning; on code generation, reaches 57.2% on MBPP and 80.4% on HumanEval while full fine-tuning drops below baseline. Also demonstrates cross-architecture transfer.
AI.conclusion: BoostLoRA is the first PEFT method whose effective rank grows with training, separating per-round parameter cost from total representational capacity while maintaining zero inference overhead after merging.

title: CoAX: Cognitive-Oriented Attribution eXplanation User Model of Human Understanding of AI Explanations
summary: Explainable AI (XAI) aims to improve user understanding and decisions when using AI models. However, despite innovations in XAI, recent user evaluations reveal that this goal remains elusive. Understanding human cognition can help explain why users struggle to effectively use AI explanations. Focusing on reasoning on structured (tabular) data, we examined various reasoning strategies for different XAI methods (none, feature importance, feature attribution) in the decision task of anticipating AI decisions (i.e., forward simulation). We i) elicited reasoning strategies from a formative user study, and ii) collected decisions from a summative user study. Using cognitive modeling, we implemented the processes underlying each reasoning strategy and evaluated their alignment with human decision-making. We found that our models better fit human decisions than baseline machine learning proxies, providing insights into which reasoning strategies are (in)effective. We then demonstrate how the fitted model can be used to form hypotheses and investigate research questions that are costly to study with real human participants. This work contributes to debugging human understanding of XAI, informing the future development of more usable and interpretable AI explanations.

AI.tldr: Researchers use cognitive modeling to understand how people reason with AI explanations, finding that different XAI methods lead to different reasoning strategies with varying effectiveness.
AI.motivation: Despite XAI innovations, users still struggle to understand AI models effectively. Understanding human cognition can help identify why XAI explanations fail to improve user decisions.
AI.method: Examined reasoning strategies for different XAI methods (none, feature importance, feature attribution) in forward simulation tasks. Used formative user study to elicit strategies and summative study to collect decisions, then implemented cognitive models to evaluate alignment with human decision-making.
AI.result: Cognitive models better fit human decisions than baseline ML proxies, revealing which reasoning strategies are effective/ineffective. Models can be used to form hypotheses and investigate research questions that are costly to study with human participants.
AI.conclusion: This work contributes to debugging human understanding of XAI, informing future development of more usable and interpretable AI explanations by bridging cognitive science and XAI evaluation.

title: PINN-Cast: Exploring the Role of Continuous-Depth NODE in Transformers and Physics Informed Loss as Soft Physical Constraints in Short-term Weather Forecasting
comment: 14 pages, 4 Figures, Accepted in 26th International Conference on Computational Science (ICCS 2026)
summary: Operational weather prediction has long relied on physics-based numerical weather prediction (NWP), whose accuracy comes at the cost of substantial compute and complex simulation workflows. Recent transformer-based forecasters offer efficient data-driven alternatives, however transformers are physics-agnostic models. Additionally, standard transformer encoders evolve representations through discrete layer updates that may be less suited to modeling smooth latent dynamics. In this work, we propose a continuous-depth transformer encoder for weather forecasting that integrates Neural Ordinary Differential Equation (Neural ODE) dynamics within each encoder block. Specifically, we replace discrete residual updates with ODE-based updates solved using adaptive numerical integration. We also introduce a two-branch attention module that combines conventional patch-wise self-attention with an auxiliary branch that applies a derivative operator to attention logits, providing an additional change-sensitive interaction signal. To further align forecasts with governing principles, we propose a customized physics-informed training objective that enforces physical consistency as a soft constraint. We evaluate the proposed method against a standard discrete transformer baseline and an existing continuous-time Neural ODE forecasting variant, demonstrating the importance of PINN-Cast in short term weather forecasting.

AI.tldr: A continuous-depth transformer encoder with Neural ODE dynamics and physics-informed training improves short-term weather forecasting by better modeling smooth latent dynamics and enforcing physical consistency.
AI.motivation: Physics-based numerical weather prediction (NWP) is accurate but computationally expensive and complex. Transformer-based forecasters offer efficient data-driven alternatives but are physics-agnostic and may not effectively model smooth latent dynamics due to discrete layer updates.
AI.method: The method replaces discrete residual updates in transformer encoder blocks with ODE-based updates solved via adaptive numerical integration, introduces a two-branch attention module combining patch-wise self-attention with a derivative-based auxiliary branch, and incorporates a physics-informed training objective to enforce physical consistency as a soft constraint.
AI.result: The proposed method outperforms a standard discrete transformer baseline and an existing continuous-time Neural ODE forecasting variant, demonstrating the importance of PINN-Cast in short-term weather forecasting.
AI.conclusion: The proposed continuous-depth transformer encoder with Neural ODE dynamics, two-branch attention, and physics-informed training objective (PINN-Cast) improves short-term weather forecasting by better capturing smooth latent dynamics and enforcing physical consistency, offering a promising data-driven alternative to traditional NWP.

title: Safe Bilevel Delegation (SBD): A Formal Framework for Runtime Delegation Safety in Multi-Agent Systems
summary:
As large language model (LLM) agents are deployed in high-stakes environments, the question of how safely to delegate subtasks to specialized sub-agents becomes critical. Existing work addresses multi-agent architecture selection at design time or provides broad empirical guidelines, but neither provides a runtime mechanism that dynamically adjusts the safety-efficiency trade-off as task context changes during execution.
  We propose Safe Bilevel Delegation (SBD), a formal framework for runtime delegation safety in hierarchical multi-agent systems. SBD formulates task delegation as a bilevel optimization problem: an outer meta-weight network phi learns context-dependent safety-efficiency weights lambda(s) in [0,1]; an inner loop optimizes the delegation policy pi subject to a probabilistic safety constraint P(safe) >= 1-delta. The continuous delegation degree alpha in [0, 1] controls how much decision authority is transferred to each sub-agent, interpolating smoothly between full human override (alpha=0) and fully autonomous execution (alpha=1).
  We establish three theoretical results: (1) Safety Monotonicity--higher outer safety weight produces a weakly safer inner policy; (2) Inner Policy Convergence--projected gradient descent on the inner problem converges linearly under standard smoothness assumptions; (3) an Accountability Propagation bound that distributes responsibility across multi-hop delegation chains with a provable per-agent ceiling. We instantiate SBD in three high-stakes domains--medical AI (MIMIC-III), financial risk control (S and P 500), and educational agent supervision (ASSISTments)--specifying datasets, safety constraint sets, baselines, and evaluation protocols. This manuscript presents the formal framework and theoretical results in full; empirical validation following the protocols described herein is planned and will be reported in a forthcoming revision.

AI.tldr: Safe Bilevel Delegation (SBD) enables runtime safety in multi-agent delegation via bilevel optimization with continuous authority control and theoretical guarantees for high-stakes applications.
AI.motivation: LLM agents deployed in high-stakes environments need safe delegation mechanisms; existing approaches don't provide runtime mechanisms to dynamically adjust safety-efficiency trade-offs as task context changes during execution.
AI.method: SBD formulates task delegation as bilevel optimization: outer meta-weight network learns context-dependent safety-efficiency weights; inner loop optimizes delegation policy subject to probabilistic safety constraint with continuous delegation degree controlling authority transfer.
AI.result: Three theoretical results: Safety Monotonicity, Inner Policy Convergence, and Accountability Propagation bound with provable per-agent ceiling across multi-hop delegation chains.
AI.conclusion: SBD provides a formal framework for runtime delegation safety in hierarchical multi-agent systems with theoretical guarantees and planned empirical validation in high-stakes domains.

title: A Short Note on Batch-efficient Divide-and-Conquer Algorithm for EigenDecomposition
summary: EigenDecomposition (ED) is at the heart of many computer vision algorithms and applications. One crucial bottleneck limiting its usage is the expensive computation cost, particularly for a mini-batch of matrices in deep neural networks. Our previous work proposed a dedicated QR-based ED algorithm for batched small matrices (dim${<}32$). This short paper targets the limitation and proposes a batch-efficient Divide-and-Conquer based ED algorithm for larger matrices. The numerical test shows that for a mini-batch of matrices whose dimensions are smaller than $64$, our method can be much faster than the Pytorch SVD function.

AI.tldr: A batch-efficient Divide-and-Conquer EigenDecomposition algorithm for larger matrices (up to dim<64) that outperforms PyTorch SVD in speed.
AI.motivation: Address the computational bottleneck of EigenDecomposition for mini-batches of matrices in deep neural networks, particularly extending beyond the previous limitation of handling only small matrices (dim<32).
AI.method: Divide-and-Conquer based EigenDecomposition algorithm
AI.result: The proposed method is much faster than the PyTorch SVD function for mini-batches of matrices with dimensions smaller than 64, providing a batch-efficient solution for larger matrices than previously possible.
AI.conclusion: This paper presents a practical solution for accelerating EigenDecomposition in deep learning applications by extending previous work to handle larger matrix batches, with the proposed Divide-and-Conquer algorithm offering significant speed improvements over existing methods for matrices smaller than 64 dimensions.

title: TIO-SHACL: Comprehensive SHACL validation for TMF Intent Ontologies
summary: Intent-based networking promises to revolutionize telecommunications network management by enabling operators to specify high-level goals rather than low-level configurations. The TM Forum Intent Ontology (tio) provides a standardized vocabulary for expressing network intents, yet lacks formal validation mechanisms to ensure intent correctness before its admission. We present tio-shacl, the first comprehensive SHACL (Shapes Constraint Language) validation framework for the TMF Intent Ontology. Our contribution includes 56 node shapes and 69 property shapes across all 15 tio v3.6.0 ontology modules, a reusable constraint library with 25 parameterized SPARQL-based constraint components, and novel validation patterns for recursive logical operators, quantity-based constraints, and cross-expectation relationships. We pursued 100% vocabulary coverage (87 classes, 109 properties, 72 functions), cross-implementation compatibility across three major SHACL engines, and validation accuracy on a corpus of 133 test cases. tio-shacl is publicly available under MIT license at https://github.com/EricssonResearch/tio-shacl and enables automated syntactic and semantic validation of network intents, addressing a critical gap in the field.

AI.tldr: First comprehensive SHACL validation framework for TM Forum Intent Ontology enabling automated validation of network intents with full coverage and high accuracy.
AI.motivation: Intent-based networking lacks formal validation mechanisms to ensure correctness before intent admission, despite the existence of standardized vocabularies like the TM Forum Intent Ontology.
AI.method: Developed a SHACL validation framework with 56 node shapes and 69 property shapes across all 15 ontology modules, a reusable constraint library of 25 parameterized SPARQL-based constraint components, and novel validation patterns for recursive logical operators, quantity-based constraints, and cross-expectation relationships.
AI.result: Achieved 100% vocabulary coverage (87 classes, 109 properties, 72 functions), cross-implementation compatibility across three major SHACL engines, and validation accuracy on 133 test cases.
AI.conclusion: tio-shacl successfully addresses the critical gap in intent-based networking by providing a comprehensive, standardized validation framework for the TM Forum Intent Ontology, enabling automated syntactic and semantic validation of network intents with full vocabulary coverage, cross-engine compatibility, and high accuracy.

title: TypeBandit: Type-Level Context Allocation and Reweighting for Effective Attribute Completion in Heterogeneous Graph Neural Networks
summary:
Heterogeneous graphs are widely used to model multi-relational systems, but missing node attributes remain a major bottleneck for downstream learning. In this paper, we identify and formalize type-dependent information asymmetry: the phenomenon that different node types provide substantially different levels of useful signal for attribute completion. Motivated by this observation, we propose TypeBandit, a lightweight, model-agnostic methodology for heterogeneous attribute completion. TypeBandit combines topology-aware initialization, type-level bandit sampling, and joint representation learning. It allocates a finite global sampling budget across node types, samples representative nodes within each type, and uses the resulting sampled type summaries as shared contextual signals during representation construction. By operating at the type level rather than over each target node's local neighborhood, TypeBandit keeps the adaptive state compact and practical for large heterogeneous graphs.
  A key advantage of TypeBandit is architectural flexibility. Rather than requiring a new heterogeneous graph neural network architecture, TypeBandit acts as a type-aware front end for representative heterogeneous GNN backbones, including R-GCN, HetGNN, HGT, and SimpleHGN. We further introduce a hybrid pretraining scheme that combines structural degree priors with feature propagation, yielding a more reliable initializer than degree-only pretraining. Under a fixed-split protocol on DBLP, IMDB, and ACM, TypeBandit provides dataset-dependent but practically meaningful gains. Additional ablation, stability, efficiency, semantic-propagation, and sampled OGBN-MAG experiments support TypeBandit as a practical strategy for heterogeneous attribute completion when type-specific information is unevenly distributed and sampling resources are limited.

AI.tldr: TypeBandit is a lightweight, model-agnostic method for heterogeneous attribute completion that uses type-level bandit sampling to allocate limited sampling budgets across node types with uneven information distribution.
AI.motivation: Heterogeneous graphs often have missing node attributes that hinder downstream learning, and there exists type-dependent information asymmetry where different node types provide substantially different levels of useful signal for attribute completion.
AI.method: TypeBandit combines topology-aware initialization, type-level bandit sampling, and joint representation learning. It allocates a finite global sampling budget across node types, samples representative nodes within each type, and uses these sampled type summaries as shared contextual signals during representation construction.
AI.result: TypeBandit achieves dataset-dependent but practically meaningful gains under fixed-split protocols on DBLP, IMDB, and ACM datasets. It demonstrates architectural flexibility as a front-end for various heterogeneous GNN backbones and shows efficiency in ablation, stability, and semantic-propagation experiments.
AI.conclusion: TypeBandit is a practical strategy for heterogeneous attribute completion when type-specific information is unevenly distributed and sampling resources are limited, offering a lightweight, model-agnostic solution that maintains architectural flexibility with existing heterogeneous GNN backbones.

title: Measurement Risk in Supervised Financial NLP: Rubric and Metric Sensitivity on JF-ICR
comment: 16 Pages, Submitted to IEEE Computational Intelligence in Financial Engineering and Economics (CIFEr) 2026, Tokyo, JP
summary: As LLMs become credible readers of earnings calls, investor-relations Q\&A, guidance, and disclosure language, supervised financial NLP benchmarks increasingly function as decision evidence for model selection and deployment. A hidden assumption is that gold labels make such evidence objective. This assumption breaks down when the benchmark ruler itself is sensitive to rubric wording, metric choice, or aggregation policy. We study this measurement risk on Japanese Financial Implicit-Commitment Recognition (JF-ICR; a pinned 253-item test split x 4 frontier LLMs x 5 rubrics x 3 temperatures x 5 ordinal metrics). Three findings follow. First, rubric wording materially changes model-assigned labels: R2--R3 agreement ranges from 70.0% to 83.4%, with the dominant movement near the +1 / 0 implicit-commitment boundary. This pattern is consistent with a pragmatic-boundary interpretation, but is not a validated linguistic-causality claim because the present rubric variants confound semantics, examples, and verbosity. Second, not every metric remains informative under the JF-ICR class distribution. Within-one accuracy is too easy because near misses receive credit and the majority class dominates; worst-class accuracy is too noisy because the rarest class has only two examples. Exact accuracy, macro-F1, and weighted \k{appa} are therefore the identifiable metrics under our operational rule. Third, ranking claims become more defensible only after this metric-identifiability audit: Bradley--Terry, Borda, and Ranked Pairs agree on the identifiable metric subset, while the full five-metric sweep produces disagreement on the closest pair. The contribution is not a new leaderboard, but a reporting discipline for supervised financial benchmarks whose gold labels exist and whose evaluation ruler still requires governance.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques the hidden assumption that gold labels make financial NLP benchmarks objective, showing that benchmark results can be sensitive to rubric wording, metric choice, and aggregation policies.
AI.motivation: As LLMs become credible readers of financial texts, supervised NLP benchmarks increasingly serve as decision evidence for model selection. However, there's a hidden assumption that gold labels make such evidence objective, which breaks down when benchmarks are sensitive to rubric wording, metric choice, or aggregation policy.
AI.method: The authors study measurement risk using the Japanese Financial Implicit-Commitment Recognition (JF-ICR) benchmark: a pinned 253-item test split x 4 frontier LLMs x 5 rubrics x 3 temperatures x 5 ordinal metrics.
AI.result: Three findings: 1) Rubric wording materially changes model-assigned labels (R2-R3 agreement ranges 70.0% to 83.4%); 2) Not every metric remains informative under JF-ICR class distribution - exact accuracy, macro-F1, and weighted κ are identifiable metrics; 3) Ranking claims become more defensible after metric-identifiability audit - Bradley-Terry, Borda, and Ranked Pairs agree on identifiable metric subset.
AI.conclusion: The contribution is not a new leaderboard, but a reporting discipline for supervised financial benchmarks whose gold labels exist and whose evaluation ruler still requires governance - emphasizing that objectivity requires auditing rubric sensitivity and metric identifiability.

title: AG-TAL: Anatomically-Guided Topology-Aware Loss for Multiclass Segmentation of the Circle of Willis Using Large-Scale Multi-Center Datasets
comment: 11 pages, 5 figures, submitted to IEEE JBHI
summary: Accurate multiclass segmentation of the Circle of Willis (CoW) is essential for neurovascular disease management but remains challenging due to complex vascular topology and variable morphology. Existing deep learning methods often suffer from vascular discontinuities and inter-class misclassification, while current topological loss functions incur prohibitive computational costs in 3D multiclass settings. To address these limitations, we propose an Anatomically-Guided Topology-Aware Loss (AG-TAL) and introduce a large-scale, multi-center CoW dataset with unified annotations to facilitate robust model training. AG-TAL specifically integrates a radius-aware Dice loss to address class imbalance in small vessels, a breakage-aware clDice loss that utilizes group convolutions to efficiently preserve local connectivity, and an adjacency-aware co-occurrence loss that leverages anatomical priors to enforce distinct boundaries between neighboring arteries. Evaluated using 5-fold cross-validation, AG-TAL achieved an average Dice score of 80.85% for all CoW arteries, with small arteries notably higher by 1.05-3.09% compared to state-of-the-art methods. Across six independent datasets, the performance of AG-TAL achieved Dice scores ranging from 74.46% to 81.17% for all CoW arteries, with improvements of 2.20% to 9.98% for small arteries compared to other methods. This study demonstrates the superiority of AG-TAL in identifying multiclass CoW arteries and its ability to generalize well to multiple independent datasets. Furthermore, reliability analyses and clinical applications in an Alzheimer's disease cohort validate the AG-TAL's robustness and its potential for discovering imaging-based morphological biomarkers.

AI.tldr: Proposed AG-TAL (Anatomically-Guided Topology-Aware Loss) for multiclass Circle of Willis segmentation improves small vessel segmentation and preserves vascular continuity while reducing computational costs.
AI.motivation: Accurate multiclass segmentation of Circle of Willis is challenging due to complex vascular topology and variable morphology. Existing methods suffer from vascular discontinuities, inter-class misclassification, and computationally expensive topological loss functions in 3D multiclass settings.
AI.method: AG-TAL integrates three components: 1) radius-aware Dice loss to address class imbalance in small vessels, 2) breakage-aware clDice loss using group convolutions to efficiently preserve local connectivity, and 3) adjacency-aware co-occurrence loss leveraging anatomical priors to enforce distinct boundaries between neighboring arteries.
AI.result: AG-TAL achieved average Dice score of 80.85% for all CoW arteries in 5-fold cross-validation, with small arteries showing 1.05-3.09% improvement over SOTA. On six independent datasets, performance ranged from 74.46% to 81.17% for all arteries, with 2.20% to 9.98% improvement for small vessels compared to other methods.
AI.conclusion: AG-TAL demonstrates superiority in multiclass CoW artery identification with good generalization across datasets. Reliability analyses and Alzheimer's disease cohort applications validate its robustness and potential for discovering imaging-based morphological biomarkers.

title: Robust Learning on Heterogeneous Graphs with Heterophily: A Graph Structure Learning Approach
summary: Heterogeneous graphs with heterophily have emerged as a powerful abstraction for modeling complex real-world systems, where nodes of different types and labels interact in diverse and often non-homophilous ways. Despite recent advances, robust representation learning for such graphs remains largely unexplored, particularly in the presence of noisy or misleading connectivity. In this work, we investigate this problem and identify structural noise as a critical challenge that significantly degrades model performance. To address this issue, we propose a unified framework, Heterogeneous Graph Unified Learning (HGUL), which jointly handles heterophily and noisy graph structures. The framework consists of three complementary modules: a kNN-based graph construction module that recovers reliable local neighborhoods, a graph structure learning module that adaptively refines the adjacency by filtering noisy edges, and a heterogeneous affinity learning module that captures class-level relationships via an extended affinity matrix derived from a polynomial graph kernel. Extensive experiments on multiple datasets demonstrate that HGUL consistently outperforms existing methods on clean graphs and maintains strong robustness under varying levels of structural noise. The results further underscore the importance of jointly modeling heterophily and noise in heterogeneous graph learning.

AI.tldr: HGUL is a unified framework for robust representation learning on heterogeneous graphs with heterophily, addressing structural noise through kNN-based graph construction, adaptive edge filtering, and heterogeneous affinity learning with polynomial graph kernels.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for heterogeneous graph representation learning largely overlook the challenge of structural noise and noisy connectivity, which significantly degrades model performance. Heterogeneous graphs with heterophily (non-homophilous relationships) present unique challenges for robust representation learning that current approaches don't adequately address.
AI.method: HGUL consists of three complementary modules: 1) A kNN-based graph construction module that recovers reliable local neighborhoods, 2) A graph structure learning module that adaptively refines the adjacency matrix by filtering noisy edges, and 3) A heterogeneous affinity learning module that captures class-level relationships using an extended affinity matrix derived from a polynomial graph kernel.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on multiple datasets show that HGUL consistently outperforms existing methods on clean graphs and maintains strong robustness under varying levels of structural noise. The framework demonstrates improved performance and resilience to noisy graph structures compared to baseline approaches.
AI.conclusion: The results highlight the importance of jointly modeling heterophily and structural noise in heterogeneous graph learning. HGUL provides an effective unified approach for robust representation learning on heterophily graphs with noisy connectivity, advancing the field of graph machine learning for complex real-world systems.

title: Stable but Wrong: An Inference Limit in Galactic Archaeology
summary: Statistical inference in observational science typically relies on a fundamental assumption: as sample size increases and uncertainties decrease, the inferred results should converge to the true physical quantities. This assumption underpins the notion that big data lead to more reliable conclusions. In Galactic archaeology, stellar ages inferred from spectroscopic surveys are widely used to reconstruct the formation history of the Milky Way disk. The age metallicity relation (AMR) and its derived formation timescale are often regarded as key physical diagnostics of early disk evolution. This interpretation carries an implicit premise: that observational quality does not introduce systematic bias into age inference. Here we show that this premise may fail. Using a large sample of subgiant stars, we identify a region in the observational quality parameter space (signal-to-noise ratio and parallax precision) where the inferred formation timescale exhibits a systematic offset of 0.5-1 Gyr relative to an independent asteroseismic reference, while the statistical uncertainties remain small, thus producing a stable-but-wrong inference state.

AI.tldr: Astronomers identify systematic bias in stellar age inference from spectroscopic surveys that produces stable-but-wrong results in Galactic archaeology
AI.motivation: The paper challenges the fundamental assumption in observational science that bigger data with smaller uncertainties necessarily leads to more reliable results converging to true physical quantities, specifically in Galactic archaeology where stellar ages are used to reconstruct Milky Way disk formation history.
AI.method: Researchers used a large sample of subgiant stars and identified specific regions in observational quality parameter space (signal-to-noise ratio and parallax precision) where systematic bias occurs, comparing spectroscopic age inferences against independent asteroseismic reference ages.
AI.result: The study found that in certain observational quality conditions, inferred formation timescales show systematic offsets of 0.5-1 Gyr relative to asteroseismic reference, while statistical uncertainties remain small, creating a "stable-but-wrong" inference state where results appear reliable but are systematically biased.
AI.conclusion: The assumption that observational quality doesn't introduce systematic bias in stellar age inference may fail, challenging the reliability of using age-metallicity relations and formation timescales from spectroscopic surveys for reconstructing Milky Way disk evolution without careful consideration of observational quality biases.

title: Leading Across the Spectrum of Human-AI Relationships: A Conceptual Framework for Increasingly Heterogeneous Teams
summary:
What shapes a consequential decision when human and artificial intelligence work on it together? The answer is becoming harder to see. A decision may look human-led after AI has set the frame, or appear automated while human judgment still carries decisive force. This paper offers a leadership-facing spectrum to see those relationships within a bounded mandate: Pure Human, Centaur (human-dominant, with AI in the loop), Co-equal, Minotaur (AI-dominant, with humans in the loop), and Pure AI.
  The spectrum asks where leadership work occurs: who frames the problem, who redirects the work, and who can answer for what follows. The five positions are landmarks that help leaders recognize configurations as they layer, drift, or change in a single decision. The central risk is misrecognition: leaders may keep a human-centered story in place after decision-shaping authority has shifted elsewhere. They may believe oversight remains meaningful when it has become ceremonial, or keep humans in the loop when their involvement could make the decision worse. The framework introduces co-adaptability, the capacity of a configuration to improve as human and non-human participants adjust together, and places it within heterogeneous teaming, where participants may vary by number, substrate, model architecture, capability, speed, memory, and form of participation.
  The aim is practical: to help strategic leaders and those designing or deploying AI systems recognize the configuration at work, notice when it shifts, and judge whether it fits the decision before them. These configurations will shape how power, responsibility, and trust are distributed in organizational life. Whether the futures they help create remain governable and worth inhabiting will depend on leaders who can see, early enough, where and how consequential decisions are actually being shaped.

AI.tldr: A leadership spectrum framework for analyzing human-AI decision-making relationships across five configurations, from Pure Human to Pure AI, focusing on where decision-shaping authority actually resides.
AI.motivation: As human and AI collaboration becomes more complex in consequential decisions, it's increasingly difficult to discern who actually shapes decisions - humans may appear to lead after AI sets the frame, or decisions may seem automated while human judgment remains decisive. This opacity creates risks of misrecognizing where decision-shaping authority actually resides.
AI.method: Develops a leadership-facing spectrum with five positions: Pure Human, Centaur (human-dominant, with AI in the loop), Co-equal, Minotaur (AI-dominant, with humans in the loop), and Pure AI. The framework examines three dimensions: who frames the problem, who redirects the work, and who can answer for outcomes. Introduces concepts of co-adaptability (improvement through mutual adjustment) and heterogeneous teaming (varied participants by number, substrate, architecture, etc.).
AI.result: Creates a practical framework that helps leaders recognize configurations in human-AI decision-making, notice shifts, and assess fitness for specific decisions. Identifies central risk as misrecognition - maintaining human-centered stories when authority has shifted elsewhere, believing oversight remains meaningful when ceremonial, or keeping humans involved when their participation worsens decisions.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables strategic leaders and AI designers to better understand how power, responsibility, and trust distribute in organizational life. Recognizing configurations early helps ensure future AI-human collaboration remains governable and worth inhabiting by understanding where and how consequential decisions are actually shaped.

title: Detecting is Easy, Adapting is Hard: Local Expert Growth for Visual Model-Based Reinforcement Learning under Distribution Shift
summary: Visual model-based reinforcement learning (MBRL) agents can perform well on the training distribution, but often break down once the test environment shifts. In visual MBRL, recognizing that a shift has occurred is often the easier part; the harder part is turning that recognition into useful action-level correction. We study several ways of responding to shift, including planning penalties, direct fine-tuning, global residual correction, and coarse gating. In our experiments, these approaches either do not improve closed-loop control or hurt in-distribution (ID) performance. Based on these negative results, we propose JEPA-Indexed Local Expert Growth. The method uses a frozen JEPA representation only for problem indexing, while cluster-specific residual experts add local action corrections on top of the original controller. The baseline controller itself is not modified. Using paired-bootstrap evaluation, we find that the original naive-preference variant is not stable under stricter testing. In contrast, the harder-pair variant produces statistically significant OOD improvements on all four evaluated shift conditions while preserving ID performance. The learned experts also remain useful when the same shift is encountered again, which supports the view of adaptation as incremental knowledge growth rather than repeated full retraining. We further show that automatic ID rejection can be achieved with simple density models, whereas fine-grained discrimination among OOD sub-families is limited by the representation. Overall, the results indicate that, for visual MBRL under distribution shift, the main challenge is not simply noticing that the environment has changed, but applying the right local action correction after the change has been recognized.

AI.tldr: For visual MBRL under distribution shift, proposing JEPA-Indexed Local Expert Growth achieves OOD improvements without harming ID performance, showing adaptation can be incremental knowledge growth.
AI.motivation: Visual MBRL agents perform well on training distribution but break down with test environment shifts; recognizing shift is easier than turning recognition into useful action-level correction
AI.method: Study of planning penalties, direct fine-tuning, global residual correction, coarse gating; then propose JEPA-Indexed Local Expert Growth - uses frozen JEPA representation only for problem indexing, with cluster-specific residual experts adding local action corrections on top of original controller
AI.result: Initial approaches (planning penalties, fine-tuning, global residual, gating) either don't improve closed-loop control or hurt in-distribution performance. JEPA-Indexed Local Expert Growth (harder-pair variant) produces statistically significant OOD improvements on all four shift conditions while preserving ID performance. Learned experts remain useful when same shift encountered again. Automatic ID rejection achievable with simple density models, but fine-grained OOD discrimination limited by representation.
AI.conclusion: For visual MBRL under distribution shift, the main challenge is not simply noticing that shift has occurred, but applying the right local action correction after recognition. JEPA-Indexed Local Expert Growth (harder-pair variant) achieves OOD improvements while preserving ID performance, supporting adaptation as incremental knowledge growth rather than repeated full retraining.

title: InteractWeb-Bench: Can Multimodal Agent Escape Blind Execution in Interactive Website Generation?
summary: With the advancement of multimodal large language models (MLLMs) and coding agents, the website development has shifted from manual programming to agent-based project-level code synthesis. Existing benchmarks rely on idealized assumptions, especially for well-structured, information-rich inputs and static execution settings. In contrast, real-world development is constrained by a critical bottleneck: the semantic misalignment between ambiguous, low-quality instructions from non-expert users and model understanding, which results in a failure mode that we term blind execution. To address this gap, we introduce InteractWeb-Bench, the first multimodal interactive benchmark for website generation under non-expert low-code user conditions. InteractWeb-Bench introduces four types of user agents and persona-driven instruction perturbations to systematically simulate diverse user behaviors, including ambiguity, redundancy, and contradiction, grounded in requirement engineering defect taxonomies. We develop an interactive execution environment for agents, featuring a unified action space comprising Clarify, Implement, Verify, and Submit, enabling iterative intent refinement, code synthesis, and visual feedback-based validation. Extensive experiments and analysis reveal that frontier MLLM-based agents remain trapped in blind execution, exposing limitations in intent recognition and adaptive interaction.

AI.tldr: InteractWeb-Bench is the first multimodal interactive benchmark for website generation under non-expert low-code user conditions, designed to address the blind execution problem where models fail to handle ambiguous user instructions.
AI.motivation: Real-world website development faces a critical bottleneck: semantic misalignment between ambiguous, low-quality instructions from non-expert users and model understanding, resulting in "blind execution." Existing benchmarks rely on idealized assumptions about well-structured inputs and static execution, failing to capture real-world development constraints.
AI.method: Introduces InteractWeb-Bench with four types of user agents and persona-driven instruction perturbations to systematically simulate diverse user behaviors (ambiguity, redundancy, contradiction) grounded in requirement engineering defect taxonomies. Also develops an interactive execution environment with unified action space (Clarify, Implement, Verify, Submit) for iterative intent refinement and visual feedback-based validation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments reveal that frontier multimodal large language model (MLLM)-based agents remain trapped in blind execution, exposing limitations in intent recognition and adaptive interaction when dealing with non-expert user instructions.
AI.conclusion: There's a significant gap between current MLLM capabilities and real-world website development needs, highlighting the need for benchmarks like InteractWeb-Bench that better simulate non-expert user interactions and expose limitations in agents' ability to handle ambiguous instructions through adaptive interaction.

title: ChipLingo: A Systematic Training Framework for Large Language Models in EDA
summary:
With the rapid advancement of semiconductor technology, Electronic Design Automation (EDA) has become an increasingly knowledge-intensive and document-driven engineering domain. Although large language models (LLMs) have shown strong general capabilities, applying them directly to EDA remains challenging due to limited domain expertise, cross-tool knowledge confusion, and degraded retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) performance after domain training. To address these issues, this paper presents ChipLingo, a systematic training pipeline for domain-adapted LLMs tailored to EDA scenarios.
  ChipLingo consists of three stages: domain corpus construction with multi-source data curation and QA augmentation, domain-adaptive pretraining with comparisons of different parameter training strategies, and instruction alignment with RAG scenario training under diverse retrieval conditions. We also curate an internal benchmark, EDA-Bench, covering representative EDA tool scenarios, with plans for public release.
  Experiments show that ChipLingo-8B achieves 59.7% accuracy on EDA-Bench, outperforming the same-scale base model and some larger general-purpose models. ChipLingo-32B reaches 70.02%, approaching leading closed-source commercial models. Further analysis shows that QA augmentation improves domain performance, Partial FT offers a better balance between adaptation and general capability retention than LoRA, and explicit RAG scenario training mitigates the decline in retrieval utilization after domain training. These results demonstrate the practical value of systematic domain training for knowledge-intensive EDA tasks and provide a foundation for future EDA agents and external-knowledge-driven systems.

AI.tldr: ChipLingo is a systematic training pipeline for domain-adapted LLMs for Electronic Design Automation (EDA) that addresses challenges like limited domain expertise and degraded RAG performance.
AI.motivation: EDA has become knowledge-intensive and document-driven, but applying LLMs directly is challenging due to limited domain expertise, cross-tool knowledge confusion, and degraded RAG performance after domain training.
AI.method: Three-stage pipeline: (1) domain corpus construction with multi-source data curation and QA augmentation, (2) domain-adaptive pretraining comparing parameter training strategies, (3) instruction alignment with RAG scenario training under diverse retrieval conditions.
AI.result: ChipLingo-8B achieves 59.7% accuracy on EDA-Bench, outperforming same-scale base models and some larger general-purpose models. ChipLingo-32B reaches 70.02%, approaching leading closed-source commercial models. QA augmentation improves domain performance, Partial FT offers better balance than LoRA, and explicit RAG training mitigates retrieval decline.
AI.conclusion: Systematic domain training provides practical value for knowledge-intensive EDA tasks and provides foundation for future EDA agents and external-knowledge-driven systems.

title: PRTS: A Primitive Reasoning and Tasking System via Contrastive Representations
summary: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models advance robotic control via strong visual-linguistic priors. However, existing VLAs predominantly frame pretraining as supervised behavior cloning, overlooking the fundamental nature of robot learning as a goal-reaching process that requires understanding temporal task progress. We present \textbf{PRTS} (\textbf{P}rimitive \textbf{R}easoning and \textbf{T}asking \textbf{S}ystem), a VLA foundation model that reformulates pretraining through Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning. By treating language instructions as goals and employing contrastive reinforcement learning, PRTS learns a unified embedding space where the inner product of state-action and goal embeddings approximates the log-discounted goal occupancy, the probability of reaching the language-specified goal from the current state-action, quantitatively assessing physical feasibility beyond static semantic matching. PRTS draws this dense goal-reachability supervision directly from offline trajectories without reward annotations, and folds it into the VLM backbone via a role-aware causal mask, incurring negligible overhead over vanilla behavior cloning. This paradigm endows the high-level reasoning system with intrinsic goal reachability awareness, bridging semantic reasoning and temporal task progress, and further benefits goal-conditioned action prediction. Pretrained on 167B tokens of diverse manipulation and embodied-reasoning data, PRTS reaches state-of-the-art performance on LIBERO, LIBERO-Pro, LIBERO-Plus, SimplerEnv, and a real-world suite of 14 complex tasks, with particularly substantial gains on long-horizon, contact-rich, and zero-shot novel-instruction settings, confirming that injecting goal-reachability awareness significantly improves both execution success and long-horizon planning of general-purpose robotic foundation policies.

AI.tldr: PRTS is a Vision-Language-Action foundation model that uses Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning instead of behavior cloning, learning to assess goal reachability through contrastive learning on offline trajectories.
AI.motivation: Existing Vision-Language-Action models use supervised behavior cloning for pretraining, ignoring that robot learning is fundamentally about goal-reaching and understanding temporal task progress.
AI.method: PRTS reformulates pretraining through Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning, treating language instructions as goals and using contrastive reinforcement learning to learn a unified embedding space where state-action and goal embeddings approximate log-discounted goal occupancy probability. It uses a role-aware causal mask and draws supervision from offline trajectories without reward annotations.
AI.result: Pretrained on 167B tokens, PRTS achieves state-of-the-art performance on LIBERO, LIBERO-Pro, LIBERO-Plus, SimplerEnv, and 14 real-world complex tasks, with substantial gains on long-horizon, contact-rich, and zero-shot novel-instruction settings.
AI.conclusion: Injecting goal-reachability awareness significantly improves both execution success and long-horizon planning of general-purpose robotic foundation policies, bridging semantic reasoning and temporal task progress.

title: AdaBFL: Multi-Layer Defensive Adaptive Aggregation for Bzantine-Robust Federated Learning
summary: Federated learning (FL) is a popular distributed learning paradigm in machine learning, which enables multiple clients to collaboratively train models under the guidance of a server without exposing private client data. However, FL's decentralized nature makes it vulnerable to poisoning attacks, where malicious clients can submit corrupted models to manipulate the system. To counter such attacks, although various Byzantine-robust methods have been proposed, these methods struggle to provide balanced defense against multiple types of attacks or rely on possessing the dataset in the server. To deal with these drawbacks, thus, we propose an effective multi-layer defensive adaptive aggregation for Bzantine-robust federated learning (AdaBFL) based on a novel three-layer defensive mechanism, which can adaptively adjust the weights of defense algorithms to counter complex attacks. Moreover, we provide convergence properties of our AdaBFL method under the non-convex setting on non-iid data. Comprehensive experiments across multiple datasets validate the superiority of our AdaBFL over the comparable algorithms.

AI.tldr: AdaBFL is a three-layer defensive adaptive aggregation method for Byzantine-robust federated learning that dynamically adjusts defense weights to counter multiple attack types, providing balanced protection without requiring server data access.
AI.motivation: Federated learning's decentralized nature makes it vulnerable to poisoning attacks by malicious clients, and existing Byzantine-robust methods either struggle with balanced defense against multiple attack types or require the server to possess the dataset, creating limitations in real-world FL deployments.
AI.method: AdaBFL proposes a novel three-layer defensive adaptive aggregation mechanism that adaptively adjusts the weights of defense algorithms to counter complex Byzantine attacks. The method provides convergence guarantees for non-convex settings on non-IID data.
AI.result: Comprehensive experiments across multiple datasets validate that AdaBFL demonstrates superior performance compared to comparable algorithms, effectively defending against various Byzantine attacks while maintaining model performance.
AI.conclusion: AdaBFL provides an effective solution to Byzantine attacks in federated learning by offering balanced, adaptive defense that doesn't require server data access, making it suitable for real-world FL applications with non-IID data distributions.

title: Belief-Guided Inference Control for Large Language Model Services via Verifiable Observations
summary: In black-box large language model (LLM) services, response reliability is often only partially observable at decision time, while stronger inference pathways incur substantial computational cost, inducing a budgeted sequential decision problem: for each request, the system should decide whether the default low-cost response is sufficiently reliable or whether additional computation should be allocated to improve response quality. In this paper, we propose \textbf{Ver}ifiable \textbf{O}bservations for Risk-aware \textbf{I}nference \textbf{C}ontrol (\textsc{Veroic}), a framework for adaptive inference control in black-box LLM settings, which formulates request-time control as a \textit{partially observable Markov decision process} to capture partial observability and sequential budget coupling. It constructs a lightweight verifiable observation channel from the input-output pair by aggregating heterogeneous quality signals into a belief state over latent response reliability, which is then used by a budget-aware policy to decide whether to return the default output or trigger a higher-cost inference pathway. Experiments on diverse tasks show that \textsc{Veroic} achieves improved quality-cost trade-offs, stronger risk estimation and calibration, and more robust long-horizon inference control than competitive baselines.

AI.tldr: VEROIC is a framework for adaptive inference control in black-box LLMs that uses verifiable observations and POMDP-based decision-making to optimize quality-cost trade-offs.
AI.motivation: In black-box LLM services, response reliability is often partially observable at decision time, while stronger inference pathways incur substantial computational cost, creating a budgeted sequential decision problem where systems must decide whether to use default low-cost responses or allocate additional computation.
AI.method: Proposes VEROIC framework that formulates request-time control as a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP) to capture partial observability and sequential budget coupling. It constructs lightweight verifiable observation channels from input-output pairs by aggregating heterogeneous quality signals into a belief state over latent response reliability, which is used by a budget-aware policy to decide whether to return default output or trigger higher-cost inference.
AI.result: Experiments on diverse tasks show that VEROIC achieves improved quality-cost trade-offs, stronger risk estimation and calibration, and more robust long-horizon inference control compared to competitive baselines.
AI.conclusion: VEROIC provides an effective framework for adaptive inference control in black-box LLM settings by combining verifiable observations with POMDP-based decision-making, enabling better management of computational budgets while maintaining response quality.

title: ABC: Any-Subset Autoregression via Non-Markovian Diffusion Bridges in Continuous Time and Space
summary: Generating continuous-time, continuous-space stochastic processes (e.g., videos, weather forecasts) conditioned on partial observations (e.g., first and last frames) is a fundamental challenge. Existing approaches, (e.g., diffusion models), suffer from key limitations: (1) noise-to-data evolution fails to capture structural similarity between states close in physical time and has unstable integration in low-step regimes; (2) random noise injected is insensitive to the physical process's time elapsed, resulting in incorrect dynamics; (3) they overlook conditioning on arbitrary subsets of states (e.g., irregularly sampled timesteps, future observations). We propose ABC: Any-Subset Autoregressive Models via Non-Markovian Diffusion Bridges in Continuous Time and Space. Crucially, we model the process with one continual SDE whose time variable and intermediate states track the real time and process states. This has provable advantages: (1) the starting point for generating future states is the already-close previous state, rather than uninformative noise; (2) random noise injection scales with physical time elapsed, encouraging physically plausible dynamics with similar time-adjacent states. We derive SDE dynamics via changes-of-measure on path space, yielding another advantage: (3) path-dependent conditioning on arbitrary subsets of the state history and/or future. To learn these dynamics, we derive a path- and time-dependent extension of denoising score matching. Our experiments show ABC's superiority to competing methods on multiple domains, including video generation and weather forecasting.

AI.tldr: ABC is a novel method for generating continuous-time, continuous-space stochastic processes (like videos, weather) conditioned on arbitrary partial observations, overcoming key diffusion model limitations via a non-Markovian diffusion bridge SDE framework with provable advantages in stability, physical plausibility, and flexible conditioning.
AI.motivation: Existing diffusion models for generating continuous-time, continuous-space stochastic processes (e.g., videos, weather forecasts) conditioned on partial observations have limitations: noise-to-data evolution fails to capture structural similarity between time-adjacent states and has unstable integration; random noise injection is insensitive to physical time elapsed, leading to incorrect dynamics; and they cannot condition on arbitrary subsets of states (e.g., irregular timesteps, future observations).
AI.method: ABC models the stochastic process with a single continual SDE whose time variable and intermediate states align with real time and process states. It derives SDE dynamics via changes-of-measure on path space, enabling path-dependent conditioning on arbitrary subsets of state history/future, and uses a path- and time-dependent extension of denoising score matching for training.
AI.result: Experiments show ABC's superiority over competing methods across multiple domains, including video generation and weather forecasting.
AI.conclusion: ABC demonstrates significant improvements in generating continuous-time, continuous-space stochastic processes by addressing key limitations of existing diffusion models, offering a more stable, physically plausible, and flexible conditioning framework.

title: In-Context Examples Suppress Scientific Knowledge Recall in LLMs
summary: Scientific reasoning rarely stops at what is directly observable; it often requires uncovering hidden structure from data. From estimating reaction constants in chemistry to inferring demand elasticities in economics, this latent structure recovery is what distinguishes scientific reasoning from curve fitting. Large language models (LLMs) can often recall and apply relevant scientific formulas, but we show that this ability is surprisingly easy to suppress. We show that adding in-context examples makes models rely less on pretrained domain knowledge, even when those examples are generated by the very same formula. Rather than reinforcing knowledge-driven derivation, examples shift computation toward empirical pattern fitting. We document this knowledge displacement on 60 latent structure recovery tasks across five scientific domains, 6,000 trials, and four models. This displacement is consistent across domains, but its accuracy consequences depend on how the displaced strategy compares to the one that replaces it: the same shift can lower accuracy, leave it unchanged, or appear to improve it. In all cases, however, the model shifts away from knowledge-driven reasoning. For practitioners deploying LLMs on scientific tasks, the message is cautionary: in-context examples may displace, rather than reinforce, the knowledge they are intended to support.

AI.tldr: LLMs shift from knowledge-driven reasoning to empirical pattern fitting when given in-context examples, even when examples reinforce domain knowledge.
AI.motivation: Scientific reasoning requires uncovering hidden structure, but LLMs' ability to recall scientific formulas can be suppressed by in-context examples, potentially undermining knowledge-driven reasoning.
AI.method: Tested 60 latent structure recovery tasks across 5 scientific domains with 6,000 trials and 4 models, analyzing how in-context examples affect reasoning strategies.
AI.result: In-context examples make LLMs rely less on pretrained knowledge and shift computation toward empirical pattern fitting, regardless of whether examples are generated by the same formula. This knowledge displacement has varying accuracy consequences depending on which strategy replaces it.
AI.conclusion: Cautionary: in-context examples may displace rather than reinforce domain knowledge, shifting LLMs away from knowledge-driven scientific reasoning toward pattern fitting.

title: Improving Graph Few-shot Learning with Hyperbolic Space and Denoising Diffusion
summary: Graph few-shot learning, which focuses on effectively learning from only a small number of labeled nodes to quickly adapt to new tasks, has garnered significant research attention. Despite recent advances in graph few-shot learning that have demonstrated promising performance, existing methods still suffer from several key limitations. First, during the meta-training phase, these methods typically perform node representation learning in Euclidean space, which often fails to capture the inherently hierarchical structure existing in real-world graph data. Second, during the meta-testing phase, they usually fit an empirical target distribution derived from only a few support samples, even when this distribution significantly deviates from the true underlying distribution. To address these issues, we propose IMPRESS, a novel framework that IMproves graPh few-shot learning with hypeRbolic spacE and denoiSing diffuSion. Specifically, our model learns node representations in a hyperbolic space and enriches the support distribution through denoising diffusion mechanisms. Theoretically, IMPRESS achieves a tighter generalization bound. Empirically, IMPRESS consistently outperforms competitive baselines across multiple benchmark datasets.

AI.tldr: IMPREESS introduces hyperbolic space learning and denoising diffusion to address limitations in graph few-shot learning, achieving tighter generalization bounds and improved performance across benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing graph few-shot learning methods have key limitations: (1) they use Euclidean space for node representation learning which fails to capture hierarchical structures, and (2) they fit empirical target distributions from few support samples even when these deviate significantly from true distributions.
AI.method: IMPREESS learns node representations in hyperbolic space (to capture hierarchical structures) and enriches the support distribution through denoising diffusion mechanisms.
AI.result: Theoretically, IMPREESS achieves a tighter generalization bound. Empirically, it consistently outperforms competitive baselines across multiple benchmark datasets.
AI.conclusion: IMPREESS addresses key limitations in graph few-shot learning by combining hyperbolic space learning with denoising diffusion, providing both theoretical improvements and empirical performance gains.

title: SpatialGrammar: A Domain-Specific Language for LLM-Based 3D Indoor Scene Generation
summary: Automatically generating interactive 3D indoor scenes from natural language is crucial for virtual reality, gaming, and embodied AI. However, existing LLM-based approaches often suffer from spatial errors and collisions, in part because common scene representations-raw coordinates or verbose code-are difficult for models to reason about 3D spatial relationships and physical constraints. We propose SpatialGrammar, a domain-specific language that represents gravity-aligned indoor layouts as BEV grid placements with deterministic compilation to valid 3D geometry, enabling verifiable constraint checking. Building on this representation, we develop (1) SG-Agent, a closed-loop system that uses compiler feedback to iteratively refine scenes and enforce collision constraints, and (2) SG-Mini, a 104M-parameter model trained entirely on compiler-validated synthetic data. Across 159 test scenes spanning five scenarios of different complexity, SG-Agent improves spatial fidelity and physical plausibility over prior methods, while SG-Mini performs competitively against larger LLM-based baselines on single-shot generation scenarios.

AI.tldr: SpatialGrammar uses a domain-specific language for 3D indoor scene generation from natural language, improving spatial accuracy and reducing collisions compared to existing LLM approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based approaches for generating interactive 3D indoor scenes from natural language suffer from spatial errors and collisions because common scene representations (raw coordinates or verbose code) are difficult for models to reason about 3D spatial relationships and physical constraints.
AI.method: Proposes SpatialGrammar, a domain-specific language that represents gravity-aligned indoor layouts as BEV (bird's-eye view) grid placements with deterministic compilation to valid 3D geometry. Develops SG-Agent (a closed-loop system using compiler feedback to iteratively refine scenes) and SG-Mini (a 104M-parameter model trained on compiler-validated synthetic data).
AI.result: Across 159 test scenes spanning five scenarios of different complexity, SG-Agent improves spatial fidelity and physical plausibility over prior methods, while SG-Mini performs competitively against larger LLM-based baselines on single-shot generation scenarios.
AI.conclusion: SpatialGrammar provides an effective approach for automatic 3D indoor scene generation from natural language by using a domain-specific language that enables verifiable constraint checking and improves spatial reasoning compared to existing methods.

title: Toward Scalable SDN for LEO Mega-Constellations: A Graph Learning Approach
summary: Terrestrial network limitations drive the integration of non-terrestrial networks (NTNs), notably mega-constellations comprising thousands of low Earth orbit (LEO) satellites. While these satellites act as interconnected network switches via inter-satellite links (ISLs), their massive scale creates severe bottlenecks for network management. To address this, we propose a scalable, hierarchical software-defined networking (SDN) framework. Our architecture leverages graph neural networks (GNNs) to compactly represent the constellation topology, and Koopman theory to linearize nonlinear dynamics. Specifically, a Graph Koopman Autoencoder (GKAE) forecasts spatio-temporal behavior within a linear subspace for each orbital shell. A central SDN controller then aggregates these shell-level predictions for globally coordinated control. Simulations on the Starlink constellation demonstrate that our approach achieves at least a 42.8\% improvement in spatial compression and a 10.81\% improvement in temporal forecasting compared to established baselines, all while utilizing a significantly smaller model footprint.

AI.tldr: Proposed a hierarchical SDN framework using Graph Koopman Autoencoders for scalable management of massive satellite constellations, achieving better compression and forecasting with smaller models.
AI.motivation: Massive scale of LEO satellite mega-constellations creates severe bottlenecks for network management, while terrestrial networks have limitations that drive integration of non-terrestrial networks.
AI.method: Hierarchical SDN framework using Graph Neural Networks to represent constellation topology and Koopman theory to linearize nonlinear dynamics. Specifically uses Graph Koopman Autoencoder to forecast spatio-temporal behavior in linear subspaces for each orbital shell, aggregated by central controller.
AI.result: Simulations on Starlink constellation show at least 42.8% improvement in spatial compression and 10.81% improvement in temporal forecasting compared to established baselines, using significantly smaller model footprint.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hierarchical SDN framework with GKAE provides scalable, efficient management of massive satellite constellations through improved compression, forecasting, and reduced computational requirements.

title: Trace-Level Analysis of Information Contamination in Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Reasoning over heterogeneous artifacts (PDFs, spreadsheets, slide decks, etc.) increasingly occurs within structured agent workflows that iteratively extract, transform, and reference external information. In these workflows, uncertainty is not merely an input-quality issue: it can redirect decomposition and routing decisions, reshape intermediate state, and produce qualitatively different execution trajectories. We study this phenomenon by treating uncertainty as a controlled variable: we inject structured perturbations into artifact-derived representations, execute fixed workflows under comprehensive logging, and quantify contamination via trace divergence in plans, tool invocations, and intermediate state.
