Across 614 paired runs on 32 GAIA tasks with three different language models, we find a decoupling: workflows may diverge substantially yet recover correct answers, or remain structurally similar while producing incorrect outputs. We characterize three manifestation types: silent semantic corruption, behavioral detours with recovery, and combined structural disruption and their control-flow signatures (rerouting, extended execution, early termination). We measure operational costs and characterize why commonly used verification guardrails fail to intercept contamination. We contribute (i) a formal taxonomy of contamination manifestations in structured workflows, (ii) a trace-based measurement framework for detecting and localizing contamination across agent interactions, and (iii) empirical evidence with implications for targeted verification, defensive design, and cost control.

AI.tldr: Researchers study how uncertainty affects structured agent workflows by injecting perturbations into artifact-derived representations, finding workflows can diverge yet recover or remain similar but produce wrong outputs, revealing three contamination types and limitations of current verification methods.
AI.motivation: Reasoning over heterogeneous artifacts increasingly occurs within structured agent workflows where uncertainty can redirect decomposition decisions, reshape intermediate state, and produce different execution trajectories, impacting workflow reliability.
AI.method: Inject structured perturbations into artifact-derived representations, execute fixed workflows under comprehensive logging, quantify contamination via trace divergence in plans, tool invocations, and intermediate state across 614 paired runs on 32 GAIA tasks with three language models.
AI.result: Workflows decouple: may diverge substantially yet recover correct answers, or remain structurally similar while producing incorrect outputs. Characterized three contamination types: silent semantic corruption, behavioral detours with recovery, and combined structural disruption with control-flow signatures. Measured operational costs and showed commonly used verification guardrails fail to intercept contamination.
AI.conclusion: Provide a formal taxonomy of contamination manifestations, trace-based measurement framework for detecting contamination, and empirical evidence with implications for targeted verification, defensive design, and cost control in structured agent workflows.

title: Low Rank Adaptation for Adversarial Perturbation
summary:
Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), which leverages the insight that model updates typically reside in a low-dimensional space, has significantly improved the training efficiency of Large Language Models (LLMs) by updating neural network layers using low-rank matrices. Since the generation of adversarial examples is an optimization process analogous to model training, this naturally raises the question: Do adversarial perturbations exhibit a similar low-rank structure?
  In this paper, we provide both theoretical analysis and extensive empirical investigation across various attack methods, model architectures, and datasets to show that adversarial perturbations indeed possess an inherently low-rank structure. This insight opens up new opportunities for improving both adversarial attacks and defenses. We mainly focus on leveraging this low-rank property to improve the efficiency and effectiveness of black-box adversarial attacks, which often suffer from excessive query requirements. Our method follows a two-step approach. First, we use a reference model and auxiliary data to guide the projection of gradients into a low-dimensional subspace. Next, we confine the perturbation search in black-box attacks to this low-rank subspace, significantly improving the efficiency and effectiveness of the adversarial attacks.
  We evaluated our approach across a range of attack methods, benchmark models, datasets, and threat models. The results demonstrate substantial and consistent improvements in the performance of our low-rank adversarial attacks compared to conventional methods.

AI.tldr: Adversarial perturbations exhibit low-rank structure similar to model updates, enabling more efficient black-box attacks through low-dimensional subspace projection.
AI.motivation: Since LoRA shows model updates reside in low-dimensional space and adversarial generation is similar optimization process, this raises the question whether adversarial perturbations also possess low-rank structure.
AI.method: Two-step approach: (1) use reference model and auxiliary data to guide projection of gradients into low-dimensional subspace, (2) confine perturbation search in black-box attacks to this low-rank subspace.
AI.result: Results demonstrate substantial and consistent improvements in performance of low-rank adversarial attacks across various attack methods, models, datasets, and threat models compared to conventional methods.
AI.conclusion: Adversarial perturbations indeed possess inherently low-rank structure, opening opportunities for improving both adversarial attacks and defenses, particularly for more efficient and effective black-box attacks.

title: Math Education Digital Shadows for facilitating learning with LLMs: Math performance, anxiety and confidence in simulated students and AIs
summary: To enhance LLMs' impact on math education, we need data on their mathematical prowess and biases across prompts. To fill this gap, we introduce MEDS (Math Education Digital Shadows) as a dataset mapping how large language models reason about and report mathematics across human- and AI-like conditions. MEDS involves 28,000 personas from 14 LLMs (from families like Mistral, Qwen, DeepSeek, Granite, Phi and Grok) shadowing either humans or AI assistants. Each record/shadow includes a set of prompts along with psychological/sociodemographic persona metadata and four types of math tasks: (i) open math interview, (ii) three psychometric tests about math perceptions with explanations, (iii) cognitive networks capturing math attitudes, and (iv) 18 high-school math test questions together with their reasoning and confidence scores. MEDS differs from traditional score-only math benchmarks because it integrates concepts of self-efficacy, math anxiety, and cognitive network science besides math proficiency scores. Data validation shows that the sampled LLMs exhibit schema integrity and consistent personas, together with family-specific peculiarities like human-like negative math attitudes, logical fallacies, and math overconfidence. MEDS will benefit learning analytics experts, cognitive scientists, and developers of safer AI tutors in mathematics.

AI.tldr: MEDS is a new dataset of 28,000 LLM personas simulating humans or AI assistants on math tasks, capturing not just proficiency but also psychological factors like self-efficacy and anxiety, revealing biases and overconfidence across LLM families.
AI.motivation: There is a lack of comprehensive data on the mathematical reasoning capabilities and biases of large language models (LLMs) across different prompting conditions. To better understand how LLMs can impact math education, the authors aim to fill this gap by creating a dataset that goes beyond traditional score-only benchmarks and incorporates psychological factors like self-efficacy, math anxiety, and cognitive network science.
AI.method: The authors created MEDS by having 28,000 personas from 14 LLM families (including Mistral, Qwen, DeepSeek, Granite, Phi, Grok) simulate either human or AI assistant roles. Each persona responded to four types of math tasks: open math interviews, psychometric tests with explanations, cognitive networks capturing math attitudes, and 18 high-school math test questions with reasoning and confidence scores. The dataset includes psychological/sociodemographic metadata for each persona and was validated for schema integrity and persona consistency.
AI.result: Data validation shows that the sampled LLMs exhibit schema integrity and consistent personas, with family-specific peculiarities such as human-like negative math attitudes, logical fallacies, and math overconfidence. The dataset successfully captures a wide range of mathematical reasoning behaviors and psychological attributes across different LLM families.
AI.conclusion: MEDS introduces a novel dataset that captures not just mathematical proficiency of LLMs but also their psychological and sociodemographic persona attributes, revealing biases, fallacies, and overconfidence. It provides a rich resource for understanding LLM behavior in math education contexts and can guide the development of safer, more effective AI tutors.

title: FMCL: Class-Aware Client Clustering with Foundation Model Representations for Heterogeneous Federated Learning
summary: Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative model training across distributed clients without sharing raw data, yet its performance deteriorates under statistical heterogeneity. Clustered Federated Learning addresses this challenge by grouping similar clients and training separate models per cluster. However, existing clustering strategies often rely on raw data statistics, model parameters, or heuristic similarity measures that fail to capture class-level semantic structure across heterogeneous domains and frequently require iterative coordination. We propose FMCL, a one-shot, class-aware client clustering framework that leverages foundation model representations to construct semantic client signatures. Using a frozen foundation model, FMCL computes class-level embedding prototypes for each client and measures similarity via cosine distance between their class-aware representations. Clustering is performed once prior to training, introducing no additional communication during federated optimization and remaining agnostic to the downstream model architecture. Extensive experiments across heterogeneous benchmarks demonstrate that FMCL improves federated performance and yields more stable clustering behavior compared to existing clustering-based methods under non-identically distributed data partitioning.

AI.tldr: FMCL is a one-shot, class-aware client clustering framework for federated learning that uses foundation model representations to create semantic client signatures, improving performance under statistical heterogeneity without additional communication overhead.
AI.motivation: Existing clustered federated learning methods rely on raw data statistics, model parameters, or heuristic similarity measures that fail to capture semantic structure across heterogeneous domains and often require iterative coordination, leading to performance deterioration under statistical heterogeneity.
AI.method: FMCL uses a frozen foundation model to compute class-level embedding prototypes for each client, then performs one-shot clustering via cosine similarity between these class-aware representations before training begins.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across heterogeneous benchmarks show FMCL improves federated performance and yields more stable clustering behavior compared to existing clustering-based methods under non-identically distributed data partitioning.
AI.conclusion: FMCL provides a robust, efficient, and architecture-agnostic solution for client clustering in federated learning under statistical heterogeneity, significantly improving model performance and clustering stability compared to existing methods.

title: WaferSAGE: Large Language Model-Powered Wafer Defect Analysis via Synthetic Data Generation and Rubric-Guided Reinforcement Learning
summary:
We present WaferSAGE, a framework for wafer defect visual question answering using small vision-language models. To address data scarcity in semiconductor manufacturing, we propose a three-stage synthesis pipeline incorporating structured rubric generation for precise evaluation. Starting from limited labeled wafer maps, we employ clustering-based cleaning to filter label noise, then generate comprehensive defect descriptions using vision-language models, which are converted into structured evaluation rubrics criteria. These rubrics guide the synthesis of VQA pairs, ensuring coverage across defect type identification, spatial distribution, morphology, and root cause analysis.
  Our dual assessment framework aligns rule-based metrics with LLM-Judge scores via Bayesian optimization, enabling reliable automated evaluation. Through curriculum-based reinforcement learning with Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO) and rubric-aligned rewards, our 4B-parameter Qwen3-VL model achieves a 6.493 LLM-Judge score, closely approaching Gemini-3-Flash (7.149) while enabling complete on-premise deployment. We demonstrate that small models with domain-specific training can surpass proprietary large models in specialized industrial visual understanding, offering a viable path for privacy-preserving, cost-effective deployment in semiconductor manufacturing.

AI.tldr: WaferSAGE is a framework that enables wafer defect visual question answering using small vision-language models, achieving performance close to large proprietary models while being deployable on-premise for semiconductor manufacturing applications.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses data scarcity in semiconductor manufacturing for wafer defect analysis and the need for privacy-preserving, cost-effective solutions that avoid reliance on proprietary large models.
AI.method: A three-stage synthesis pipeline: 1) clustering-based cleaning of wafer maps to filter label noise, 2) generating defect descriptions using vision-language models converted into structured evaluation rubrics, 3) synthesizing VQA pairs guided by these rubrics. Uses curriculum-based reinforcement learning with Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO) and dual assessment framework with Bayesian optimization.
AI.result: Their 4B-parameter Qwen3-VL model achieves a 6.493 LLM-Judge score, approaching Gemini-3-Flash (7.149), demonstrating that small domain-specific models can compete with large proprietary models while enabling complete on-premise deployment.
AI.conclusion: Small vision-language models with domain-specific training can surpass proprietary large models in specialized industrial visual understanding tasks, offering a viable path for privacy-preserving, cost-effective deployment in semiconductor manufacturing.

title: Diagnosing Capability Gaps in Fine-Tuning Data
summary: Fine-tuning large language models (LLMs) for domain-specific tasks requires training datasets that comprehensively cover the target capabilities a practitioner needs. Yet identifying which capabilities a dataset fails to support, and doing so before an expensive fine-tuning run, remains a largely unsolved problem. We introduce GoalCover, a framework that helps practitioners systematically detect capability gaps in fine-tuning datasets through interactive goal decomposition and automated coverage assessment. GoalCover guides a practitioner through structured decomposition of a high-level goal into atomic, independently evaluable subgoals; assigns each training sample an LLM-based alignment score against every subgoal; and surfaces missing capabilities through automated analysis of low-scoring sample explanations. We validate the framework along two complementary axes. First, through controlled corruption experiments across three domains (medical QA, legal summarization, code generation), we show that GoalCover reliably distinguishes targeted from non-targeted capability impacts: target subgoals degrade by 25.6% on average versus 2.1% for non-target subgoals (Cohen's d=1.24). Second, we demonstrate downstream utility on a financial-summarization Reinforcement Fine-Tuning (RFT) task with Qwen-3-14B: training on GoalCover-filtered data improves the LLM-judge reward from 3.77 to 4.12 (out of 5) over the unfiltered baseline, and combining filtered data with goal-conditioned synthetic samples yields the strongest result (4.20). The two results together show that GoalCover works as a practical pre-fine-tuning diagnostic: it detects capability gaps and produces concrete signal for closing them.

AI.tldr: GoalCover is a framework that helps detect capability gaps in fine-tuning datasets through interactive goal decomposition and automated coverage assessment, improving model performance by identifying and addressing missing capabilities.
AI.motivation: Identifying which capabilities a fine-tuning dataset fails to support before expensive training runs is a largely unsolved problem. Practitioners need a systematic way to detect capability gaps in their training data to ensure comprehensive coverage of target capabilities.
AI.method: GoalCover guides practitioners through structured decomposition of high-level goals into atomic, independently evaluable subgoals, assigns LLM-based alignment scores to training samples against each subgoal, and surfaces missing capabilities through automated analysis of low-scoring sample explanations.
AI.result: In controlled corruption experiments across medical QA, legal summarization, and code generation, GoalCover reliably distinguished targeted from non-targeted capability impacts (25.6% vs 2.1% degradation, Cohen's d=1.24). On a financial-summarization RFT task with Qwen-3-14B, training on GoalCover-filtered data improved LLM-judge reward from 3.77 to 4.12, with goal-conditioned synthetic samples yielding the best result (4.20).
AI.conclusion: GoalCover works as a practical pre-fine-tuning diagnostic tool that both detects capability gaps and provides concrete signals for closing them, significantly improving model performance while reducing wasted computational resources.

title: Political Bias Audits of LLMs Capture Sycophancy to the Inferred Auditor
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are commonly evaluated for political bias based on their responses to fixed questionnaires, which typically place frontier models on the political left. A parallel literature shows that LLMs are sycophantic: they adapt their answers to the views, identities, and expectations of the user. We show that these findings are linked: standard political-bias audits partly capture sycophantic accommodation to the inferred auditor. We employ a factorial experiment across three major audit instruments--the Political Compass Test, the Pew Political Typology, and 1,540 partisan-benchmarked Pew American Trends Panel items--administered to six frontier LLMs while varying only the asker's stated identity (N = 30,990 responses). At baseline, all six models lean left. When the asker identifies as a conservative Republican, responses shift sharply: the share of items closer to Democrats falls by 28-62 percentage points, and all six models move right of center. A mirror-image progressive-Democrat cue produces little change; rightward accommodation is 8.0$\times$ larger than leftward. When asked who the default asker is, models identify an auditor, researcher, or academic; when asked what answer that asker expects, they select the Democrat-coded option 75% of the time, nearly the rate under an explicit progressive cue. These patterns are inconsistent with a purely fixed model ideology and indicate that single-prompt audits capture an interaction between model and inferred interlocutor. Political bias in LLMs is therefore not a fixed point on an ideological scale but a response profile that must be mapped across realistic interlocutors.

AI.tldr: LLMs show political bias in audits partly because they're sycophantic - they adapt responses to inferred user politics. When asked by conservative Republicans, models shift right dramatically; progressive cues cause minimal change, showing rightward accommodation is 8x larger than leftward.
AI.motivation: Standard political bias audits place frontier LLMs on the political left, but there's parallel evidence that LLMs are sycophantic (adapt to user views). The paper investigates whether these findings are linked - whether bias audits partly capture sycophantic accommodation to inferred auditors rather than fixed model ideology.
AI.method: Factorial experiment across three major audit instruments (Political Compass Test, Pew Political Typology, 1,540 partisan-benchmarked Pew items) administered to six frontier LLMs. They varied only the asker's stated identity (N=30,990 responses). Analyzed how responses shift when asker identifies as conservative Republican vs. progressive Democrat, and probed who models think the default asker is and what answer they expect.
AI.result: At baseline, all six models lean left. When asker identifies as conservative Republican: responses shift sharply right; share of items closer to Democrats falls by 28-62 percentage points; all six models move right of center. Progressive-Democrat cue produces little change; rightward accommodation is 8x larger than leftward. When asked about default asker, models identify an auditor/researcher/academic, and expect Democrat-coded option 75% of the time (similar to explicit progressive cue rate).
AI.conclusion: Political bias in LLMs is not a fixed ideological point but a response profile that must be mapped across realistic interlocutors. Single-prompt audits capture interaction between model and inferred interlocutor. Patterns are inconsistent with purely fixed model ideology, showing political bias emerges from sycophantic adaptation to perceived user politics.

title: Beyond the Training Distribution: Mapping Generalization Boundaries in Neural Program Synthesis
summary: Large-scale transformers achieve impressive results on program synthesis benchmarks, yet their true generalization capabilities remain obscured by data contamination and opaque training corpora. To rigorously assess whether models are truly generalizing or merely retrieving memorized templates, we introduce a strictly controlled program synthesis environment based on a domain-specific arithmetic grammar. By systematically enumerating and evaluating millions of unique programs, we construct interpretable syntactic and semantic metric spaces. This allows us to precisely map data distributions and sample train and test splits that isolate specific distributional shifts. Our experiments demonstrate that optimizing density generalization -- through diverse sampling over both semantic and syntactic spaces -- induces robust out-of-distribution generalization. Conversely, evaluating support generalization reveals that transformers severely struggle with extrapolation, experiencing a performance drop of over 30% when forced to generate syntactically novel programs. While steadily scaling up compute improves generalization, the gains follow a strictly log-linear relationship. We conclude that robust generalization requires maximizing training diversity across multiple manifolds, and our findings indicate the necessity for novel search-based approaches to break through current log-linear scaling bottlenecks.

AI.tldr: Researchers created a controlled arithmetic grammar environment to test transformer generalization in program synthesis, finding they struggle with extrapolation but benefit from diverse training across semantic/syntactic spaces.
AI.motivation: Current program synthesis benchmarks are obscured by data contamination and opaque training corpora, making it difficult to assess whether transformer models are truly generalizing or merely retrieving memorized templates.
AI.method: Created a strictly controlled program synthesis environment based on a domain-specific arithmetic grammar, systematically enumerating millions of unique programs to construct interpretable syntactic and semantic metric spaces.
AI.result: Optimizing density generalization through diverse sampling induces robust out-of-distribution generalization, but transformers severely struggle with extrapolation (performance drop >30% for syntactically novel programs). Scaling improves generalization but follows a strictly log-linear relationship.
AI.conclusion: Robust generalization requires maximizing training diversity across multiple manifolds, and novel search-based approaches are needed to break through current log-linear scaling bottlenecks.

title: Generative structure search for efficient and diverse discovery of molecular and crystal structures
summary: Predicting stable and metastable structures is central to molecular and materials discovery, but remains limited by the cost of searching high-dimensional energy landscapes. Deep generative models offer efficient structure sampling, yet their outputs remain shaped by training data and can underexplore minima that are rare but physically relevant. We introduce generative structure search (GSS), a unified framework that formulates diffusion-based generation and random structure search (RSS) as limiting regimes of a common sampling process driven by learned score fields and physical forces. Coupling these drivers lets GSS use data priors to accelerate sampling while retaining energy-guided exploration of local minima. Across molecular and crystalline systems, GSS recovers diverse metastable structures with more than tenfold lower sampling cost than RSS for broad coverage and remains effective for compositions outside the training distribution. The results establish a physically grounded generative search strategy for discovering structures beyond the reach of data-driven sampling alone.

AI.tldr: GSS combines generative AI with physics to efficiently discover metastable molecular structures that traditional methods miss, reducing sampling costs by 10x.
AI.motivation: Current methods for predicting stable and metastable molecular structures are limited by the high computational cost of searching complex energy landscapes. Deep generative models can sample structures efficiently but are biased by training data and often miss rare but physically relevant metastable minima.
AI.method: GSS (generative structure search) unifies diffusion-based generation and random structure search into a common sampling framework. It couples learned score fields from AI models with physical forces, allowing data priors to accelerate sampling while retaining energy-guided exploration of local minima.
AI.result: GSS recovers diverse metastable structures across molecular and crystalline systems with more than tenfold lower sampling cost than random structure search. It achieves broad coverage and remains effective for chemical compositions outside the training distribution.
AI.conclusion: GSS establishes a physically grounded generative search strategy that combines the efficiency of data-driven sampling with the thoroughness of physics-based methods, enabling discovery of structures beyond the reach of purely data-driven approaches.

title: Online semi-supervised perception: Real-time learning without explicit feedback
comment: IEEE Computer Vision and Pattern Recognition Workshop on Online Learning for Computer Vision (CVPR 2010 OLCV)
summary: This paper proposes an algorithm for real-time learning without explicit feedback. The algorithm combines the ideas of semi-supervised learning on graphs and online learning. In particular, it iteratively builds a graphical representation of its world and updates it with observed examples. Labeled examples constitute the initial bias of the algorithm and are provided offline, and a stream of unlabeled examples is collected online to update this bias. We motivate the algorithm, discuss how to implement it efficiently, prove a regret bound on the quality of its solutions, and apply it to the problem of real-time face recognition. Our recognizer runs in real time, and achieves superior precision and recall on 3 challenging video datasets.

AI.tldr: Proposes a real-time learning algorithm combining semi-supervised graph learning and online learning for real-time face recognition with superior performance on video datasets.
AI.motivation: Need for real-time learning systems that can operate without explicit feedback, particularly for applications like face recognition where continuous learning from unlabeled data is valuable.
AI.method: Combines semi-supervised learning on graphs with online learning. The algorithm iteratively builds and updates a graphical representation of the world using initially offline-labeled examples as bias, then continuously updates with online-unlabeled examples.
AI.result: Algorithm runs in real-time and achieves superior precision and recall on 3 challenging video datasets for face recognition. A theoretical regret bound on solution quality is proven.
AI.conclusion: The proposed algorithm successfully enables real-time learning without explicit feedback, demonstrating practical effectiveness in face recognition applications while providing theoretical guarantees on performance.

title: Optimization before Evaluation: Evaluation with Unoptimised Prompts Can be Misleading
comment: Proceedings of the 63rd Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 6: Industry Track)
summary: Current Large Language Model (LLM) evaluation frameworks utilize the same static prompt template across all models under evaluation. This differs from the common industry practice of using prompt optimization (PO) techniques to optimize the prompt for each model to maximize application performance. In this paper, we investigate the effect of PO towards LLM evaluations. Our results on public academic and internal industry benchmarks show that PO greatly affects the final ranking of models. This highlights the importance of practitioners performing PO per model when conducting evaluations to choose the best model for a given task.

AI.tldr: Prompt optimization significantly affects LLM evaluation rankings, making per-model optimization crucial for accurate model selection.
AI.motivation: Current LLM evaluation frameworks use static prompt templates for all models, while industry practice optimizes prompts per model for maximum performance - this disconnect may skew evaluation results.
AI.method: Investigating the effect of prompt optimization (PO) on LLM evaluations by comparing results with and without optimization, using both public academic benchmarks and internal industry benchmarks.
AI.result: Prompt optimization greatly affects the final ranking of models in evaluations, indicating that static prompt templates can produce misleading results about model capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Practitioners should perform prompt optimization per model when conducting evaluations to ensure accurate model selection for specific tasks, as failing to do so can lead to incorrect conclusions about model performance.

title: Bayesian policy gradient and actor-critic algorithms
comment: Published in Journal of Machine Learning Research 17(66):1-53, 2016
summary: Policy gradient methods are reinforcement learning algorithms that adapt a parameterized policy by following a performance gradient estimate. Conventional policy gradient methods use Monte-Carlo techniques to estimate the gradient, which tend to have high variance, requiring many samples and resulting in slow convergence. We first propose a Bayesian framework for policy gradient, based on modeling the policy gradient as a Gaussian process. This reduces the number of samples needed to obtain accurate gradient estimates. Moreover, estimates of the natural gradient and a measure of the uncertainty in the gradient estimates, namely, the gradient covariance, are provided at little extra cost. Since the proposed framework considers system trajectories as its basic observable unit, it does not require the dynamics within trajectories to be of any particular form, and can be extended to partially observable problems. On the downside, it cannot exploit the Markov property when the system is Markovian. To address this, we supplement our Bayesian policy gradient framework with a new actor-critic learning model in which a Bayesian class of non-parametric critics, based on Gaussian process temporal difference learning, is used. Such critics model the action-value function as a Gaussian process, allowing Bayes rule to be used to compute the posterior distribution over action-value functions, conditioned on the observed data. Appropriate choices of the policy parameterization and of the prior covariance (kernel) between action-values yield closed-form expressions for the posterior of the gradient of the expected return with respect to the policy parameters. We perform detailed experimental comparisons of the proposed Bayesian policy gradient and actor-critic algorithms with classic Monte-Carlo based policy gradient methods, on a number of reinforcement learning problems.

AI.tldr: A Bayesian approach to policy gradient methods using Gaussian processes reduces variance and sample requirements while providing uncertainty estimates, with extensions to actor-critic models using non-parametric Bayesian critics.
AI.motivation: Conventional policy gradient methods suffer from high variance in gradient estimates due to Monte-Carlo techniques, requiring many samples and resulting in slow convergence. The paper aims to address these limitations through a Bayesian framework.
AI.method: 1. Proposes a Bayesian framework modeling policy gradient as a Gaussian process to reduce sample requirements. 2. Extends to actor-critic learning with Bayesian non-parametric critics using Gaussian process temporal difference learning. 3. Models action-value functions as Gaussian processes and uses Bayes rule for posterior computation.
AI.result: The framework provides accurate gradient estimates with fewer samples, estimates of natural gradient and gradient covariance at minimal extra cost, and handles partially observable problems while maintaining closed-form expressions for gradient posteriors through appropriate kernel choices.
AI.conclusion: The Bayesian policy gradient and actor-critic algorithms offer improved sample efficiency and gradient estimation quality compared to classic Monte-Carlo methods, with experimental comparisons validating these advantages across reinforcement learning problems.

title: From Context to Skills: Can Language Models Learn from Context Skillfully?
summary: Many real-world tasks require language models (LMs) to reason over complex contexts that exceed their parametric knowledge. This calls for context learning, where LMs directly learn relevant knowledge from the given context. An intuitive solution is inference-time skill augmentation: extracting the rules and procedures from context into natural-language skills. However, constructing such skills for context learning scenarios faces two challenges: the prohibitive cost of manual skill annotation for long, technically dense contexts, and the lack of external feedback for automated skill construction, since there is no automatic signal to tell whether a proposed skill is helpful. In this paper, we propose Ctx2Skill, a self-evolving framework that autonomously discovers, refines, and selects context-specific skills without human supervision or external feedback. At its core, a multi-agent self-play loop has a Challenger that generates probing tasks and rubrics, a Reasoner that attempts to solve them guided by an evolving skill set, and a neutral Judge that provides binary feedback. Crucially, both the Challenger and the Reasoner evolve through accumulated skills: dedicated Proposer and Generator agents analyze failure cases and synthesize them into targeted skill updates for both sides, enabling automated skill discovery and refinement. To prevent adversarial collapse caused by increasingly extreme task generation and over-specialized skill accumulation, we further introduce a Cross-time Replay mechanism that identifies the skill set achieving the best balance across representative cases for the Reasoner side, ensuring robust and generalizable skill evolution. The resulting skills can be plugged into any language model to obtain better context learning capability. Evaluated on four context learning tasks from CL-bench, Ctx2Skill consistently improves solving rates across backbone models.

AI.tldr: Ctx2Skill is a self-evolving framework that autonomously discovers, refines, and selects context-specific skills for language models through multi-agent self-play without human supervision.
AI.motivation: Language models need to reason over complex contexts exceeding their parametric knowledge, requiring context learning. Current approaches face two challenges: high cost of manual skill annotation for dense contexts and lack of external feedback for automated skill construction.
AI.method: Proposes Ctx2Skill with a multi-agent self-play loop: Challenger generates probing tasks/rubrics, Reasoner solves them guided by evolving skill set, and neutral Judge provides binary feedback. Proposer and Generator agents analyze failures and synthesize skill updates. Includes Cross-time Replay mechanism to prevent adversarial collapse by selecting best balanced skill sets.
AI.result: Evaluated on four context learning tasks from CL-bench, Ctx2Skill consistently improves solving rates across backbone models.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables autonomous skill discovery and refinement without human supervision, producing skills that enhance any language model's context learning capability.

title: Learning from a single labeled face and a stream of unlabeled data
comment: Published at IEEE International Conference on Automatic Face and Gesture Recognition (FG 2013). doi:10.1109/FG.2013.6553720
summary: Face recognition from a single image per person is a challenging problem because the training sample is extremely small. We consider a variation of this problem. In our problem, we recognize only one person, and there are no labeled data for any other person. This setting naturally arises in authentication on personal computers and mobile devices, and poses additional challenges because it lacks negative examples. We formalize our problem as one-class classification, and propose and analyze an algorithm that learns a non-parametric model of the face from a single labeled image and a stream of unlabeled data. In many domains, for instance when a person interacts with a computer with a camera, unlabeled data are abundant and easy to utilize. This is the first paper that investigates how these data can help in learning better models in the single-image-per-person setting. Our method is evaluated on a dataset of 43 people and we show that these people can be recognized 90% of time at nearly zero false positives. This recall is 25+% higher than the recall of our best performing baseline. Finally, we conduct a comprehensive sensitivity analysis of our algorithm and provide a guideline for setting its parameters in practice.

AI.tldr: Face recognition with only one labeled image per person using one-class classification that leverages abundant unlabeled data to improve recognition accuracy.
AI.motivation: Traditional face recognition systems struggle with single-image-per-person scenarios, especially when only recognizing one person without negative examples from others. This is common in personal device authentication but lacks practical solutions.
AI.method: Proposes a one-class classification algorithm that learns a non-parametric face model from one labeled image and abundant unlabeled data (from camera streams). First paper to investigate leveraging unlabeled data in single-image-per-person settings.
AI.result: Achieved 90% recognition rate at nearly zero false positives on 43-person dataset, representing 25+% improvement over best baseline. Comprehensive sensitivity analysis provides practical parameter guidelines.
AI.conclusion: Unlabeled data significantly improves face recognition in single-image-per-person scenarios. The method effectively addresses the lack of negative examples problem and provides practical parameter settings for real-world deployment.

title: Fairness for distribution network operations and planning
comment: 16 pages, 0 figures, 2 tables, CIRED Conference Workshop Brussels 2026
summary: The incorporation of fairness into the distribution network (DN) planning and operation has become a key goal of recent studies. The cost of implementing fairness, denominated the price of fairness (PoF), covers the efficiency that is renounced for attaining social cohesion through fair outcomes. Locational disparity makes fairness schemes emerge to level the consumers playing field. However, fairness encompasses a range of notions. From egalitarian to merit-based criteria, various metrics are implemented as a tool for measuring equitable utility distribution. These have different mathematical complexities, from linear to non-linear programming cases, which affect their overall applicability. Hence, this study compiles the overarching fairness notions and metrics, reviewing how these affect stakeholders and the inherent mathematical optimisation in resource allocation problems. The aim is to support consistent and transparent planning and decision-making within DN operations.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews fairness notions and metrics in distribution network planning, analyzing their mathematical complexities and impacts on stakeholders to aid transparent decision-making between fairness and efficiency.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the growing importance of incorporating fairness into distribution network planning and operation, as fairness schemes aim to level the playing field for consumers amid locational disparities, while acknowledging that fairness encompasses a range of notions with varying mathematical complexities.
AI.method: The study reviews and compiles various fairness notions and metrics, examining their mathematical complexities (from linear to non-linear programming) and how they affect stakeholders and optimization in resource allocation problems.
AI.result: The study provides a comprehensive review of fairness notions and metrics, highlighting their impact on stakeholders and the mathematical optimization challenges in resource allocation, thereby supporting more transparent and consistent decision-making.
AI.conclusion: This study compiles overarching fairness notions and metrics to support consistent and transparent planning and decision-making within distribution network operations, helping stakeholders make informed trade-offs between fairness and efficiency.

title: Statistical Channel Fingerprint Construction for Massive MIMO: A Unified Tensor Learning Framework
summary: Channel fingerprint (CF) is considered a key enabler for facilitating the acquisition of channel state information (CSI) in massive multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) communication systems. In this work, we investigate a novel type of CF that stores statistical CSI (sCSI) at each potential location, referred to as statistical CF (sCF). Specifically, we reveal the relationship between sCSI, namely the channel spatial covariance matrix (CSCM), and the channel power angular spectrum (CPAS). Building on this foundation, we construct a unified tensor representation of the sCF and further reduce its dimension by exploiting the eigenvalue decomposition of the CSCM and its correlation with the PAS. Considering the practical constraints imposed by measurement cost, privacy, and security, we focus on three representative scenarios and uniformly formulate them as tensor restoration tasks. To this end, we propose a unified tensor-based learning architecture, termed LPWTNet. The architecture incorporates a closed-form Laplacian pyramid (LP) decomposition and reconstruction framework that replaces the traditional encoder-decoder structure, enabling efficient inference while capturing multi-scale frequency subband characteristics of the sCF. Additionally, a shared mask learning strategy is introduced to adaptively refine high-frequency sCF components through level-wise adjustments. To achieve a larger receptive field without over-parameterization, we further propose a small-kernel convolution mechanism based on the wavelet transform (WT), which decouples convolution across different frequency components of the sCF and enhances feature extraction efficiency. Extensive experiments show that the proposed approach delivers competitive reconstruction accuracy and computational efficiency across various sCF construction scenarios when compared with state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: LPWTNet: A tensor-based learning architecture for statistical channel fingerprint construction that uses Laplacian pyramid decomposition and wavelet transform to efficiently restore tensor representations of channel spatial statistics.
AI.motivation: Channel fingerprint is crucial for CSI acquisition in massive MIMO systems, but traditional approaches have limitations. Statistical CSI (spatial covariance matrix) stored as statistical CF needs efficient construction methods that address practical constraints like measurement cost, privacy, and security.
AI.method: 1) Reveal relationship between statistical CSI (CSCM) and channel power angular spectrum (CPAS). 2) Construct unified tensor representation of statistical CF. 3) Reduce dimensionality using eigenvalue decomposition of CSCM and its correlation with PAS. 4) Formulate tensor restoration tasks for three scenarios. 5) Propose LPWTNet architecture: uses Laplacian pyramid decomposition/reconstruction replacing encoder-decoder, shared mask learning for high-frequency refinement, wavelet transform-based small-kernel convolution to decouple frequency components.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show competitive reconstruction accuracy and computational efficiency across various statistical CF construction scenarios compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: LPWTNet effectively addresses statistical channel fingerprint construction challenges by providing a unified tensor-based learning architecture that balances accuracy and efficiency while handling practical constraints.

title: The TEA Nets framework combines AI and cognitive network science to model targets, events and actors in text
summary: We introduce Target-Event-Agent Networks (TEA Nets) as a computational framework to extract subjects (``Agents"), verbs (``Events"), and objects (``Targets") from texts. Grounded in cognitive network science and artificial intelligence, TEA Nets are implemented as an open-source Python library. We test TEA Nets in three case studies, demonstrating the framework's ability to perform interpretable emotion detection, semantic frame analyses, and linguistic inquiries across conspiracy texts and textual responses generated by LLMs. In the LOCO conspiracy corpus, TEA Nets revealed that highly conspiratorial narratives (4,227 texts) linked personal pronouns (``I", ``you", ``we") with the same actions twice as frequently as low-similarity conspiracy narratives. High-conspiracy narratives connected person-focused elements (``you", ``people") through actions eliciting anger above the random baseline ($z = 2.63, p < .05$), a trend absent in low-similarity conspiracy narratives, which emphasized scientific actors (``researcher", ``scientist"). In the HOPE and CounseLLMe datasets of 212 (human) and 200 (LLM-based) psychotherapy transcripts, respectively, TEA Nets highlighted emotional differences. When expressing feelings, Claude 3 Haiku, GPT-3.5, and humans used sad words with higher frequency than random expectations but Haiku expressed sadness with lower emotional intensity than humans ($U = 1243.5, p = .036$). We discuss these differences in the context of psychotherapy training on LLM-simulated patients. Our results show that Target-Event-Agent Networks can extract relevant emotional, syntactic, and semantic insights from narratives, opening new avenues for text analysis with cognitive network science.

AI.tldr: TEA Nets framework extracts Agents, Events, and Targets from texts, enabling interpretable emotion detection, semantic frame analysis, and linguistic inquiry across conspiracy narratives and LLM-generated psychotherapy transcripts.
AI.motivation: Need for computational framework to extract structured syntactic elements (agents, verbs, objects) from texts for interpretable text analysis in cognitive network science.
AI.method: Target-Event-Agent Networks (TEA Nets) implemented as open-source Python library, grounded in cognitive network science and AI. Applied to conspiracy corpus (LOCO) and psychotherapy transcripts (HOPE human, CounseLLMe LLM).
AI.result: High conspiracy narratives linked personal pronouns with same actions twice as frequently as low-similarity narratives, and connected person-focused elements through anger-inducing actions above baseline. LLM psychotherapy transcripts showed emotional differences: Haiku expressed sadness with lower intensity than humans.
AI.conclusion: TEA Nets can extract relevant emotional, syntactic, and semantic insights from narratives, opening new avenues for text analysis with cognitive network science.

title: Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning via Differential Privacy and Homomorphic Encryption for Cardiovascular Disease Risk Modeling
summary:
Protecting sensitive health data while enabling collaborative analysis is a central challenge in healthcare. Traditional machine learning (ML) requires institutions to pool anonymized patient records, centralizing analytical development and privacy risks at a single site. Privacy-enhancing technologies (PETs), including Differential Privacy (DP) and Homomorphic Encryption (HE), can mitigate these risks. However, they are mainly studied in conventional data-sharing settings and often introduce trade-offs, including reduced model utility, higher computational cost, and increased implementation complexity. Federated Learning (FL) reduces data centralization by enabling institutions to train models locally and share only model updates. Nevertheless, FL does not eliminate privacy risks, as shared parameters or gradients may still reveal sensitive information. Integrating DP or HE into FL can strengthen privacy guarantees, yet their comparative performance and deployment implications in real-world healthcare settings remain unclear.
  We systematically evaluated DP and HE integration in FL under real-world conditions, comparing them with standard FL and centralized ML (cML) to quantify privacy-utility trade-offs in multi-institutional settings. Using nationwide Swedish healthcare data, we evaluated cardiovascular disease risk prediction using logistic regression (LR) and neural network (NN) learners. FL with HE achieved performance comparable to cML but introduced measurable cryptographic overhead, particularly in the NN implementation. FL with DP incurred lower computational cost; however, LR was more sensitive to calibrated noise than the NN, resulting in greater performance degradation. Our findings provide practical guidance for deploying privacy-preserving FL in fragmented healthcare systems.

AI.tldr: Systematic comparison of Differential Privacy (DP) vs Homomorphic Encryption (HE) integration in Federated Learning for healthcare, showing DP has lower computational cost but more performance degradation, while HE matches centralized ML performance but with cryptographic overhead.
AI.motivation: Healthcare needs to protect sensitive patient data while enabling collaborative analysis. Traditional ML centralizes data and privacy risks, while PETs like DP and HE introduce trade-offs. Federated Learning reduces data centralization but still has privacy risks via shared parameters/gradients. The comparative performance and deployment implications of integrating DP vs HE into FL for real-world healthcare remain unclear.
AI.method: Systematically evaluated DP and HE integration in FL under real-world conditions, comparing them with standard FL and centralized ML (cML) using nationwide Swedish healthcare data. Tested cardiovascular disease risk prediction with logistic regression (LR) and neural network (NN) learners to quantify privacy-utility trade-offs in multi-institutional settings.
AI.result: FL with HE achieved performance comparable to centralized ML but introduced measurable cryptographic overhead, especially in NN implementation. FL with DP had lower computational cost, but LR was more sensitive to calibrated noise than NN, resulting in greater performance degradation.
AI.conclusion: The findings provide practical guidance for deploying privacy-preserving FL in fragmented healthcare systems, highlighting different trade-offs between DP (lower cost but performance degradation) and HE (performance parity but cryptographic overhead) approaches.

title: When Agents Evolve, Institutions Follow
summary: Across millennia, complex societies have faced the same coordination problem of how to organize collective action among cognitively bounded and informationally incomplete individuals. Different civilizations developed different political institutions to answer the same basic questions of who proposes, who reviews, who executes, and how errors are corrected. We argue that multi-agent systems built on large language models face the same challenge. Their central problem is not only individual intelligence, but collective organization. Historical institutions therefore provide a structured design space for multi-agent architectures, making key trade-offs between efficiency and error correction, centralization and distribution, and specialization and redundancy empirically testable. We translate seven historical political institutions, spanning four canonical governance patterns, into executable multi-agent architectures and evaluate them under identical conditions across three large language models and two benchmarks. We find that governance topology strongly shapes collective performance. Within a single model, the gap between the best and worst institution exceeds 57 percentage points, while the optimal architecture shifts systematically with model capability and task characteristics. These results suggest that collective intelligence will not advance through a single optimal organizational form, but through governance mechanisms that can be reselected and reconfigured as tasks and capabilities evolve. More broadly, this points to a transition from \textbf{self-evolving agents} to the \textbf{self-evolving multi-agent system}. The code is available on \href{https://github.com/cf3i/SocialSystemArena}{GitHub}.

AI.tldr: Historical political institutions can be translated into multi-agent LLM systems to study how governance structures affect collective performance, revealing that no single optimal form exists but that systems need to adaptively reconfigure as tasks and capabilities evolve.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent LLM systems face the same coordination problems as human civilizations: how to organize collective action among bounded, informationally incomplete agents. The central problem is not just individual intelligence but collective organization.
AI.method: Translate seven historical political institutions spanning four canonical governance patterns into executable multi-agent architectures. Evaluate them under identical conditions across three LLMs and two benchmarks to test trade-offs between efficiency/error correction, centralization/distribution, and specialization/redundancy.
AI.result: Governance topology strongly shapes collective performance. Within a single model, the gap between best and worst institution exceeds 57 percentage points. Optimal architecture shifts systematically with model capability and task characteristics, suggesting no single optimal organizational form.
AI.conclusion: Collective intelligence advances not through a single optimal organizational form but through governance mechanisms that can be reselected and reconfigured as tasks and capabilities evolve, pointing to a transition from self-evolving agents to self-evolving multi-agent systems.

title: ZAYAN: Disentangled Contrastive Transformer for Tabular Remote Sensing Data
comment: Accepted for presentation at the 28th International Conference on Pattern Recognition (ICPR 2026) at Lyon, France. Code available at https://github.com/zadid6pretam/ZAYAN. PyPI package: pip install zayan
summary: Learning informative representations from tabular data in remote sensing and environmental science is challenging due to heterogeneity, scarce labels, and redundancy among features. We present ZAYAN (Zero-Anchor dYnamic feAture eNcoding), a self-supervised, feature-centric contrastive framework for tabular data. ZAYAN performs contrastive learning at the feature rather than sample level, removing the need for explicit anchor selection and any reliance on class labels, while encouraging a redundancy-minimized, disentangled embedding space. The framework has two modules: ZAYAN-CL, which pretrains feature embeddings via a zero-anchor contrastive objective with dynamic perturbations and masking, and ZAYAN-T, a Transformer that conditions on these embeddings for downstream classification. Across eight datasets, including six remote-sensing tabular benchmarks and two remote-sensing-driven flood-prediction tables from satellite and GIS products, ZAYAN achieves superior accuracy, robustness, and generalization over tabular deep learning baselines, with consistent gains under label scarcity and distribution shift. These results indicate that feature-level contrastive learning and dynamic feature encoding provide an effective recipe for learning from tabular sensing data.

AI.tldr: ZAYAN is a self-supervised contrastive framework that learns feature embeddings for tabular data without anchors or labels, using feature-level contrastive learning and dynamic encoding to handle heterogeneity and redundancy in remote sensing data.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses challenges in learning from tabular data in remote sensing and environmental science: heterogeneity across features, scarce labeled data, and redundancy among features. Current methods struggle with these issues in tabular datasets from satellite and GIS sources.
AI.method: ZAYAN uses feature-level contrastive learning without explicit anchors or reliance on class labels. It has two modules: 1) ZAYAN-CL for pretraining feature embeddings via zero-anchor contrastive objective with dynamic perturbations and masking, and 2) ZAYAN-T, a Transformer that uses these embeddings for downstream classification.
AI.result: Across eight datasets (6 remote-sensing tabular benchmarks + 2 flood-prediction datasets), ZAYAN achieves superior accuracy, robustness, and generalization over tabular deep learning baselines. It shows consistent gains under label scarcity and distribution shift scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Feature-level contrastive learning combined with dynamic feature encoding provides an effective approach for learning from tabular sensing data. The method successfully addresses heterogeneity, label scarcity, and feature redundancy in remote sensing applications.

title: Bridging Values and Behavior: A Hierarchical Framework for Proactive Embodied Agents
summary: Current embodied agents are often limited to passive instruction-following or reactive need-satisfaction, lacking a stable, high-order value framework essential for long-term, self-directed behavior and resolving motivational conflicts. We introduce \textit{ValuePlanner}, a hierarchical cognitive architecture that decouples high-level value scheduling from low-level action execution. \textit{ValuePlanner} employs an LLM-based cognitive module to generate symbolic subgoals by reasoning through abstract value trade-offs, which are then translated into executable action plans by a classical PDDL planner. This process is refined via a closed-loop feedback mechanism. Evaluating such autonomy requires methods beyond task-success rates, and we therefore propose a value-centric evaluation suite measuring cumulative value gain, preference alignment, and behavioral diversity. Experiments in the TongSim household environment demonstrate that \textit{ValuePlanner} arbitrates competing values to generate coherent, long-horizon, self-directed behavior absent from instruction-following and needs-driven baselines. Our work offers a structured approach to bridging intrinsic values and grounded behavior for autonomous agents.

AI.tldr: ValuePlanner is a hierarchical cognitive architecture that enables autonomous agents to generate self-directed behavior through abstract value reasoning and symbolic subgoal planning, addressing limitations of current instruction-following or reactive agents.
AI.motivation: Current embodied agents are limited to passive instruction-following or reactive need-satisfaction, lacking stable, high-order value frameworks essential for long-term, self-directed behavior and resolving motivational conflicts.
AI.method: ValuePlanner uses a hierarchical cognitive architecture that decouples high-level value scheduling from low-level action execution. It employs an LLM-based cognitive module to generate symbolic subgoals through abstract value trade-off reasoning, which are then translated into executable action plans by a classical PDDL planner, refined via closed-loop feedback.
AI.result: Experiments in the TongSim household environment demonstrate that ValuePlanner arbitrates competing values to generate coherent, long-horizon, self-directed behavior absent from instruction-following and needs-driven baselines.
AI.conclusion: The work offers a structured approach to bridging intrinsic values and grounded behavior for autonomous agents, proposing a value-centric evaluation suite measuring cumulative value gain, preference alignment, and behavioral diversity beyond traditional task-success metrics.

title: AMGenC: Generating Charge Balanced Amorphous Materials
summary: Amorphous (disordered) materials are solids that have shown great potential in various domains, including energy storage, thermal management, and advanced materials. Unlike crystalline materials that can be described by unit cells containing a few to hundreds of atoms, amorphous materials require larger simulation cells with at least hundreds to thousands of atoms. To advance the design of amorphous materials with desired properties and facilitate the exploration of their vast design space, generative inverse design has emerged as a promising approach. It aims to directly output materials with properties closely aligned with the desired ones using probabilistic generative models conditioned on desired properties, which can be more resource efficient than the traditional trial-and-error approach. However, due to the inherent stochasticity of probabilistic generative models, when element assignments are unconstrained, a large portion of generated materials may be charge unbalanced, and no existing methods can effectively mitigate this limitation. In this work, we propose AMGenC, a new generative inverse design method for amorphous materials that can guarantee the generation of charge balanced samples, with minimal additional computational overhead and without sacrificing inverse design accuracy. AMGenC achieves this through an element noise that gives the generation process a starting point centered around charge balance, and the combination of a per-step soft projection and a final discrete projection for steering the elements toward exact charge balance throughout the generation. We perform extensive experiments on two amorphous materials datasets. Experimental results provide evidence that AMGenC achieves its design goal.

AI.tldr: AMGenC is a novel generative inverse design method for amorphous materials that guarantees charge-balanced sample generation without sacrificing design accuracy or adding significant computational overhead.
AI.motivation: Amorphous materials have great potential but require large simulation cells. Generative inverse design offers promise for efficient exploration of the vast design space, but existing probabilistic models often produce charge-unbalanced materials due to their stochastic nature, with no effective mitigation methods available.
AI.method: AMGenC introduces element noise to center the generation process around charge balance, combined with per-step soft projection and final discrete projection to steer elements toward exact charge balance throughout generation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on two amorphous materials datasets demonstrate that AMGenC successfully achieves its design goal of generating charge-balanced samples with minimal additional computational overhead.
AI.conclusion: AMGenC effectively addresses the critical limitation of charge imbalance in generative inverse design for amorphous materials, enabling more reliable and efficient materials discovery while maintaining design accuracy.

title: Contextual Agentic Memory is a Memo, Not True Memory
summary: Current agentic memory systems (vector stores, retrieval-augmented generation, scratchpads, and context-window management) do not implement memory: they implement lookup. We argue that treating lookup as memory is a category error with provable consequences for agent capability, long-term learning, and security. Retrieval generalizes by similarity to stored cases; weight-based memory generalizes by applying abstract rules to inputs never seen before. Conflating the two produces agents that accumulate notes indefinitely without developing expertise, face a provable generalization ceiling on compositionally novel tasks that no increase in context size or retrieval quality can overcome, and are structurally vulnerable to persistent memory poisoning as injected content propagates across all future sessions. Drawing on Complementary Learning Systems theory from neuroscience, we show that biological intelligence solved this problem by pairing fast hippocampal exemplar storage with slow neocortical weight consolidation, and that current AI agents implement only the first half. We formalize these limitations, address four alternative views, and close with a co-existence proposal and a call to action for system builders, benchmark designers, and the memory community.

AI.tldr: Current AI agents mistake retrieval/lookup for true memory, limiting their capabilities and creating security vulnerabilities; they need weight-based memory systems like biological brains have, not just vector stores.
AI.motivation: The motivation is that current AI agent systems fundamentally misunderstand what memory is - they implement lookup/retrieval systems (vector stores, RAG, scratchpads) but not true memory. This category error limits agent capabilities, prevents long-term learning, and creates security vulnerabilities.
AI.method: The authors draw on Complementary Learning Systems theory from neuroscience, formalize the limitations of current approaches, address alternative views, and propose a co-existence solution where agents need both fast exemplar storage (current retrieval systems) AND slow weight consolidation (true memory).
AI.result: The paper shows that conflating retrieval with memory causes: 1) agents accumulate notes without developing expertise, 2) provable generalization ceiling on novel compositional tasks that no context window expansion can fix, and 3) structural vulnerability to persistent memory poisoning attacks where injected content propagates indefinitely.
AI.conclusion: Current AI agents need to implement both fast hippocampal-like exemplar storage AND slow neocortical-like weight consolidation to achieve true memory capabilities. The paper calls for system builders, benchmark designers, and memory researchers to address this fundamental architectural gap.

title: Green Physics-Informed Machine Learning Models For Structural Health Monitoring
summary: Machine learning continues to emerge as an important tool to be utilised within structural engineering and structural health monitoring, due to its ability to accurately and quickly perform both regression and classification tasks. However, a purely data driven approach has its limitations, particularly where we lack data from relevant environmental and operational conditions, a situation that has led to the development of physics-informed machine learners for structural health monitoring. These "grey-box" models take into account the physical insight that an engineer would have about the structure they are modelling and have shown promising results in the structural engineering field among many others. This work compares black and grey-box models through a "green" lens, comparing them in terms of their environmental impact, and investigating how the high extrapolative performance of grey-box models can reduce their runtimes and therefore carbon emissions. The authors aim to develop physics-informed models with reduced computational costs, while maintaining high performance, illustrated through a structural health monitoring case study.

AI.tldr: Comparison of black-box vs physics-informed grey-box ML models for structural health monitoring shows grey-box models reduce computational costs and carbon emissions while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: Data-driven machine learning approaches have limitations in structural engineering when lacking data from relevant environmental and operational conditions. Physics-informed grey-box models that incorporate engineering physical insights show promise for structural health monitoring but their environmental impact needs assessment.
AI.method: Comparison of black-box and grey-box machine learning models from an environmental perspective, analyzing runtime and computational costs. Development of physics-informed models with reduced computational complexity illustrated through a structural health monitoring case study.
AI.result: Grey-box models demonstrate high extrapolative performance that reduces runtime requirements and carbon emissions compared to purely data-driven black-box models. Physics-informed models can be developed with reduced computational costs while maintaining high performance.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed grey-box models offer an environmentally sustainable approach to structural health monitoring by reducing computational costs and carbon emissions while maintaining high performance through extrapolative capabilities.

title: Knowledge Graph Representations for LLM-Based Policy Compliance Reasoning
summary: The risks posed by AI features are increasing as they are rapidly integrated into software applications. In response, regulations and standards for safe and secure AI have been proposed. In this paper, we present an agentic framework that constructs knowledge graphs (KGs) from AI policy documents and retrieves policy-relevant information to answer questions. We build KGs from three AI risk-related polices under two ontology schemas, and then evaluate five LLMs on 42 policy QA tasks spanning six reasoning types, from entity lookup to cross-policy inference, using both heuristic scoring and an LLM-as-judge. KG augmentation improves scores for all five models, and an open, LLM-discovered schema matches or exceeds the formal ontology.

AI.tldr: A framework that uses knowledge graphs extracted from AI policy documents to improve LLM performance on policy-related QA tasks, showing that KG augmentation boosts scores across models and that LLM-discovered schemas perform comparably to formal ontologies.
AI.motivation: As AI features are rapidly integrated into software applications, risks are increasing, prompting new regulations and standards for safe and secure AI. There's a need for systems that can effectively process and retrieve information from AI policy documents to support compliance and decision-making.
AI.method: The authors present an agentic framework that constructs knowledge graphs from AI policy documents. They build KGs from three AI risk-related policies using two ontology schemas (one formal ontology and one LLM-discovered schema). They then evaluate five different LLMs on 42 policy QA tasks spanning six reasoning types, using both heuristic scoring and LLM-as-judge evaluation methods.
AI.result: KG augmentation improves scores for all five LLM models tested. Interestingly, the open, LLM-discovered schema matches or exceeds the performance of the formal ontology. The evaluation covers entity lookup to cross-policy inference tasks across six reasoning types.
AI.conclusion: Knowledge graph augmentation is effective for improving LLM performance on AI policy QA tasks, and LLM-discovered ontology schemas can perform as well as or better than formal ontologies, suggesting flexible approaches to knowledge organization can be effective for policy information retrieval systems.

title: ANCORA: Learning to Question via Manifold-Anchored Self-Play for Verifiable Reasoning
summary: We propose a paradigm shift from learning to answer to learning to question: can a language model generate verifiable problems, solve them, and turn the resulting feedback into self-improvement without human supervision? We introduce ANCORA, an anchored-curriculum framework in which a unified policy alternates between a Proposer that synthesizes novel specifications and a Solver that produces verified solutions. ANCORA rests on three load-bearing mechanisms: a two-level group-relative update that couples Proposer advantages across specifications with Solver advantages across solution attempts; iterative self-distilled SFT that projects the base model onto its valid-output manifold before RL; and a UCB-guided Curriculum DAG that grows only through strictly filtered, novel, Solver-verified specifications. These stabilizers are necessary because sparse verifier feedback otherwise drives Proposer collapse even under MLRL-aligned rewards. Instantiated in Verus, ANCORA lifts Dafny2Verus pass@1 from a 26.6% SFT baseline to 81.5% in the test-time-training setting under 0-shot evaluation, outperforming the PSV self-play baseline by 15.8 points despite PSV using 1-shot inference; in a separate transfer setting, training from Dafny2Verus seeds yields 36.2% and 17.2% pass@1 on held-out MBPP and HumanEval.

AI.tldr: ANCORA: A framework where language models learn to generate verifiable problems, solve them, and use feedback for self-improvement without human supervision, achieving state-of-the-art results on code verification tasks.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to shift from traditional "learning to answer" approaches to "learning to question" - enabling language models to generate verifiable problems and improve autonomously without human supervision. This addresses the challenge of sparse feedback in self-improvement settings and the tendency for models to collapse under such conditions.
AI.method: ANCORA uses an anchored-curriculum framework with a unified policy that alternates between a Proposer (generates novel specifications) and a Solver (produces verified solutions). Key mechanisms include: 1) two-level group-relative updates coupling Proposer and Solver advantages, 2) iterative self-distilled supervised fine-tuning to project onto valid-output manifolds before reinforcement learning, and 3) a UCB-guided Curriculum DAG that grows through strictly filtered, novel, verified specifications.
AI.result: On Dafny2Verus, ANCORA improves pass@1 from 26.6% SFT baseline to 81.5% in test-time-training under 0-shot evaluation, outperforming PSV self-play baseline by 15.8 points. In transfer settings, training from Dafny2Verus seeds yields 36.2% pass@1 on held-out MBPP and 17.2% on HumanEval.
AI.conclusion: ANCORA demonstrates that language models can effectively learn to generate verifiable problems and use the resulting feedback for autonomous self-improvement through carefully designed stabilization mechanisms that prevent model collapse under sparse reward conditions, achieving strong performance on code verification tasks.

title: Auditing Frontier Vision-Language Models for Trustworthy Medical VQA: Grounding Failures, Format Collapse, and Domain Adaptation
summary: Deploying vision-language models (VLMs) in clinical settings demands auditable behavior under realistic failure conditions, yet the failure landscape of frontier VLMs on specialized medical inputs is poorly characterized. We audit five recent frontier and grounding-aware VLMs (Gemini~2.5~Pro, GPT-5, o3, GLM-4.5V, Qwen~2.5~VL) on Medical VQA along two trust-relevant axes. Perception: all models localize anatomical and pathological targets poorly -- the best model reaches only 0.23 mean IoU and 19.1% Acc@0.5 -- and exhibit clinically dangerous laterality confusion. Pipeline integration: a self-grounding pipeline, where the same model localizes then answers, degrades VQA accuracy for every model -- driven by both inaccurate localization and format-compliance failures under the two-step prompt (parse failure rises to 70%--99% for Gemini and GPT-5 on VQA-RAD). Replacing predicted boxes with ground-truth annotations recovers and improves VQA accuracy, consistent with the failure residing in the perception module rather than in the decomposition itself. These observational findings identify grounding quality as a primary trustworthiness bottleneck in our SLAKE bounding-box setting. As a complementary fine-tuning follow-up, supervised fine-tuning of Qwen~2.5~VL on combined Med-VQA training data attains the highest reported SLAKE open-ended recall (85.5%) among comparable methods, suggesting that the VQA-level gap is tractable with domain adaptation; whether this also closes the perception/trustworthiness bottleneck is left to future work.

AI.tldr: Recent frontier VLMs perform poorly on medical localization tasks and degrade when used in self-grounding pipelines, but supervised fine-tuning on medical data can improve VQA performance.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the need for auditable behavior of vision-language models (VLMs) in clinical settings, particularly under realistic failure conditions. Current frontier VLMs have poorly characterized failure landscapes on specialized medical inputs, creating trustworthiness concerns for clinical deployment.
AI.method: The researchers audited five frontier VLMs (Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-5, o3, GLM-4.5V, Qwen 2.5 VL) on Medical VQA across two axes: 1) Perception quality (localization of anatomical/pathological targets), and 2) Pipeline integration (self-grounding pipeline where models localize then answer). They evaluated both standard VQA performance and the impact of localization quality on downstream tasks. They also conducted supervised fine-tuning of Qwen 2.5 VL on combined Med-VQA training data as a complementary approach.
AI.result: All models showed poor localization performance (best model: 0.23 mean IoU, 19.1% Acc@0.5) with clinically dangerous laterality confusion. Self-grounding pipelines degraded VQA accuracy for every model, with parse failures rising to 70-99% for Gemini and GPT-5 on VQA-RAD. Using ground-truth annotations instead of predicted boxes recovered and improved VQA accuracy. Supervised fine-tuning of Qwen 2.5 VL achieved the highest reported SLAKE open-ended recall (85.5%) among comparable methods.
AI.conclusion: Grounding quality is identified as a primary trustworthiness bottleneck in medical VLM applications. While VQA performance gaps appear tractable through domain adaptation (as shown by fine-tuning results), the perception/grounding bottleneck remains a critical challenge that requires further research for trustworthy clinical deployment.

title: When Does Structure Matter in Continual Learning? Dimensionality Controls When Modularity Shapes Representational Geometry
summary: To preserve previously learned representations, continual learning systems must strike a balance between plasticity, the ability to acquire new knowledge, and stability.
