This stability-plasticity dilemma affects how representations can be reused across tasks: shared structure enables transfer when tasks are similar but may also induce interference when new learning disrupts existing representations. However, it remains unclear when and why structural separation influences this trade-off. In this study, we examine how network architecture, task similarity, and representational dimensionality jointly shape learning in a sequential task paradigm inspired by transfer-interference studies. We compare a task-partitioned modular recurrent network with a single-module baseline by systematically varying task similarity (low, medium, high) and the scale of weight initialization, which induces different learning regimes that we empirically characterize through the effective dimensionality of the learned representations. We find that architecture has minimal impact in high-dimensional regimes where representations are sufficiently unconstrained to accommodate multiple tasks without strong interference. In contrast, in lower-dimensional (rich) regimes, architectural separation is decisive: modular networks exhibit graded alignment of task-specific subspaces with overlap for similar tasks, partial orthogonalization for moderately dissimilar tasks, and stronger separation for dissimilar tasks. This graded geometry is absent in the single network baseline. Our findings suggest that representational dimensionality acts as a key organizing variable governing when structural separation becomes functionally relevant, and highlight adaptive geometry as a central principle for designing continual learning systems.

AI.tldr: Study finds representational dimensionality determines when architectural modularity matters in continual learning: minimal impact in high-dimensional regimes but decisive in low-dimensional regimes where modular networks show adaptive task-specific geometry absent in single networks.
AI.motivation: To understand when and why structural separation influences the stability-plasticity trade-off in continual learning systems, particularly how network architecture, task similarity, and representational dimensionality jointly shape learning.
AI.method: Sequential task paradigm comparing task-partitioned modular recurrent network with single-module baseline, varying task similarity (low, medium, high) and weight initialization scale to induce different learning regimes characterized through effective dimensionality of learned representations.
AI.result: Architecture has minimal impact in high-dimensional regimes where representations accommodate multiple tasks without strong interference. In lower-dimensional regimes, architectural separation is decisive: modular networks show graded alignment of task-specific subspaces with overlap for similar tasks, partial orthogonalization for moderately dissimilar tasks, and stronger separation for dissimilar tasks - geometry absent in single network baseline.
AI.conclusion: Representational dimensionality acts as a key organizing variable governing when structural separation becomes functionally relevant, and highlight adaptive geometry as a central principle for designing continual learning systems.

title: Iterative Multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Medical Question Answering
summary: Medical retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems typically operate on text chunks extracted from biomedical literature, discarding the rich visual content (tables, figures, structured layouts) of original document pages. We propose MED-VRAG, an iterative multimodal RAG framework that retrieves and reasons over PMC document page images instead of OCR'd text. The system pairs ColQwen2.5 patch-level page embeddings with a sharded MapReduce LLM filter, scaling to ~350K pages while keeping Stage-1 retrieval under 30 ms via an offline coarse-to-fine index (C=8 centroids per page, ANN over centroids, exact two-way scoring on the top-R shortlist). A vision-language model (VLM) then iteratively refines its query and accumulates evidence in a memory bank across up to 3 reasoning rounds, with a single iteration costing ~15.9 s and the full three-round pipeline ~47.8 s on 4xA100. Across four medical QA benchmarks (MedQA, MedMCQA, PubMedQA, MMLU-Med), MEDVRAG reaches 78.6% average accuracy. Under controlled comparison with the same Qwen2.5-VL-32B backbone, retrieval contributes a +5.8 point gain over the no-retrieval baseline; we also note a +1.8 point edge over MedRAG + GPT-4 (76.8%), with the caveat that this is a cross-paper rather than head-to-head comparison. Ablations isolate +1.0 from page-image vs text-chunk retrieval, +1.5 from iteration, and +1.0 from the memory bank.

AI.tldr: MED-VRAG is a multimodal medical RAG system that retrieves full document page images instead of text chunks, using vision-language models for iterative reasoning with memory banks to improve medical QA performance.
AI.motivation: Current medical RAG systems operate only on text chunks from biomedical literature, discarding valuable visual content like tables, figures, and structured layouts present in original document pages. This limits their ability to leverage multimodal information crucial for medical reasoning.
AI.method: 1) Retrieves PMC document page images using ColQwen2.5 patch-level page embeddings with sharded MapReduce LLM filter; 2) Scales to ~350K pages via offline coarse-to-fine indexing (C=8 centroids per page, ANN over centroids, exact scoring on top-R shortlist); 3) Vision-language model iteratively refines queries and accumulates evidence in memory bank across up to 3 reasoning rounds.
AI.result: Achieves 78.6% average accuracy across four medical QA benchmarks (MedQA, MedMCQA, PubMedQA, MMLU-Med). Retrieval contributes +5.8 point gain over no-retrieval baseline. Outperforms MedRAG+GPT-4 by +1.8 points (though cross-paper comparison). Ablations show: +1.0 from page-image vs text-chunk retrieval, +1.5 from iteration, and +1.0 from memory bank.
AI.conclusion: Multimodal retrieval using document page images instead of text chunks provides significant performance gains for medical QA. The iterative reasoning approach with memory banks further enhances answer quality. The system demonstrates practical efficiency with ~30ms retrieval and ~47.8s full pipeline on 4xA100 GPUs.

title: Optimized Deferral for Imbalanced Settings
summary: Learning algorithms can be significantly improved by routing complex or uncertain inputs to specialized experts, balancing accuracy with computational cost. This approach, known as learning to defer, is essential in domains like natural language generation, medical diagnosis, and computer vision, where an effective deferral can reduce errors at low extra resource consumption. However, the two-stage learning to defer setting, which leverages existing predictors such as a collection of LLMs or other classifiers, often faces challenges due to an expert imbalance problem. This imbalance can lead to suboptimal performance, with deferral algorithms favoring the majority expert. We present a comprehensive study of two-stage learning to defer in expert imbalance settings. We cast the deferral loss optimization as a novel cost-sensitive learning problem over the input-expert domain. We derive new margin-based loss functions and guarantees tailored to this setting, and develop novel algorithms for cost-sensitive learning. Leveraging these results, we design principled deferral algorithms, MILD (Margin-based Imbalanced Learning to Defer), specifically suited for expert imbalance settings. Extensive experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, showing clear improvements over existing baselines on both image classification and real-world Large Language Model (LLM) routing tasks.

AI.tldr: MILD is a novel deferral algorithm that addresses expert imbalance in two-stage learning-to-defer systems, using margin-based loss functions and cost-sensitive learning to prevent deferral bias toward majority experts.
AI.motivation: Learning-to-defer systems that route uncertain inputs to specialized experts face expert imbalance problems, where deferral algorithms tend to favor majority experts, leading to suboptimal performance despite potential accuracy gains from minority experts.
AI.method: The authors frame deferral loss optimization as a cost-sensitive learning problem and develop novel margin-based loss functions with theoretical guarantees. They propose MILD (Margin-based Imbalanced Learning to Defer), a principled deferral algorithm specifically designed for expert imbalance settings.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show MILD outperforms existing baselines on both image classification tasks and real-world LLM routing applications, demonstrating clear improvements in handling expert imbalance.
AI.conclusion: The MILD algorithm provides an effective solution to expert imbalance in learning-to-defer systems, enabling better utilization of specialized experts while maintaining computational efficiency, with demonstrated success across diverse domains.

title: Consumer Attitudes Towards AI in Digital Health: A Mixed-Methods Survey in Australia
summary: AI applications are increasingly being introduced into digital health. While technical performance has advanced rapidly, successful deployment mainly depends on consumer attitudes, especially to patient-facing applications. However, most existing research examines consumer attitudes towards healthcare AI at an abstract level rather than in response to concrete artefacts. We report a mixed-methods survey study in Australia (N=275) examining consumer readiness, acceptance, trust, and risk perceptions of healthcare AI, combined with a scenario-based evaluation of an AI-generated versus clinician-written consultation summary. Participants expressed moderate optimism and strong perceived usefulness and ease of use, but also substantial concerns about accuracy, safety, and data use. In the scenario task, the AI-generated summary was strongly preferred for quality, empathy, and overall usefulness, yet identification of the AI summary was near chance. Findings show that consumers judge AI through concrete communication quality and visible human governance, underscoring the need for clinically supervised deployment frameworks beyond technical performance alone.

AI.tldr: Consumers moderately optimistic about healthcare AI but have substantial concerns; AI-generated consultation summaries preferred over clinician-written ones, though identification of AI source is near chance.
AI.motivation: While technical performance of AI in digital health has advanced, successful deployment depends on consumer attitudes, especially for patient-facing applications. Most existing research examines attitudes at an abstract level rather than in response to concrete AI artifacts.
AI.method: Mixed-methods survey study in Australia (N=275) examining consumer readiness, acceptance, trust, and risk perceptions of healthcare AI, combined with a scenario-based evaluation comparing AI-generated versus clinician-written consultation summaries.
AI.result: Participants expressed moderate optimism, strong perceived usefulness and ease of use, but substantial concerns about accuracy, safety, and data use. In the scenario task, AI-generated summaries were strongly preferred for quality, empathy, and overall usefulness, yet identification of AI summary was near chance level.
AI.conclusion: Consumers judge AI through concrete communication quality and visible human governance, underscoring the need for clinically supervised deployment frameworks beyond technical performance alone.

title: Mind the Gap: Structure-Aware Consistency in Preference Learning
summary: Preference learning has become the foundation of aligning Large Language Models (LLMs) with human intent. Popular methods, such as Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), minimize surrogate losses as proxies for the intractable pairwise ranking loss. However, we demonstrate that for the equicontinuous hypothesis sets typical of neural networks, these standard surrogates are theoretically inconsistent, yielding vacuous generalization guarantees. To resolve this, we formulate LLM alignment within a margin-shifted ranking framework. We derive rigorous $H$-consistency bounds that depend on enforcing a separation margin $γ$. Crucially, we extend this to Structure-Aware $H$-consistency, introducing a novel objective (SA-DPO) that adapts the margin based on the semantic distance between responses to handle synonyms and hard pairs. Finally, we analyze the trade-off between consistency and model limitations via the Margin-Capacity Profile, proving that heavy-tailed surrogates (such as the Polynomial Hinge family) offer superior consistency guarantees for capacity-bounded models compared to the standard logistic loss used in DPO.

AI.tldr: SA-DPO improves DPO with structure-aware consistency bounds and margin-adaptive losses for better LLM alignment.
AI.motivation: Current DPO methods use theoretically inconsistent surrogate losses with vacuous generalization guarantees for neural networks, especially in equicontinuous hypothesis sets, limiting reliable LLM alignment with human preferences.
AI.method: Formulates LLM alignment in margin-shifted ranking framework, derives H-consistency bounds, introduces Structure-Aware DPO (SA-DPO) with semantic-distance-adaptive margins, and analyzes heavy-tailed surrogates via Margin-Capacity Profile.
AI.result: Theoretical proofs show SA-DPO provides rigorous consistency guarantees, heavy-tailed surrogates (like Polynomial Hinge family) offer superior consistency for capacity-bounded models compared to DPO's logistic loss, enabling better handling of synonyms and hard pairs.
AI.conclusion: Structure-aware margin adaptation and heavy-tailed surrogates address DPO's theoretical limitations, providing more reliable consistency guarantees for LLM preference alignment while handling semantic nuance.

title: Differential Subgroup Discovery: Characterizing Where Two Populations Differ, and Why
summary: We study the problem of understanding where two populations differ within a feature space, which we formalize in the concept of a differential subgroup: a subset of individuals from both populations who, despite sharing similar characteristics, exhibit exceptional differences in a target outcome. Differential subgroups reveal the regions of the feature space where population-level gaps are most pronounced and can help practitioners identify the covariate combinations that are structurally responsible for these differences, e.g.~in clinical analysis, model diagnostics, or treatment-effect studies. We introduce a general optimization objective for discovering differential subgroups and establish conditions under which the resulting subgroups admit a causal interpretation of population differences. We propose DiffSub, a gradient-based approach that discovers interpretable differential subgroups in tabular data. Across synthetic benchmarks, medical case studies, model-error analyses, and treatment-effect settings, DiffSub identifies informative subgroups that reveal where population differences arise and why.

AI.tldr: A method called DiffSub identifies interpretable subgroups where two populations show exceptional differences in outcomes, despite having similar characteristics - helping reveal where and why population gaps arise in applications like clinical analysis and treatment-effect studies.
AI.motivation: There's a need to understand where two populations differ within a feature space, particularly to identify covariate combinations that are structurally responsible for population-level differences. This is important for clinical analysis, model diagnostics, and treatment-effect studies where practitioners need to locate regions where population gaps are most pronounced.
AI.method: The paper introduces the concept of "differential subgroups" - subsets of individuals from both populations who share similar characteristics but exhibit exceptional differences in target outcomes. They propose DiffSub, a gradient-based approach that discovers interpretable differential subgroups in tabular data through a general optimization objective.
AI.result: DiffSub successfully identifies informative differential subgroups across multiple domains: synthetic benchmarks, medical case studies, model-error analyses, and treatment-effect settings. The method reveals where population differences arise and provides insights into why these differences occur.
AI.conclusion: Differential subgroups provide a valuable framework for understanding population differences, and DiffSub offers an effective gradient-based approach for discovering interpretable subgroups that help practitioners identify the structural sources of population gaps in various application domains.

title: Intent2Tx: Benchmarking LLMs for Translating Natural Language Intents into Ethereum Transactions
summary: The emergence of Large Language Models (LLMs) offers a transformative interface for Web3, yet existing benchmarks fail to capture the complexity of translating high-level user intents into functionally correct, state-dependent on-chain transactions. We present \textsc{Intent2Tx}, a high-fidelity benchmark featuring 29,921 single-step and 1,575 multi-step instances meticulously derived from 300 days of real-world Ethereum mainnet traces. Unlike prior works that rely on synthetic instructions, \textsc{Intent2Tx} grounds natural language intents in real-world protocol interactions across 11 categories, including diverse long-tail Decentralized Finance (DeFi) primitives. To enable rigorous evaluation, we propose an execution-aware framework that transcends surface-level text matching by employing differential state analysis on forked mainnet environments. Our extensive evaluation of 16 state-of-the-art LLMs reveals that while scaling and retrieval-augmentation enhance logical consistency and parameter precision, current models struggle with out-of-distribution generalization and multi-step planning. Crucially, our execution-based analysis demonstrates that syntactically valid outputs often fail to achieve intended state transitions, highlighting a significant gap in current "reasoning-to-execution" capabilities. \textsc{Intent2Tx} serves as a critical foundation for developing autonomous, reliable agents in intent-centric Web3 ecosystems. Code and data: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/Intent2Tx_Bench-97FF .

AI.tldr: Intent2Tx benchmark for evaluating LLMs' ability to translate natural language user intents into correct Ethereum transactions, using real-world transaction traces for comprehensive evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks fail to capture the complexity of translating high-level user intents into functionally correct, state-dependent on-chain transactions in Web3 environments.
AI.method: Created Intent2Tx benchmark with 29,921 single-step and 1,575 multi-step instances from 300 days of Ethereum mainnet traces across 11 categories including diverse DeFi primitives. Uses execution-aware framework with differential state analysis on forked mainnet environments.
AI.result: Evaluation of 16 state-of-the-art LLMs shows that scaling and retrieval-augmentation enhance logical consistency and parameter precision, but models struggle with out-of-distribution generalization and multi-step planning. Syntactically valid outputs often fail to achieve intended state transitions.
AI.conclusion: Intent2Tx serves as a critical foundation for developing autonomous, reliable agents in intent-centric Web3 ecosystems, highlighting significant gap in current "reasoning-to-execution" capabilities.

title: Linear-Core Surrogates: Smooth Loss Functions with Linear Rates for Classification and Structured Prediction
summary: The choice of loss function in classification involves a fundamental trade-off: smooth losses (like Cross-Entropy) enable fast optimization rates but yield slow square-root consistency bounds, while piecewise-linear losses (like Hinge) offer fast linear consistency rates but suffer from non-differentiability. We propose Linear-Core (LC) Surrogates, a new family of convex loss functions that resolve this tension by stitching a linear core to a smooth tail. We prove that these surrogates are differentiable everywhere while retaining strict linear $H$-consistency bounds, effectively combining the optimization benefits of smoothness with the statistical efficiency of margin-based losses. In the structured prediction setting, we show that this smoothness unlocks a massive computational and energy advantage: it allows for an unbiased stochastic gradient estimator that bypasses the quadratic complexity $O(|\mathscr{Y}|^2)$ of exact inference (e.g., Viterbi). Empirically, our method achieves a 23$\times$ speedup over Structured SVMs on large-vocabulary sequence tagging tasks and demonstrates superior robustness to instance-dependent label noise, outperforming Cross-Entropy by 2.6% on corrupted CIFAR-10.

AI.tldr: A new family of convex loss functions called Linear-Core (LC) Surrogates that combines the optimization benefits of smooth losses with the statistical efficiency of piecewise-linear losses, achieving both fast optimization rates and fast consistency bounds.
AI.motivation: There's a fundamental trade-off in classification loss functions: smooth losses like Cross-Entropy enable fast optimization rates but have slow consistency bounds, while piecewise-linear losses like Hinge offer fast consistency rates but suffer from non-differentiability.
AI.method: Propose Linear-Core (LC) Surrogates - a family of convex loss functions that stitch a linear core to a smooth tail, making them differentiable everywhere while retaining strict linear H-consistency bounds. In structured prediction, the smoothness enables an unbiased stochastic gradient estimator that bypasses quadratic complexity exact inference.
AI.result: Empirical results show a 23x speedup over Structured SVMs on large-vocabulary sequence tagging tasks and superior robustness to label noise, outperforming Cross-Entropy by 2.6% on corrupted CIFAR-10.
AI.conclusion: LC Surrogates successfully resolve the fundamental tension between optimization efficiency and statistical consistency, providing differentiable loss functions that combine the best properties of both smooth and piecewise-linear loss families.

title: WindowsWorld: A Process-Centric Benchmark of Autonomous GUI Agents in Professional Cross-Application Environments
summary: While GUI agents have shown impressive capabilities in common computer-use tasks such as OSWorld, current benchmarks mainly focus on isolated and single-application tasks. This overlooks a critical real-world requirement of coordinating across multiple applications to accomplish complex profession-specific workflows. To bridge this gap, we present a computer-use benchmark in cross-application workflows, named WindowsWorld, designed to systematically assess GUI Agents on complex multi-step tasks that mirror real-world professional activities. Our methodology uses a multi-agent framework steered by 16 occupations to generate four difficulty-level tasks with intermediate inspection, which are then refined by human review and executed in a simulated environment. The resulting benchmark contains 181 tasks with an average of 5.0 sub-goals across 17 common desktop applications, of which 78% are inherently multi-application. Experimental results of leading large models and agents show that: 1) All computer-use agents perform poorly on multi-application tasks (< 21% success rate), far below the performance of simple single-app tasks; 2) They largely fail at tasks requiring conditional judgment and reasoning across $\geq$ 3 applications, stalling at early sub-goals; 3) Low execution efficiency, where tasks often fail despite far exceeding human step limits. Code, benchmark data, and evaluation resources are available at github.com/HITsz-TMG/WindowsWorld.

AI.tldr: WindowsWorld benchmark tests GUI agents on complex multi-application professional workflows, revealing poor performance on multi-app tasks and conditional reasoning
AI.motivation: Current GUI agent benchmarks focus on isolated single-app tasks, overlooking real-world need for coordinating across multiple applications to accomplish complex professional workflows
AI.method: Multi-agent framework steered by 16 occupations generates four difficulty-level tasks with intermediate inspection, refined by human review, executed in simulated environment
AI.result: Benchmark contains 181 tasks with avg 5.0 sub-goals across 17 desktop apps (78% multi-app); agents perform poorly (<21% success) on multi-app tasks, fail at conditional reasoning across ≥3 apps, have low execution efficiency
AI.conclusion: GUI agents struggle significantly with cross-application professional workflows despite capabilities in single-app tasks; benchmark highlights need for improvement in multi-app coordination and conditional reasoning

title: On the Expressive Power of GNNs to Solve Linear SDPs
summary: Semidefinite programs (SDPs) are a powerful framework for convex optimization and for constructing strong relaxations of hard combinatorial problems. However, solving large SDPs can be computationally expensive, motivating the use of machine learning models as fast computational surrogates. Graph neural networks (GNNs) are a natural candidate in this setting due to their sparsity-awareness and ability to model variable-constraint interactions. In this work, we study what expressive power is sufficient to recover optimal SDP solutions. We first prove negative results showing that standard GNN architectures fail on recovering linear SDP solutions. We then identify a more expressive architecture that captures the key structure of SDPs and can, in particular, emulate the updates of a standard first-order solver. Empirically, on both synthetic and \textsc{SdpLib} benchmarks of various classes of SDPs, this more expressive architecture achieves consistently lower prediction error and objective gap than theoretically weaker baselines. Finally, using the learned high-quality predictions to warm-start the first-order solver yields practical speedups of up to 80%.

AI.tldr: Standard GNNs fail to recover optimal SDP solutions, but a more expressive GNN architecture that emulates first-order solvers achieves high accuracy and enables 80% speedups through warm-starting.
AI.motivation: SDPs are powerful but computationally expensive to solve, creating need for fast computational surrogates. GNNs are natural candidates due to their ability to handle sparse structures and model variable-constraint interactions in SDPs.
AI.method: The authors first prove theoretical limitations of standard GNN architectures for SDP solution recovery, then design a more expressive GNN architecture that captures key SDP structure and can emulate updates of standard first-order solvers. Empirical evaluation includes both synthetic and SdpLib benchmarks.
AI.result: The more expressive GNN architecture consistently achieves lower prediction error and objective gap than weaker baselines. Using learned predictions to warm-start first-order solvers yields practical speedups of up to 80%.
AI.conclusion: Expressive power beyond standard GNNs is required to recover optimal SDP solutions. The proposed architecture successfully captures SDP structure and provides practical benefits through warm-starting, bridging machine learning with traditional optimization methods.

title: Post-Optimization Adaptive Rank Allocation for LoRA
summary: Exponential growth in the scale of modern foundation models has led to the widespread adoption of Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) as a parameter-efficient fine-tuning technique. However, standard LoRA implementations disregard the varying intrinsic dimensionality of model layers and enforce a uniform rank, leading to parameter redundancy. We propose Post-Optimization Adaptive Rank Allocation (PARA), a data-free compression method for LoRA that integrates seamlessly into existing fine-tuning pipelines. PARA leverages Singular Value Decomposition to prune LoRA ranks using a global threshold over singular values across all layers. This results in non-uniform rank allocation based on layer-wise spectral importance. As a post-hoc method, PARA circumvents the training modifications and resulting instabilities that dynamic architectures typically incur. We empirically demonstrate that PARA reduces parameter count by 75-90\% while preserving the predictive performance of the original, uncompressed LoRA across multiple vision and language benchmarks. Code will be published upon acceptance.

AI.tldr: PARA is a post-optimization method that compresses LoRA adapters by adaptively allocating ranks across layers using singular value thresholding, reducing parameters by 75-90% while maintaining performance.
AI.motivation: Standard LoRA implementations use uniform rank across all model layers, ignoring varying intrinsic dimensionality and creating parameter redundancy as foundation models scale exponentially.
AI.method: PARA uses Singular Value Decomposition to prune LoRA ranks with a global threshold over singular values across all layers, allocating ranks non-uniformly based on layer-wise spectral importance as a data-free post-hoc compression method.
AI.result: Empirical results show PARA reduces parameter count by 75-90% while preserving predictive performance of original uncompressed LoRA across multiple vision and language benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: PARA provides an efficient, seamless compression method for LoRA fine-tuning that addresses parameter redundancy through adaptive rank allocation without modifying training pipelines or sacrificing performance.

title: Hyper-Dimensional Fingerprints as Molecular Representations
summary: Computational molecular representations underpin virtual screening, property prediction, and materials discovery. Conventional fingerprints are efficient and deterministic but lose structural information through hash-based compression, particularly at low dimensionalities. Learned representations from graph neural networks recover this expressiveness but require task-specific training and substantial computational resources. Here we introduce hyperdimensional fingerprints (HDF), which replace the learned transformations of message-passing neural networks with algebraic operations on high-dimensional vectors, producing deterministic molecular representations without any training. Across diverse property prediction benchmarks, HDF outperforms conventional fingerprints in the majority of tasks while exhibiting greater consistency across datasets and models. Crucially, HDF embeddings preserve molecular similarity faithfully: at 32 dimensions, distances in HDF space achieve a 0.9 Pearson correlation with graph edit distance, compared to 0.55 for Morgan fingerprints at equivalent size. This structural fidelity persists at low dimensions where hash-based methods degrade, allowing simple nearest-neighbor regression to remain predictive with as few as 64 components. We further demonstrate the practical impact in Bayesian molecular optimization, where HDF-based surrogate models achieve substantially improved sample efficiency in regimes where Morgan fingerprints perform comparably to random search. HDF thus provides a general-purpose, training-free alternative to conventional molecular fingerprints, suggesting that the information loss long accepted as inherent to fixed-length fingerprints is a limitation of the hash-based encoding scheme rather than the fingerprint paradigm itself.

AI.tldr: Hyperdimensional fingerprints (HDF) create deterministic molecular representations using algebraic operations on high-dimensional vectors without training, outperforming conventional fingerprints while preserving structural fidelity at low dimensions.
AI.motivation: Conventional molecular fingerprints lose structural information through hash-based compression, especially at low dimensionalities. Learned representations from graph neural networks recover expressiveness but require task-specific training and substantial computational resources. There's a need for efficient, deterministic molecular representations that preserve structural information without training requirements.
AI.method: Introduces hyperdimensional fingerprints (HDF) that replace learned transformations of message-passing neural networks with algebraic operations on high-dimensional vectors. HDF produces deterministic molecular representations without any training by using algebraic operations on high-dimensional vectors instead of hash-based compression or learned transformations.
AI.result: HDF outperforms conventional fingerprints in most property prediction tasks with greater consistency across datasets and models. At 32 dimensions, HDF achieves 0.9 Pearson correlation with graph edit distance (vs. 0.55 for Morgan fingerprints). Structural fidelity persists at low dimensions, allowing simple nearest-neighbor regression to remain predictive with as few as 64 components. In Bayesian molecular optimization, HDF-based surrogate models achieve substantially improved sample efficiency where Morgan fingerprints perform comparably to random search.
AI.conclusion: HDF provides a general-purpose, training-free alternative to conventional molecular fingerprints that preserves structural information. The information loss previously accepted as inherent to fixed-length fingerprints is a limitation of the hash-based encoding scheme rather than the fingerprint paradigm itself.

title: Focus Session: Autonomous Systems Dependability in the era of AI: Design Challenges in Safety, Security, Reliability and Certification
summary: The design of embedded safety-critical systems such as those used in next-generation automotive and autonomous platforms, is increasingly challenged by escalating system complexity, hardware-software heterogeneity, and the integration of intelligent, data-driven components. Ensuring dependability in such systems requires a holistic approach that spans multiple abstraction layers and encompasses both design- and run-time assurance. Traditional methods for reliability, safety, and security management often fall short in addressing the dynamic and uncertain behaviors introduced by Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML) components, especially under stringent real-time, power, and safety constraints. While AI and ML offer powerful predictive, adaptive, and self-optimizing capabilities that can enhance system dependability, their inherent non-determinism, data-dependence, and lack of formal guarantees introduce new challenges for verification, validation, and certification. This paper explores emerging methodologies, architectures, and frameworks for designing dependable autonomous and embedded systems in the era of AI. It highlight advances in reliability modeling, secure system design, and certification approaches that account for imperfect, learning-enabled components, aiming to bridge the gap between AI innovation and certifiable system-level dependability.

AI.tldr: This paper reviews emerging methods and frameworks for designing dependable AI-enabled embedded safety-critical systems, addressing challenges in reliability, safety, and certification due to AI/ML's non-determinism and data-dependence.
AI.motivation: The increasing complexity, hardware-software heterogeneity, and integration of AI/ML components in safety-critical embedded systems (e.g., automotive, autonomous platforms) create challenges for dependability, as traditional reliability, safety, and security methods are inadequate for dynamic, uncertain AI behaviors under real-time, power, and safety constraints.
AI.method: The paper explores emerging methodologies, architectures, and frameworks for designing dependable autonomous and embedded systems in the AI era, focusing on holistic approaches that span multiple abstraction layers and encompass both design- and run-time assurance.
AI.result: The paper highlights advances in reliability modeling, secure system design, and certification approaches that address imperfect, learning-enabled components, aiming to bridge the gap between AI innovation and certifiable system-level dependability.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that bridging the gap between AI innovation and certifiable system-level dependability requires new methodologies, architectures, and frameworks that can handle the unique challenges of AI/ML components, and highlights the need for advances in reliability modeling, secure system design, and certification approaches tailored for learning-enabled systems.

title: Probabilistic Circuits for Irregular Multivariate Time Series Forecasting
summary: Joint probabilistic modeling is essential for forecasting irregular multivariate time series (IMTS) to accurately quantify uncertainty. Existing approaches often struggle to balance model expressivity with consistent marginalization, frequently leading to unreliable or contradictory forecasts. To address this, we propose CircuITS, a novel architecture for probabilistic IMTS forecasting based on probabilistic circuits. Our model is flexible in capturing intricate dependencies between time series channels while structurally guaranteeing valid joint distributions. Experiments on four real world datasets demonstrate that CircuITS achieves superior joint and marginal density estimation compared to state of the art baselines.

AI.tldr: CircuITS is a probabilistic circuit-based architecture for irregular multivariate time series forecasting that ensures valid joint distributions while capturing complex dependencies.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches for joint probabilistic modeling of irregular multivariate time series often struggle to balance model expressivity with consistent marginalization, leading to unreliable or contradictory forecasts.
AI.method: Proposes CircuITS, a novel architecture based on probabilistic circuits that flexibly captures intricate dependencies between time series channels while structurally guaranteeing valid joint distributions.
AI.result: Experiments on four real-world datasets demonstrate that CircuITS achieves superior joint and marginal density estimation compared to state-of-the-art baselines.
AI.conclusion: CircuITS offers a powerful solution for probabilistic IMTS forecasting that balances expressivity with consistent marginalization, achieving state-of-the-art performance on real-world datasets.

title: MCPHunt: An Evaluation Framework for Cross-Boundary Data Propagation in Multi-Server MCP Agents
comment: 21 pages, 1 figure, 16 tables. Code: https://github.com/lihaonan0716/MCPHunt Data: https://huggingface.co/datasets/lihaonan0716/mcphunt-agent-traces
summary: Multi-server MCP agents create an information-flow control problem: faithful tool composition can turn individually benign read/write permissions into cross-boundary credential propagation -- a structural side effect of workflow topology, not necessarily malicious model behavior. We present MCPHunt, to our knowledge the first controlled benchmark that isolates non-adversarial, verbatim credential propagation across multi-server MCP trust boundaries, with three methodological contributions: (1) canary-based taint tracking that reduces propagation detection to objective string matching; (2) an environment-controlled coverage design with risky, benign, and hard-negative conditions that validates pipeline soundness and controls for credential-format confounds; (3) CRS stratification that disentangles task-mandated propagation (faithful execution of verbatim-transfer instructions) from policy-violating propagation (credentials included despite the option to redact). Across 3,615 main-benchmark traces from 5 models spanning 147 tasks and 9 mechanism families, policy-violating propagation rates reach 11.5--41.3% across all models. This propagation is pathway-specific (25x cross-mechanism range) and concentrated in browser-mediated data flows; hard-negative controls provide evidence that production-format credentials are not necessary -- prompt-directed cross-boundary data flow is sufficient. A prompt-mitigation study across 3 models reduces policy-violating propagation by up to 97% while preserving 80.5% utility, but effectiveness varies with instruction-following capability -- suggesting that prompt-level defenses alone may not suffice. Code, traces, and labeling pipeline are released under MIT and CC BY 4.0.

AI.tldr: MCPHunt benchmark demonstrates significant credential propagation across multi-server MCP trust boundaries despite non-adversarial conditions, with prompt mitigations showing partial effectiveness.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the information-flow control problem in multi-server MCP agents where benign read/write permissions can lead to cross-boundary credential propagation due to workflow topology.
AI.method: The methodology involves canary-based taint tracking, an environment-controlled coverage design with risky/benign/hard-negative conditions, and CRS stratification to distinguish task-mandated from policy-violating propagation.
AI.result: Results show policy-violating propagation rates of 11.5-41.3% across 5 models, concentrated in browser-mediated data flows. Prompt mitigations reduced propagation by up to 97% while preserving 80.5% utility.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that prompt-level defenses can significantly reduce policy-violating propagation but may not be sufficient on their own, highlighting the need for comprehensive safeguards in multi-agent systems.

title: CastFlow: Learning Role-Specialized Agentic Workflows for Time Series Forecasting
summary: Recently, large language models (LLMs) have shown great promise in time series forecasting. However, most existing LLM-based forecasting methods still follow a static generative paradigm that directly maps historical observations to future values in a single pass. Under this paradigm, forecasting is constrained by limited temporal pattern extraction, single-round acquisition of contextual features, one-shot forecast generation, and lack of support from ensemble forecasts. To address these limitations, in this work, we propose CastFlow, a dynamic agentic forecasting framework that enables multi-view temporal pattern extraction, multi-round contextual features acquisition, iterative forecast refinement, and forecasting with ensemble forecasts. First, CastFlow organizes the forecasting process into planning, action, forecasting, and reflection, establishing an agentic workflow. Second, this workflow is supported by a memory module that retrieves prior experience and a multi-view toolkit that constructs diagnostic evidence and provides a reliable ensemble forecast baseline. Third, CastFlow adopts a role-specialized design that combines general-purpose reasoning with specialized numerical forecasting. Under this design, a frozen LLM preserves general-purpose reasoning, while a fine-tuned domain-specific LLM performs evidence-guided numerical forecasting based on the ensemble forecast baseline, rather than from scratch. To optimize a fine-tuned domain-specific LLM, we further develop a two-stage workflow-oriented training that combines supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR). To evaluate the effectiveness of CastFlow, we conduct extensive experiments on diverse datasets and show that it achieves superior overall results against strong baselines. We hope that this work can serve as a step toward more adaptive and accurate time series forecasting.

AI.tldr: CastFlow is a dynamic agentic framework for time series forecasting using LLMs, addressing limitations of static approaches through multi-view pattern extraction, iterative refinement, and ensemble support.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based forecasting methods use static generative paradigms with limited temporal pattern extraction, single-round feature acquisition, one-shot generation, and lack of ensemble support.
AI.method: CastFlow organizes forecasting into planning, action, forecasting, and reflection workflow with memory module and multi-view toolkit. It combines frozen general-purpose LLM for reasoning with fine-tuned domain-specific LLM for evidence-guided numerical forecasting using ensemble baseline. Uses two-stage workflow-oriented training (SFT + RLVR).
AI.result: Extensive experiments on diverse datasets show CastFlow achieves superior overall results against strong baselines.
AI.conclusion: CastFlow represents progress toward more adaptive and accurate time series forecasting.

title: Physical Foundation Models: Fixed hardware implementations of large-scale neural networks
summary:
Foundation models are deep neural networks (such as GPT-5, Gemini~3, and Opus~4) trained on large datasets that can perform diverse downstream tasks -- text and code generation, question answering, summarization, image classification, and so on. The philosophy of foundation models is to put effort into a single, large (${\sim}10^{12}$-parameter) general-purpose model that can be adapted to many downstream tasks with no or minimal additional training. We argue that the rise of foundation models presents an opportunity for hardware engineers: in contrast to when different models were used for different tasks, it now makes sense to build special-purpose, fixed hardware implementations of neural networks, manufactured and released at the roughly 1-year cadence of major new foundation-model versions.
  Beyond conventional digital-electronic inference hardware with read-only weight memory, we advocate a more radical re-thinking: hardware in which the neural network is realized directly at the level of the physical design and operates via the hardware's natural physical dynamics -- \textit{Physical Foundation Models} (PFMs). PFMs could enable orders-of-magnitude advantages in energy efficiency, speed, and parameter density. For ${\sim}10^{12}$-parameter models, this would both reduce the high energy burden of AI in datacenters and enable AI in edge devices that today are power-constrained to far smaller models. PFMs could also enable inference hardware for models much larger than current ones: $10^{15}$- or even $10^{18}$-parameter PFMs seem plausible by some measures. We present back-of-the-envelope calculations illustrating PFM scaling using an optical example -- a 3D nanostructured glass medium -- and discuss prospects in nanoelectronics and other physical platforms. We conclude with the major research challenges that must be resolved for trillion-parameter PFMs and beyond to become reality.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Physical Foundation Models (PFMs) - hardware where neural networks are realized directly in physical design and operate through natural physical dynamics, offering orders-of-magnitude improvements in efficiency and enabling trillion-parameter AI at the edge.
AI.motivation: Foundation models enable single large general-purpose models for diverse tasks, creating an opportunity for specialized hardware implementations. Current digital-electronic inference hardware has energy and scaling limitations that physical hardware could overcome.
AI.method: The authors advocate for physical foundation models where neural networks are directly implemented in hardware using natural physical dynamics. They provide back-of-the-envelope calculations using optical examples like 3D nanostructured glass media, and discuss prospects in nanoelectronics and other platforms.
AI.result: PFMs could provide orders-of-magnitude advantages in energy efficiency, speed, and parameter density compared to conventional digital hardware. This could enable trillion-parameter models at datacenter scale while also making such models feasible for power-constrained edge devices.
AI.conclusion: Physical foundation models represent a radical rethinking of AI hardware that could overcome current limitations, enabling massive parameter scaling and edge deployment, though significant research challenges remain to realize trillion-parameter PFMs.

title: A Grid-Aware Agent-Based Model for Analyzing Electric Vehicle Charging Systems
comment: This is the author's version of a paper submitted to SIMULTECH. 12 Pages, 1 Table, 10 Figures
summary: This paper presents a configurable, grid-aware Agent-Based Model (ABM) for the systematic analysis of electric vehicle (EV) charging systems under configurable infrastructure and operational conditions. The model integrates heterogeneous EV behavior, charging column constraints, and a shared Energy Sandbox that regulates aggregate power allocation, enabling the joint study of user-centric charging dynamics and facility-level power behavior. Implemented in Python using the SimPy discrete-event framework, the approach supports scalable, event-driven simulations across varying system sizes, charger compositions, and scheduling strategies. A representative workplace charging scenario is investigated to illustrate how infrastructure configuration and coordination mechanisms influence energy delivery performance, infrastructure utilization, and aggregate load characteristics. The results highlight the context-dependence of infrastructure suitability and demonstrate how charging strategies and charger types reshape both service-level outcomes and grid-facing behavior. The proposed ABM provides a flexible and extensible simulation environment for exploring technical, operational, and grid-aware aspects of EV charging ecosystems, and for serving as a methodological basis for subsequent studies on advanced coordination strategies beyond the specific scenario analyzed in this study.

AI.tldr: A configurable Agent-Based Model (ABM) for simulating EV charging systems that integrates heterogeneous EV behaviors, charging infrastructure constraints, and power allocation mechanisms to study user dynamics and grid impacts.
AI.motivation: Need for systematic analysis of EV charging systems under varying infrastructure and operational conditions to understand how configuration choices affect energy delivery, infrastructure utilization, and grid behavior.
AI.method: Developed a grid-aware Agent-Based Model using Python/SimPy framework with discrete-event simulation. The model integrates heterogeneous EV behavior, charging column constraints, and a shared Energy Sandbox for aggregate power allocation.
AI.result: Demonstrated through workplace charging scenario that infrastructure configuration and coordination mechanisms significantly influence performance metrics. Results show context-dependent infrastructure suitability and how charging strategies reshape both service outcomes and grid behavior.
AI.conclusion: The ABM provides a flexible, extensible simulation environment for exploring technical, operational, and grid-aware aspects of EV charging ecosystems, serving as a methodological foundation for future coordination strategy studies.

title: Beyond the Baseband: Adaptive Multi-Band Encoding for Full-Spectrum Bioacoustics Classification
summary: Animals hear and vocalize across frequency ranges that differ substantially from humans, often extending into the ultrasonic domain. Yet most computational bioacoustics systems rely on audio models pre-trained at 16 kHz, restricting their usable bandwidth to the 0-8 kHz baseband and discarding higher-frequency information present in many bioacoustic recordings. We investigate a multi-band encoding framework that decomposes the full spectrum of animal calls into band features and fuses them into a unified representation. Similarity analyses on models show that certain encoders produce decorrelated band embeddings that improve class separation after fusion. Classification experiments on three bioacoustic datasets using eight pre-trained models and five fusion strategies show that fused representations consistently outperform the baseband and time-expansion baselines on two datasets, showing the potential of multi-band methods for full-spectrum encoding of animal calls.

AI.tldr: A multi-band encoding framework for bioacoustics that decomposes full-spectrum animal calls into band features and fuses them improves classification performance over traditional 16 kHz models by utilizing ultrasonic frequency information.
AI.motivation: Most computational bioacoustics systems use audio models pre-trained at 16 kHz, which restricts usable bandwidth to the 0-8 kHz baseband and discards higher-frequency information present in many bioacoustic recordings. This limitation hinders the analysis of animal vocalizations that extend into ultrasonic domains.
AI.method: The paper proposes a multi-band encoding framework that decomposes the full spectrum of animal calls into band features and fuses them into a unified representation. The study investigates eight pre-trained models and five fusion strategies, conducting similarity analyses and classification experiments on three bioacoustic datasets.
AI.result: Similarity analyses show that certain encoders produce decorrelated band embeddings that improve class separation after fusion. Classification experiments demonstrate that fused representations consistently outperform baseband and time-expansion baselines on two out of three datasets, indicating the potential of multi-band methods for full-spectrum encoding of animal calls.
AI.conclusion: Multi-band encoding and fusion strategies can effectively utilize the full spectrum of bioacoustic recordings, overcoming the limitations of standard 16 kHz models and improving classification performance for animal vocalizations.

title: Rethinking Agentic Reinforcement Learning In Large Language Models
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has traditionally focused on training specialized agents to optimize predefined reward functions within narrowly defined environments. However, the advent of powerful Large Language Models (LLMs) and increasingly complex, open-ended tasks has catalyzed a paradigm shift towards agentic paradigms within RL. This emerging framework extends beyond traditional RL by emphasizing the development of autonomous agents capable of goal-setting, long-term planning, dynamic strategy adaptation, and interactive reasoning in uncertain, real-world environments. Unlike conventional approaches that rely heavily on static objectives and episodic interactions, LLM-based Agentic RL incorporates cognitive-like capabilities such as meta-reasoning, self-reflection, and multi-step decision-making directly into the learning loop. In this paper, we provide a deep insight for looking the conceptual foundations, methodological innovations, and effective designs underlying this trend. Furthermore, we identify critical challenges and outline promising future directions for building LLM-based Agentic RL.

AI.tldr: The paper examines the emerging paradigm shift from traditional reinforcement learning to LLM-based Agentic RL, which incorporates cognitive capabilities like goal-setting, planning, and reasoning into autonomous agents for complex real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Traditional RL focuses on specialized agents optimizing predefined rewards in narrow environments, but the advent of powerful LLMs and complex open-ended tasks has created a need for more autonomous agents capable of handling uncertain, real-world environments with cognitive-like capabilities.
AI.method: The paper provides a conceptual analysis and survey of methodological innovations in LLM-based Agentic RL, examining how it extends beyond traditional RL by incorporating meta-reasoning, self-reflection, multi-step decision-making, and other cognitive capabilities directly into the learning loop.
AI.result: The analysis provides deep insights into the conceptual foundations and effective designs underlying the emerging trend of LLM-based Agentic RL, identifying how it represents a paradigm shift from static objectives and episodic interactions toward more autonomous, adaptive agents.
AI.conclusion: LLM-based Agentic RL represents a significant evolution in RL, moving toward autonomous agents with cognitive capabilities for complex real-world environments, but critical challenges remain that require further research and development in this promising direction.

title: Calibrating Attribution Proxies for Reward Allocation in Participatory Weather Sensing
summary: Large-scale IoT weather sensing networks require incentive mechanisms to sustain participation, yet determining how much value individual data contributions bring to the network remains an open problem. Existing approaches address data quality but not data valuation; in operational meteorology, adjoint-based methods derive value from the forecast model itself but require full data assimilation infrastructure. We propose to utilise differentiable AI weather models to fill this gap and characterise gradient-based attribution on gridded GFS analysis inputs as a candidate value signal, evaluating fidelity, calibration, cost, and gaming vulnerability across more than 400 configurations. Attribution captures near-optimal sensor placement utility with monotonically faithful payments, but can be inflated by adversarial inputs, with detection requiring external baseline data. These findings establish gradient attribution as a computationally validated signal for model-informed reward allocation in participatory weather sensing.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes using gradient attribution from differentiable AI weather models to value individual data contributions in IoT weather sensing networks, addressing the gap between data quality assessment and actual valuation of data contributions.
AI.motivation: Large-scale IoT weather sensing networks need incentive mechanisms to sustain participation, but there's no established method to determine how much value individual data contributions bring to the network. Existing approaches focus on data quality rather than data valuation, and traditional meteorological methods require full data assimilation infrastructure.
AI.method: The researchers propose using differentiable AI weather models to fill this gap. They characterize gradient-based attribution on gridded GFS analysis inputs as a candidate value signal. They evaluate this approach across more than 400 configurations, examining fidelity, calibration, cost, and gaming vulnerability.
AI.result: Gradient attribution captures near-optimal sensor placement utility with monotonically faithful payments. However, it can be inflated by adversarial inputs, and detecting such gaming requires external baseline data.
AI.conclusion: Gradient attribution represents a computationally validated signal for model-informed reward allocation in participatory weather sensing, establishing it as a promising approach for incentivizing data contributions in IoT weather networks.

title: KellyBench: A Benchmark for Long-Horizon Sequential Decision Making
summary: Language models are saturating benchmarks for procedural tasks with narrow objectives. But they are increasingly being deployed in long-horizon, non-stationary environments with open-ended goals. In this paper we introduce KellyBench, an environment for evaluating sequential decision-making in sports betting markets. Agents are placed in a sequential simulation of the 2023-24 English Premier League season and tasked with maximising their long-term bankroll growth. They are given detailed historical data, including advanced statistics, lineups, and public odds. To succeed they must build machine learning models, identify edge in public markets, and adapt as the environment changes over time. We find that all frontier models evaluated lose money on average over the course of the season for five seeds. The best performing model achieves an average return of -8%, and many models experiencing ruin across seeds. To judge strategy sophistication, we use a human expert rubric to grade each model and find their approaches to be unsophisticated compared to human baselines; Claude Opus 4.6 achieves a rubric score of 26.5%, which means there is significant room for improvement. KellyBench is available as an open-access API endpoint at https://openreward.ai/GeneralReasoning/KellyBench.

AI.tldr: KellyBench is a sports betting simulation environment for evaluating language models' sequential decision-making in long-horizon, non-stationary environments, where current frontier models perform poorly and lose money on average.
AI.motivation: Language models excel at narrow procedural tasks but struggle in real-world long-horizon, non-stationary environments with open-ended goals. There's a need for evaluation benchmarks that test sequential decision-making, adaptation to changing conditions, and complex real-world reasoning beyond traditional benchmarks.
AI.method: Created KellyBench, a simulated environment based on the 2023-24 English Premier League season. Agents must maximize long-term bankroll growth using historical data including advanced statistics, lineups, and public odds. They need to build ML models, identify market edges, and adapt to changing conditions. Evaluation uses five different seeds and includes both financial performance metrics and a human expert rubric to assess strategy sophistication.
AI.result: All frontier language models evaluated lose money on average over the season. The best performing model achieved average return of -8%, with many models experiencing complete ruin across seeds. Strategy sophistication was poor - Claude Opus 4.6 scored only 26.5% on human expert rubric, indicating significant room for improvement compared to human baselines.
AI.conclusion: Current language models are inadequate for complex sequential decision-making tasks in non-stationary environments like sports betting markets. KellyBench provides a challenging benchmark for evaluating such capabilities, and the significant performance gap between models and humans highlights the need for improved approaches to long-horizon reasoning and adaptation.

title: Differentiable latent structure discovery for interpretable forecasting in clinical time series
comment: This manuscript is under review at BioData Mining
summary: Background: Timely, uncertainty-aware forecasting from irregular electronic health records (EHR) can support critical-care decisions, yet most approaches either impute to a grid or sacrifice interpretability. We introduce StructGP, a continuous-time multi-task Gaussian process that couples process convolutions with differentiable structure learning to uncover a sparse, ordered directed acyclic graph (DAG) of inter-variable dependencies while preserving principled uncertainty. We further propose LP-StructGP, which augments StructGP with latent pathways-shared, temporally shifted trajectories inferred via subject-specific coupling filters and a softmax gating mechanism-to capture cross-patient progression patterns. Both models are trained under sparsity and acyclicity constraints (augmented Lagrangian, Adam) using scalable low-rank updates. Results: In simulations, the approach reliably recovers ground-truth graphs (Structural Hamming Distance approaching 0 as cohorts grow) and pathway assignments (high Adjusted Rand Index). On a MIMIC-IV septic shock cohort (n=1,008; norepinephrine, creatinine, mean arterial pressure), StructGP improves short-horizon (6 h) forecasting over independent-task baselines (average RMSE 0.68 [95%CI: 0.63--0.74] vs. 0.88 [0.83-0.94]) and, with 15 additional inputs, markedly outperforms unstructured kernels (0.63 [0.58-0.69] vs. 3.02 [2.85-3.18]) with superior calibration (coverage 0.96 vs. 0.84). On the PhysioNet Challenge (12k patients, 41 variables), StructGP attains competitive accuracy (MAE 3.72e-2) relative to a state-of-the-art graph neural model while maintaining calibrated uncertainty. Conclusion: These results show that structured process convolutions with latent pathways deliver interpretable, scalable, and well-calibrated forecasting for irregular clinical time series.

AI.tldr: StructGP and LP-StructGP: Gaussian process models that learn interpretable DAG dependencies and latent pathways for uncertainty-aware forecasting of irregular clinical time series, showing improved accuracy and calibration over baselines.
AI.motivation: Current approaches for forecasting from irregular electronic health records either require imputation to a grid or sacrifice interpretability, limiting their clinical utility. There's a need for uncertainty-aware forecasting that preserves interpretability while handling irregular time series.
AI.method: Introduced StructGP, a continuous-time multi-task Gaussian process combining process convolutions with differentiable structure learning to discover sparse, ordered DAG dependencies. Also proposed LP-StructGP which adds latent pathways with subject-specific coupling filters and softmax gating to capture cross-patient progression patterns. Both models trained under sparsity and acyclicity constraints using scalable low-rank updates.
AI.result: Simulations showed reliable recovery of ground-truth graphs and pathway assignments. On MIMIC-IV septic shock cohort, StructGP improved short-horizon forecasting over baselines (average RMSE 0.68 vs 0.88) and outperformed unstructured kernels with superior calibration (coverage 0.96 vs 0.84). On PhysioNet Challenge, achieved competitive accuracy (MAE 3.72e-2) relative to state-of-the-art while maintaining calibrated uncertainty.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that structured process convolutions with latent pathways provide interpretable, scalable, and well-calibrated forecasting for irregular clinical time series, successfully balancing uncertainty-aware predictions with interpretable structure learning.

title: Modeling Clinical Concern Trajectories in Language Model Agents
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents deployed in clinical settings often exhibit abrupt, threshold-driven behavior, offering little visibility into accumulating risk prior to escalation. In real-world care, however, clinicians act on gradually rising concern rather than instantaneous triggers. We study whether explicit state dynamics can expose such pre-escalation signals without delegating clinical authority to the agent. We introduce a lightweight agent architecture in which a memoryless clinical risk encoder is integrated over time using first- and second-order dynamics to produce a continuous escalation pressure signal. Across synthetic ward scenarios, stateless agents exhibit sharp escalation cliffs, while second-order dynamics produce smooth, anticipatory concern trajectories despite similar escalation timing. These trajectories surface sustained unease prior to escalation, enabling human-in-the-loop monitoring and more informed intervention. Our results suggest that explicit state dynamics can make LLM agents more clinically legible by revealing how long concern has been rising, not just when thresholds are crossed.

AI.tldr: LLM agents in clinical settings show abrupt threshold-based behavior, but clinicians act on gradual concern buildup. The paper introduces a lightweight agent architecture using first- and second-order dynamics to create continuous escalation pressure signals, making pre-escalation concerns visible for human monitoring.
AI.motivation: Current LLM agents deployed in clinical settings exhibit abrupt, threshold-driven behavior with little visibility into accumulating risk prior to escalation, unlike real-world clinicians who act on gradually rising concern.
AI.method: Introduces a lightweight agent architecture where a memoryless clinical risk encoder is integrated over time using first- and second-order dynamics to produce a continuous escalation pressure signal.
AI.result: In synthetic ward scenarios, stateless agents show sharp escalation cliffs, while second-order dynamics produce smooth, anticipatory concern trajectories with similar escalation timing. These trajectories reveal sustained unease prior to escalation.
AI.conclusion: Explicit state dynamics can make LLM agents more clinically legible by revealing how long concern has been rising, not just when thresholds are crossed, enabling human-in-the-loop monitoring and more informed intervention.

title: ITS-Mina: A Harris Hawks Optimization-Based All-MLP Framework with Iterative Refinement and External Attention for Multivariate Time Series Forecasting
summary: Multivariate time series forecasting plays a pivotal role in numerous real-world applications, including financial analysis, energy management, and traffic planning. While Transformer-based architectures have gained popularity for this task, recent studies reveal that simpler MLP-based models can achieve competitive or superior performance with significantly reduced computational cost. In this paper, we propose ITS-Mina, a novel all-MLP framework for multivariate time series forecasting that integrates three key innovations: (1) an iterative refinement mechanism that progressively enhances temporal representations by repeatedly applying a shared-parameter residual mixer stack, effectively deepening the model's computational capacity without multiplying the number of distinct parameters; (2) an external attention module that replaces traditional self-attention with learnable memory units, capturing cross-sample global dependencies at linear computational complexity; and (3) a Harris Hawks Optimization (HHO) algorithm for automatic dropout rate tuning, enabling adaptive regularization tailored to each dataset. Extensive experiments on six widely-used benchmark datasets demonstrate that ITS-Mina achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance compared to eleven baseline models across multiple forecasting horizons.

AI.tldr: ITS-Mina is an all-MLP framework for multivariate time series forecasting that uses iterative refinement, external attention with linear complexity, and adaptive dropout tuning via HHO optimization to achieve SOTA performance with lower computational cost than Transformers.
AI.motivation: Transformers are popular for time series forecasting but are computationally expensive. Recent studies show simpler MLP-based models can achieve competitive performance with reduced computational cost. There's a need for efficient MLP-based frameworks that maintain high accuracy.
AI.method: Three key innovations: (1) iterative refinement mechanism using shared-parameter residual mixer stack to deepen model capacity without parameter multiplication; (2) external attention module with learnable memory units to capture global dependencies at linear complexity instead of self-attention; (3) Harris Hawks Optimization (HHO) for automatic dropout rate tuning to provide dataset-specific adaptive regularization.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on six benchmark datasets show ITS-Mina achieves state-of-the-art or highly competitive performance compared to eleven baseline models across multiple forecasting horizons.
AI.conclusion: ITS-Mina demonstrates that MLP-based frameworks with well-designed components (iterative refinement, linear-complexity attention, and adaptive regularization) can achieve superior performance to Transformers in time series forecasting while being more computationally efficient.

title: Building Persona-Based Agents On Demand: Tailoring Multi-Agent Workflows to User Needs
summary: Recent advances in agentic AI are shifting automation from discrete tools to proactive multi-agent systems that coordinate multi-specialized capabilities behind unified interfaces. However, today's agent systems typically rely on hard-coded agent architectures with fixed roles, coordination patterns, and interaction flows that limit end-user personalization and make adaptation to individual needs and contexts difficult. Given this limitation, we argue that on-demand persona-based agent generation offers a promising path towards more efficient and contextually appropriate interaction within agentic workflows. By dynamically crafting agents and personas at run-time to match user characteristics, task demands, and workflow context, agentic platforms can move beyond one-size-fits-all configurations. We present a pipeline for on-demand persona generation in agentic platforms, detailing how real-time crafting of AI personas can be systematically integrated within agent systems, aiming to open new possibilities in agentic platform design paradigms.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes on-demand persona-based agent generation for agentic AI systems to overcome limitations of fixed agent architectures and enable personalized, context-appropriate interactions.
AI.motivation: Current agentic AI systems use hard-coded agent architectures with fixed roles, coordination patterns, and interaction flows, which limit end-user personalization and make adaptation to individual needs and contexts difficult. There's a need to move beyond one-size-fits-all configurations.
AI.method: The authors present a pipeline for on-demand persona generation in agentic platforms, enabling real-time crafting of AI personas that match user characteristics, task demands, and workflow context. This involves dynamically generating agents and personas at run-time within agent systems.
AI.result: The proposed approach offers a path toward more efficient and contextually appropriate interaction within agentic workflows by enabling dynamic, personalized agent configurations that adapt to specific user needs and contexts.
AI.conclusion: On-demand persona-based agent generation represents a promising direction for agentic platform design paradigms, potentially opening new possibilities for creating more adaptive, personalized, and context-aware multi-agent systems.

title: Dynamic Scaled Gradient Descent for Stable Fine-Tuning for Classifications
summary: Fine-tuning pretrained models has become a standard approach to adapting pretrained knowledge to improve the accuracy on new sparse, imbalance datasets. However, issues arise when optimization falls into a collapsed state, where the model gets stuck, leading to degraded performance and unstable training. One possible reason for this is the cancellation of gradients across training examples. To address this problem, we propose a novel algorithm, dynamic scaled gradient descent (\mName), that directly modifies the gradients returned by training examples, specifically, scaling down the gradients of correctly classified examples using a dynamic scaler. This strategy offers both theoretical and empirical advantages in improving training stability. Experiments on a variety of benchmark datasets, spanning multiple tasks and large pretrained models, demonstrate that our method consistently reduces performance variance and surpasses the accuracy of existing approaches.

AI.tldr: A novel dynamic scaled gradient descent method improves fine-tuning stability by scaling down gradients of correctly classified examples to prevent optimization collapse.
AI.motivation: During fine-tuning of pretrained models on sparse, imbalanced datasets, optimization can fall into collapsed states where models get stuck, leading to degraded performance and unstable training. This is potentially caused by gradient cancellation across training examples.
AI.method: Proposes dynamic scaled gradient descent (DSGD) that directly modifies gradients by scaling down gradients of correctly classified examples using a dynamic scaler. This prevents gradient cancellation and improves training stability.
AI.result: Experiments on various benchmark datasets across multiple tasks and large pretrained models show the method consistently reduces performance variance and surpasses accuracy of existing approaches.
AI.conclusion: The proposed dynamic scaled gradient descent algorithm effectively addresses optimization collapse in fine-tuning scenarios, offering both theoretical and empirical advantages for stable training on sparse, imbalanced datasets.

title: In-Context Prompting Obsoletes Agent Orchestration for Procedural Tasks
summary: Agent orchestration frameworks -- LangGraph, CrewAI, Google ADK, OpenAI Agents SDK, and others -- place an external orchestrator above the LLM, tracking state and injecting routing instructions at every turn. We present a controlled comparison showing that for procedural tasks, this architecture is dominated by a simpler alternative: putting the entire procedure in the system prompt and letting the model self-orchestrate. Across three domains -- travel booking (14 nodes), Zoom technical support (14 nodes), and insurance claims processing (55 nodes) -- we evaluate 200 conversations per condition using LLM-as-judge scoring on five quality criteria. The in-context approach scores 4.53--5.00 on a 5-point scale while a LangGraph orchestrator using the same model scores 4.17--4.84. The orchestrated system fails on 24% of travel, 9% of Zoom, and 17% of insurance conversations, compared to 11.5%, 0.5%, and 5% for the in-context baseline. While external orchestration may have been necessary for earlier models, advances in frontier model capabilities have made it unnecessary for multi-turn conversations following a defined procedure.

AI.tldr: For procedural multi-turn conversations, putting the entire procedure in the system prompt and letting the LLM self-orchestrate outperforms external orchestration frameworks (LangGraph, CrewAI, etc.) across three domains, with significantly lower failure rates.
AI.motivation: External orchestration frameworks (LangGraph, CrewAI, Google ADK, OpenAI Agents SDK) add complexity by placing an orchestrator above the LLM to track state and inject routing instructions. The paper questions whether this architecture is still necessary given advances in frontier model capabilities.
AI.method: Conducted a controlled comparison across three procedural domains: travel booking (14 nodes), Zoom technical support (14 nodes), and insurance claims processing (55 nodes). Evaluated 200 conversations per condition using LLM-as-judge scoring on five quality criteria. Compared an in-context approach (putting entire procedure in system prompt) against a LangGraph orchestrator using the same model.
AI.result: In-context approach scored 4.53-5.00 on a 5-point scale while LangGraph scored 4.17-4.84. Failure rates: orchestrated system failed on 24% of travel, 9% of Zoom, and 17% of insurance conversations vs 11.5%, 0.5%, and 5% for the in-context baseline.
AI.conclusion: While external orchestration may have been necessary for earlier models, advances in frontier model capabilities have made it unnecessary for multi-turn conversations following a defined procedure. The simpler in-context approach outperforms complex orchestration frameworks for procedural tasks.

title: Latent-GRPO: Group Relative Policy Optimization for Latent Reasoning
comment: This is an actively developing work, and we will continue to update the arXiv version
summary: Latent reasoning offers a more efficient alternative to explicit reasoning by compressing intermediate reasoning into continuous representations and substantially shortening reasoning chains. However, existing latent reasoning methods mainly focus on supervised learning, and reinforcement learning in latent space remains highly unstable.
